pull_quote: Most of the time you end up in a bad part of a tree, in a local maxima of a path-dependent environment, by only making good choices. This is the most dangerous thing. Continuously re-derive your foundational assumptions.

episode_title: Tobi Lutke
insight: The biggest product breakthroughs often come from valuing unquantifiable things like taste and delight, which traditional metrics usually miss.
pull_quote: The overlap of most valuable things you can do with a product, and for things that happen to be fully quantifiable, is maybe 20%. Get comfortable with the 80% unquantifiable things like taste, quality, and passion.

episode_title: Tobi Lutke
insight: Most companies obsess over short-term 'tactics'; true, enduring growth comes from playing the long-term 'positional game' to build trust and market role.
pull_quote: The business world only talks about tactics... we lionize the easy hack. But the positional game—your role, trust with merchants—is where all the value resides long-term.

episode_title: Tobi Lutke
insight: Ruthlessly simplifying your product's UX isn't just about polish; it's a direct growth lever that enables more people to become entrepreneurs.
pull_quote: Every single time we make a complex thing simpler, more businesses will exist on a platform. Overly complex software stuns users and stops progress, killing potential businesses.

episode_title: Tobi Lutke
insight: A product's quality is a direct reflection of how much the team genuinely 'gives a shit' about it, a passion great leaders must infectiously spread.
pull_quote: Every product in the world, the quality at the end of the day is simply a reflection of how much the people who created it gave a shit about the product. Product leaders must be exothermically infectious with caring.

episode_title: A framework for finding product-market fit | Todd Jackson (First Round Capital)
insight: Most PMs miss that efficiency is as critical as demand and satisfaction for true product-market fit; without it, your business model just isn't viable.
pull_quote: Imagine a $100 vending machine: put in a dollar, get a hundred out. Insane demand, extreme satisfaction. But it's ridiculous – not viable. That's why efficiency is key to product-market fit.

episode_title: A framework for finding product-market fit | Todd Jackson (First Round Capital)
insight: At nascent product-market fit, don't chase efficiency; focus purely on deep customer satisfaction, even if it means doing things manually or being completely inefficient.
pull_quote: When Vanta started, the founder manually filled out spreadsheets for customers, posing as AI. It's okay to be inefficient at level one, as long as you're delivering incredible satisfaction.

episode_title: A framework for finding product-market fit | Todd Jackson (First Round Capital)
insight: Many founders assume success at $5M ARR, but 60% of companies get stuck before hitting truly strong product-market fit, often not realizing it.
pull_quote: Roughly 60-70% of companies never get past Level 2 product-market fit – even with 20 employees and $5 million ARR. Most founders never 'feel' PMF until they're much, much larger.

episode_title: A framework for finding product-market fit | Todd Jackson (First Round Capital)
insight: When stuck, don't fear a radical pivot; successful companies often change their persona, problem, promise, or even product entirely, not just tweak.
pull_quote: Most founders do a 10% pivot, but what they really need is a 200% pivot. When you're stuck, be willing to change your persona, problem, promise, or even your product entirely.

episode_title: A framework for finding product-market fit | Todd Jackson (First Round Capital)
insight: Stop asking customers if they like your idea; instead, ask 'what's a fair price?' or for a 'wow' reaction and specific behaviors, not polite 'interesting' feedback.
pull_quote: Customers are nice; 'interesting' is a polite 'no.' To truly test demand, ask 'what's a fair, expensive, or prohibitively expensive price?' Look for 'wow' statements or tangible actions, not just agreement.

episode_title: Billion dollar failures, and billion dollar success | Tom Conrad (Quibi, Pandora, Pets.com, Snap, Zero)
insight: No amount of great product execution can save a business if the underlying “math” or market economics are fundamentally broken from the start.
pull_quote: Companies are also like a math problem that describes how you take investment and pour them into the equation and out the other side comes returns. If the equation is fundamentally broken, no amount of iteration and execution can get you out of the failed outputs.

episode_title: Billion dollar failures, and billion dollar success | Tom Conrad (Quibi, Pandora, Pets.com, Snap, Zero)
insight: Building deeply genuine, unscripted human connections with early users can be an incredibly powerful, cost-effective driver of word-of-mouth growth.
pull_quote: We made a decision to have no customer support team in the first year. You would write to us and get the founder, me, or the CEO directly. We just engaged with our audience as real human beings, not a shiny brand.

episode_title: Billion dollar failures, and billion dollar success | Tom Conrad (Quibi, Pandora, Pets.com, Snap, Zero)
insight: As a product-oriented CEO, your casual suggestions carry disproportionate weight; exercise extreme caution to avoid accidentally dictating outcomes.
pull_quote: No matter how much you tell the team that when I swing by their virtual desk and say, 'I've been thinking about this little detail, what do you think?' nine out of ten times, that person is going to go and start working on that thing.

episode_title: Billion dollar failures, and billion dollar success | Tom Conrad (Quibi, Pandora, Pets.com, Snap, Zero)
insight: The founder-hero narrative is overblown; many smart people could achieve more success and impact by joining a high-potential existing team.
pull_quote: There's this belief that everybody needs to be a founder. Our industry would be much better off if there were fewer founders. You can achieve acclaim, financial reward, and impact in collaboration with others.

episode_title: Why LinkedIn is turning PMs into AI-powered \"full stack builders” | Tomer Cohen (LinkedIn CPO)
insight: Don't get stuck on being right; prioritize clear thinking and unified execution, because confusion means you're already losing.
pull_quote: We might be wrong but we're not confused. Confusion in the system means only luck can save you. You must all pull in the same direction, even if it’s the wrong one, to have any chance.

episode_title: Why LinkedIn is turning PMs into AI-powered \"full stack builders” | Tomer Cohen (LinkedIn CPO)
insight: When transforming an entrenched product, carve out a dedicated user cohort to build and prove new experiences without disrupting the entire system.
pull_quote: When transforming an entrenched product, I carved out 2 million members, giving them full liberty for a new experience. This 'secluded country' dramatically changed behavior and proved the new vision.

episode_title: Why LinkedIn is turning PMs into AI-powered \"full stack builders” | Tomer Cohen (LinkedIn CPO)
insight: As an AI-first PM, you must grasp the algorithm's objective, features, data strategy, and even infrastructure – these are your biggest product levers.
pull_quote: Most PMs delegate AI, but you *must* hold the 'pedals.' Ask: What's the algorithm's objective? What features? What's the data strategy? That could be a bigger lever than any button.

episode_title: Why LinkedIn is turning PMs into AI-powered \"full stack builders” | Tomer Cohen (LinkedIn CPO)
insight: When adopting major new tech, first let teams diverge to explore creatively, then strategically converge on the top bets to focus resources.
pull_quote: When ChatGPT emerged, we told teams: 'Let go of your roadmap, revisit problems with this new tech.' We let them diverge and go crazy, then top-down converged on the best 4-5 bets.

episode_title: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome your speaking anxiety | Tristan de Montebello (CEO & co-founder of Ultraspeaking)
insight: Stop chasing symptoms and endless tactics; get into a flow state and fix the underlying root causes for true improvement.
pull_quote: The day I understood that speaking was a subconscious flow-oriented process, not a conscious one, everything changed. Solve the root cause, and not only do the symptoms take care of themselves, but you suddenly have more mind space.

episode_title: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome your speaking anxiety | Tristan de Montebello (CEO & co-founder of Ultraspeaking)
insight: Build resilience by intentionally creating 'turbulence' in low-stakes practice environments, like running simulations or small, rapid experiments.
pull_quote: Every game we play creates turbulence. The gain is highlighting areas to work on, seeing how you tend to leak or struggle, and getting reps in to recover gracefully from any mistake, making you confident in any scenario.

episode_title: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome your speaking anxiety | Tristan de Montebello (CEO & co-founder of Ultraspeaking)
insight: Don't let the fear of being perceived as a 'bullshitter' stop you from communicating with conviction; that self-censorship limits your impact.
pull_quote: Many avoid speaking freely because they don't want to be perceived as a bullshitter—it's an immune response. But if that thought's in your mind, you're the type who can spot bullshit, so you won't become one. It's just too loud right now.

episode_title: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome your speaking anxiety | Tristan de Montebello (CEO & co-founder of Ultraspeaking)
insight: Craft powerful product narratives by iteratively speaking your ideas, compressing to their core essence, then intentionally expanding back out.
pull_quote: The old way of preparing talks by writing a script is broken. Instead, prepare by speaking: go down the accordion to create extreme clarity on your talk's essence, then back up to bring in only the right pieces. Your talk will be internalized, not memorized.

episode_title: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome your speaking anxiety | Tristan de Montebello (CEO & co-founder of Ultraspeaking)
insight: When communicating, stop focusing on what you want to say and obsess about the single, most important thing your audience should remember.
pull_quote: The biggest mistake most people make is focusing on what they want to say, not what they want their audience to remember. You only remember one thing from any talk, so make sure that 'one thing' is your core message—your arrow.

episode_title: An inside look at how CNN builds product | Upasna Gautam
insight: In high-stress product environments, actively planning for chaos with buffers and cultivating equanimity is more critical than just reacting to ambiguity.
pull_quote: Forget just 'thriving in ambiguity' – in news product, you must thrive in *chaos*. That means building buffers into every plan for when breaking news inevitably derails your most critical user testing sessions.

episode_title: An inside look at how CNN builds product | Upasna Gautam
insight: To truly serve critical users, deeply embed your team into their daily realities, even staging 'dress rehearsals' for their most chaotic workflows.
pull_quote: Most PMs run user tests, but at CNN, we run 'breaking news dress rehearsals.' We simulate chaos with a script, engineers on standby, and users publishing live to stress test our platform. It’s the only way to ensure it works when minutes count.

episode_title: An inside look at how CNN builds product | Upasna Gautam
insight: Embedding engineers into early user discovery isn't just nice to have; it's a 'game-changer' that provides deep context and makes the whole product process more efficient.
pull_quote: Engineers aren't just resources, they're partners. When our tech lead joins user testing and design jams, engineers understand *why* we're building, not just *what*. This upfront context defines 'how' with incredible clarity.

episode_title: An inside look at how CNN builds product | Upasna Gautam
insight: Truly effective product communication isn't about having all the answers; it's about deep, mindful listening and speaking the listener's language.
pull_quote: My boss hired me not for technical skills, but for 'mindful communication.' That means shutting up and listening when a user vents, then extracting the root cause. Speaking the *listener's* language is more powerful than just speaking articulately.

episode_title: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member | Uri Levine (co-founder of Waze)
insight: Don't just find a problem, truly fall in love with it; it becomes your North Star and makes customers care enough to help you succeed.
pull_quote: If I tell you I'm going to help you avoid traffic jams, then you do care. When your customer cares, they want you to be successful, and they'll help you get there.

episode_title: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member | Uri Levine (co-founder of Waze)
insight: You likely know within 30 days if a new hire isn't right; if so, fire them immediately, because top performers will leave if you don't.
pull_quote: Every time you hire someone new, mark your calendar for 30 days down the road. Ask yourself: 'Knowing what I know today, would I hire this person?' If no, fire them immediately.

episode_title: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member | Uri Levine (co-founder of Waze)
insight: The most important slides in your pitch deck are the first and last ones, as they're seen longest; use them to repeat your strongest point.
pull_quote: Most people miss the most important slide: the first one. It's presented longest. Put your strongest point there. The second most important is the last one – use it to repeat that point.

episode_title: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member | Uri Levine (co-founder of Waze)
insight: Product-market fit has only one metric: retention; if customers keep coming back, you're creating value, if not, you're not.
pull_quote: Product market fit has one metric. That's it. Retention. If you create value, they will come back. If they're not coming back, that means you're not creating value.

episode_title: Lessons from a two-time unicorn builder, 50-time startup advisor, and 20-time company board member | Uri Levine (co-founder of Waze)
insight: Don't assume users use your product like you do; watch new users, especially unsuccessful ones, to understand what truly blocks them.
pull_quote: Different people use products differently. If you are building a product and don't know that, you are building the wrong product. Watch new users, especially those who failed, to understand why.

episode_title: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1 million developers in four months: The untold story of Windsurf | Varun Mohan (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Always be ready to cannibalize your own product; it should make your existing product look silly every 6-12 months.
pull_quote: We should be cannibalizing the existing state of our product every six to 12 months. Every six to 12 months, it should make our existing product look silly. It should almost make the form factor of existing product look dumb.

episode_title: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1 million developers in four months: The untold story of Windsurf | Varun Mohan (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Even with identical ML models, a superior UI can triple user acceptance rates by providing proper, integrated capabilities.
pull_quote: We had to dynamically generate images in VSCode due to API limits. When we moved to our own IDE, our acceptance rate *tripled* with the same ML models. A superior UI, with proper integrated capabilities, delivers immense value.

episode_title: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1 million developers in four months: The untold story of Windsurf | Varun Mohan (co-founder and CEO)
insight: In the AI era, true agency—the drive to proactively build and solve problems rather than just follow instructions—is the most undervalued skill.
pull_quote: One of the most undervalued things is agency. We don't prioritize getting people with real agency who *want* to build something. This is crucial as AI takes over, demanding proactive problem-solvers.

episode_title: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1 million developers in four months: The untold story of Windsurf | Varun Mohan (co-founder and CEO)
insight: Niche vertical SaaS products are highly vulnerable because domain specialists can now easily build custom tools, rendering bloated software obsolete.
pull_quote: Verticalized niche software products are going to get competed down a ton. Domain specialists now have access to build custom tools they always wanted, often replacing bloated SaaS with 10% of the features.

episode_title: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1 million developers in four months: The untold story of Windsurf | Varun Mohan (co-founder and CEO)
insight: AI writing 90% of code doesn't shrink engineering teams; it dramatically boosts the ROI of building technology, leading companies to hire more.
pull_quote: If AI writes 90% of code, engineers aren't suddenly 10x more productive. AI boosts the ROI of building technology, making it cheaper and faster. So, companies actually hire *more* engineers, not fewer.

episode_title: An inside look at how Miro builds product: Lessons on outmaneuvering competitors, team structure, product quality, and moving fast | Varun Parmar (CPO of Miro)
insight: Products never stay static; every single release pushes them either forward or backward in the eyes of the customer and against competitors.
pull_quote: Products either get better or they get worse. Every release, every code push, you're making chess moves – gaining positive points or going negative against your competitors in the customer's mind.

episode_title: An inside look at how Miro builds product: Lessons on outmaneuvering competitors, team structure, product quality, and moving fast | Varun Parmar (CPO of Miro)
insight: To combat feature silos and ensure a holistic product perspective, deeply embed product marketing, analytics, and design with product and engineering early.
pull_quote: Our 'AMPED' product org (Analytics, Marketing, Product, Engineering, Design) deeply embeds product marketing. This prevents persona-based teams from siloing, bringing critical end-user, enterprise, and positioning perspectives early.

episode_title: An inside look at how Miro builds product: Lessons on outmaneuvering competitors, team structure, product quality, and moving fast | Varun Parmar (CPO of Miro)
insight: In competitive markets, don't fear failure; prioritize speed to 'hit the brick wall' and discover what doesn't work, learning faster than competitors.
pull_quote: Be the first one to hit the brick wall. In competitive markets, speed is the single biggest determinant of success. You want to learn what doesn't work faster than anyone else to pivot and stay ahead.

episode_title: An inside look at how Miro builds product: Lessons on outmaneuvering competitors, team structure, product quality, and moving fast | Varun Parmar (CPO of Miro)
insight: Forget abstract quality definitions. Our design leadership continuously classifies shipped features as high/not high, using concrete examples to build shared team intuition.
pull_quote: We debated for ages how to *define* 'high quality.' So we just started classifying every shipped feature as high or not. Our design leaders triage releases, showing examples, to build a shared organizational muscle for 'what great looks like'.

episode_title: An inside look at how Miro builds product: Lessons on outmaneuvering competitors, team structure, product quality, and moving fast | Varun Parmar (CPO of Miro)
insight: Empowering users to create and share content, like templates, can organically transform into a powerful, even SEO-driven, top-of-funnel acquisition engine.
pull_quote: A FIFA World Cup template in Miroverse got 100,000 views, 15,000 copies, and even ranked on Google! Community-generated content isn't just engagement; it's a huge, unexpected acquisition channel.

episode_title: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey | Vijay Iyengar (Head of Product)
insight: When expanding, invest profits into new ventures, not people from your core team, or you risk neglecting and losing your primary market.
pull_quote: Don't take people away from the core to go to these other things because then you end up distracted. Invest profits and not people, or venture capital, into the next venture.

episode_title: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey | Vijay Iyengar (Head of Product)
insight: When facing high churn and existential threats, sometimes ignoring "best practices" and building a roadmap directly from top churn reasons by ARR is the fastest path to recovery.
pull_quote: We threw away all our planning... took all the churn reasons... sorted descending by ARR, took the top 10 things and made that our roadmap. This goes against about a million product best practices.

episode_title: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey | Vijay Iyengar (Head of Product)
insight: To build a truly cohesive product, you might need to temporarily stop tactical feature work and dedicate design time to build a strong system architecture.
pull_quote: We said, 'Hey, we can actually do the next three months of projects without any design,'... so that you can take three months with a set of designers and go think about the system architecture of the product.

episode_title: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey | Vijay Iyengar (Head of Product)
insight: RICE's confidence and effort scores often prematurely kill truly innovative, high-impact ideas; explore them longer before letting C&E deprioritize them.
pull_quote: The C and E, the confidence and effort tends to cause you to prematurely deprioritize potentially high reach, high impact bets... Give it a fair shot. You can probably find a higher confident, lower effort way to do them.

episode_title: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey | Vijay Iyengar (Head of Product)
insight: The biggest mistake in product analytics is using client-side SDKs, which leads to poor data quality; instead, default to reliable server-side tracking.
pull_quote: The biggest mistake is setting up analytics using client side SDKs, client side tracking... it leads to poor data quality and difficulty to maintain that data. We recommend instead, track events from your servers.

episode_title: A framework for PM skill development | Vikrama Dhiman (Gojek)
insight: Early in your career, don't obsess over 'impact'; focus on shipping *outputs* and being useful, even in small ways.
pull_quote: Your advice is early in your career, focus on just getting stuff out and done. Don't so obsess with the impact. As you get more senior, you'll have more opportunity to think about strategy.

episode_title: A framework for PM skill development | Vikrama Dhiman (Gojek)
insight: Proving your product craft isn't just about impact; it's about the consistent, high-quality *artifacts* you produce, like PRDs and product notes.
pull_quote: You have to also show that yes, you were a useful contributor to having that particular impact. Can you show me your last PRD? Can you show me the last product note that you sent? Usually, you'll find something or the other missing.

episode_title: A framework for PM skill development | Vikrama Dhiman (Gojek)
insight: A successful operating model means getting decisions made, not making them all yourself, and raising tough issues without being difficult to work with.
pull_quote: Raise difficult issues without being difficult to work with. Bring out important topics without drawing importance to yourself. Be able to get decisions made without having to make all the decisions yourself.

episode_title: A framework for PM skill development | Vikrama Dhiman (Gojek)
insight: Stalled career growth often comes from the 'stories' you tell yourself about who you are as a PM; challenge those self-perceptions to unlock new growth.
pull_quote: Figure out what is the story that you are telling yourself. If you are not able to figure that out, talk to the people you trust. The moment you are able to correct those stories, you may be back on the growth path again.

episode_title: A framework for PM skill development | Vikrama Dhiman (Gojek)
insight: Don't try to improve every skill at once; identify the single area that offers you maximum leverage, and focus on mastering that first.
pull_quote: You can't improve on every single area. That's what overwhelms you. You need to pick which is the area which is the maximum leverage for you and improve on that particular aspect and then move on to the next area.

episode_title: Become a better communicator: Specific frameworks to improve your clarity, influence, and impact | Wes Kao (coach, entrepreneur, advisor)
insight: Don't resent managing your boss; embrace it to unlock more opportunities, trust, and appreciation in your career.
pull_quote: Most people assume their boss has to manage them and feel resentful. But if you embrace managing your boss, they'll appreciate you more, you'll get more opportunities, and build greater trust.

episode_title: Become a better communicator: Specific frameworks to improve your clarity, influence, and impact | Wes Kao (coach, entrepreneur, advisor)
insight: Stop wasting time on the 'what' and 'why' — your audience already gets it; dive straight into the 'super specific how' to truly engage them.
pull_quote: Most writers spend too much time on the 'what' and 'why.' Your audience already knows that stuff. They just want to know: How do I do this? How do I apply this to my life? What are the nuances?

episode_title: Become a better communicator: Specific frameworks to improve your clarity, influence, and impact | Wes Kao (coach, entrepreneur, advisor)
insight: Your monologues are putting people to sleep; punctuate them with 'state changes' every few minutes to keep attention and engagement high in meetings.
pull_quote: Most Zoom meetings are one person talking at you, silently. It's draining. Punctuate your monologues with 'state changes' every 3-5 minutes – a chat question, a poll, a breakout – to keep your audience awake.

episode_title: Become a better communicator: Specific frameworks to improve your clarity, influence, and impact | Wes Kao (coach, entrepreneur, advisor)
insight: Stop saying 'no' directly; discuss trade-offs to shift prioritization to what matters most, rather than personal helpfulness.
pull_quote: Saying 'no' doesn't come naturally for most of us people-pleasers. Instead, talk about trade-offs: 'Yes, I can do that, but it means X will have to wait.' This shifts it from 'are you helpful?' to 'what's most important?'

episode_title: The engineering mindset | Will Larson (Carta, Stripe, Uber, Calm, Digg)
insight: Stop coddling engineers; holding them truly accountable is the key to unlocking their growth and placing them in senior leadership.
pull_quote: We often treat engineers a little bit like children instead of giving them the responsibilities to thrive as adults. Now, we get to actually hold them accountable, which means we can put them in senior roles and help them grow.

episode_title: The engineering mindset | Will Larson (Carta, Stripe, Uber, Calm, Digg)
insight: When applying systems thinking, remember reality is always right; if your model conflicts, your model is wrong, not reality.
pull_quote: A lot of people who get too far in systems thinking make the same mistake where they think reality is wrong. Reality is never wrong. Your model is always wrong if it's in conflict with reality.

episode_title: The engineering mindset | Will Larson (Carta, Stripe, Uber, Calm, Digg)
insight: The most effective strategies are often "boring" constraints, like sticking to existing tools, because they focus energy and accelerate real progress.
pull_quote: Often good strategy is so boring, it's hard to talk about. A really good strategy for almost all companies is: we just use the standard kit we already have today. This focuses people’s energy on problems we value.

episode_title: The engineering mindset | Will Larson (Carta, Stripe, Uber, Calm, Digg)
insight: To truly align EM and PM incentives, give them the exact same performance rating, forcing shared accountability for outcomes.
pull_quote: My biggest thing has been trying to force this idea that EM/PM pairs are peers and they generally have the same performance rating. This drives shared accountability for the entire set of constraints, not just functional ones.

episode_title: The engineering mindset | Will Larson (Carta, Stripe, Uber, Calm, Digg)
insight: Don't treat engineering metrics like DORA as definitive performance scores; they're diagnostic tools to uncover where to focus and improve.
pull_quote: These [DORA metrics] are really good diagnosis metrics. Your deployments being slow doesn't make you a good company or a bad company, it just tells you where you should focus on improving. Don't use them to judge.

episode_title: Yuhki Yamashata
insight: Don't just build a tool; offer a new, potentially controversial, way of working that customers will passionately champion as a revolution.
pull_quote: The emotional response to your product has to be almost irrational. People love Figma because it offers a philosophy around a different, sometimes controversial, way of working that they want to champion.

episode_title: Yuhki Yamashata
insight: PMs must uniquely own the 'why' behind any product or feature; it’s the only way teams can scale and make great local decisions autonomously.
pull_quote: While 'what' and 'how' are shared, the 'why' is uniquely the PM's responsibility. If everyone understands *why* we're doing this, they can make great local decisions, and that's the only way you can really scale.

episode_title: Yuhki Yamashata
insight: True customer obsession means actively seeking out even the most obscure, low-engagement feedback to find 'canaries in the coal mine.'
pull_quote: Dylan drops tweets with zero likes into our 'Concerning Tweets' channel. He feels there's an 'essence of truth' to them, helping us spot bigger issues by balancing vocal minority with data.

episode_title: Yuhki Yamashata
insight: Foster personal accountability for quality by finding creative ways to make *everyone* in the company use your product as much as possible, daily.
pull_quote: We became a 'deck culture' to force PMs to use Figma, and HR uses FigJam for performance reviews. The more hours people spend in your product internally, the better it naturally becomes.

episode_title: Yuhki Yamashata
insight: Traditional OKRs are often performative and fail; focus instead on legibility, actionability, and authenticity in your goals, whatever you call them.
pull_quote: OKRs were the bane of my existence. They often felt performative, either anchoring to meaningless metrics or top-level goals you couldn't control. Goals need legibility, actionability, and authenticity.

episode_title: How to grow a subscription business | Yuriy Timen (Grammarly, Canva, Airtable)
insight: Most early-stage PMs miss that improving onboarding is almost *always* a huge opportunity, potentially 2-4x-ing early activation rates.
pull_quote: Don't underestimate onboarding. Early-stage companies can easily 2-4x activation rates by just fixing this, while later-stage still see 20-30% lifts. It's almost always a big opportunity.

episode_title: How to grow a subscription business | Yuriy Timen (Grammarly, Canva, Airtable)
insight: Don't try to force network effects if they're not inherent to your product from day one; it's extremely difficult to manufacture them.
pull_quote: True product network effects are rarely something you can just 'manufacture' later on. They're either there from inception, making it easy to lean in, or they're an uphill battle to create.

episode_title: How to grow a subscription business | Yuriy Timen (Grammarly, Canva, Airtable)
insight: While others cut paid, disciplined companies with strong unit economics are winning big by leveraging decreased competition and lower CPMs.
pull_quote: In a market downturn, disciplined companies with strong cash and unit economics are actually *winning* on paid platforms. They benefit from decreased competition and lower ad costs while others pull back.

episode_title: How to grow a subscription business | Yuriy Timen (Grammarly, Canva, Airtable)
insight: Successful companies often have 80%+ of their growth from one core engine; don't get distracted by diversification until that core is strong.
pull_quote: It's a myth that successful companies have highly diversified growth engines. Oftentimes, 80%+ of growth comes from one core strategy. Don't diversify until that one is truly firing.

episode_title: How to grow a subscription business | Yuriy Timen (Grammarly, Canva, Airtable)
insight: Early and consistent customer research, from interviews to surveys, always pays off by providing clarity and galvanizing teams.
pull_quote: Want to galvanize your team and get clarity? Invest in proper customer research. User interviews and surveys always pay off, especially early on, creating immense momentum.

episode_title: The non-technical PM’s guide to building with Cursor | Zevi Arnovitz (Meta)
insight: As a non-technical PM, you now have superpowers to build and ship real products yourself, completely solo, with AI tools.
pull_quote: I have zero technical background, did music in high school... when Sonnet 3.5 came out, it felt like someone came up to me and said, 'You have superpowers now.'

episode_title: The non-technical PM’s guide to building with Cursor | Zevi Arnovitz (Meta)
insight: Don't just ask AI to code; create a 'CTO' agent to challenge your ideas and make technical decisions, preventing costly mistakes.
pull_quote: If regular ChatGPT was a CTO, it would go along with your dumbest ideas. I created a 'CTO' with the custom prompt of it being the complete technical owner of the project to challenge me, not be a people pleaser.

episode_title: The non-technical PM’s guide to building with Cursor | Zevi Arnovitz (Meta)
insight: Don't trust one AI's code; have multiple models with distinct 'personalities' peer review each other's work to catch more issues, like having different dev leads.
pull_quote: I will have each of them review the code. I have a /command called peer review which basically says, 'Other team leads found these issues. Either explain why they're not real or fix them yourself.'

episode_title: The non-technical PM’s guide to building with Cursor | Zevi Arnovitz (Meta)
insight: When AI makes a mistake, ask it *why* it failed, then update its system prompt or tooling based on its introspection to prevent future errors.
pull_quote: When Claude fails, I ask it, 'What in your system prompt or tooling made you make this mistake?' Then I update your tooling and documentation so this mistake never occurs again. It's one of the biggest hacks for productivity.

episode_title: Lessons from Airtable’s unconventional growth strategy | Zoelle Egner
insight: Small, unsexy details like polished sample content or proofread emails build brand trust faster than you'd expect, making customers feel understood and rooted for.
pull_quote: It's small stuff, but it tells that person, 'The people who worked on this were thinking about me as a customer.' That builds up brand trust and makes people want to root for you. When you are small, you need everyone rooting for you that you can possibly get.

episode_title: Lessons from Airtable’s unconventional growth strategy | Zoelle Egner
insight: Even in high-stakes situations like a pandemic, starting with a 'laughably small MVP' delivers immediate impact and reveals true user needs faster than over-building.
pull_quote: By the end, we had custom software. In the beginning it was truly, basically, a spreadsheet and phones. Even that had tremendous impact at one of the most important parts in the pandemic, but also gave us so much information about what we actually needed to build.

episode_title: Lessons from Airtable’s unconventional growth strategy | Zoelle Egner
insight: Proactively over-investing in new sign-ups, even non-buyers, helps identify future champions and effectively seeds internal company virality.
pull_quote: In the early days, we'd email sign-ups to get feedback, then over-invest in one person to help them build their first base. If they were successful, they'd essentially become support for everybody else, catalyzing word-of-mouth.

episode_title: Lessons from Airtable’s unconventional growth strategy | Zoelle Egner
insight: Instead of trying to create a new software category, focus on elevating an existing or new profession, because people identify with jobs, not budget line items.
pull_quote: Creating your own category is often a waste of time. It's a huge lift, and I'm not always sure it's helpful. Instead, elevate a profession. A job is an identity, and people will fight for an identity. A category of software is a line item on your budget.

insight: Word of mouth, driven by consistently providing immense value, is the only truly sustainable and scalable growth engine for content products, far outweighing direct marketing efforts.
pull_quote: I tried every growth lever: paid ads, SEO, referrals, BD. None worked. The only way my newsletter grew to 1M subscribers was old-fashioned word of mouth, by simply being useful.

insight: Prioritize personal curiosity and passion over chasing virality or perceived 'market fit' to sustain long-term engagement and avoid burnout in product creation.
pull_quote: Don't chase virality. I write 100% about what genuinely interests me, even if it won't 'do well.' Sustaining a product for years means prioritizing your stamina and playing an 'infinite game.'

insight: AI will shrink engineering teams and raise the PM bar, shifting focus to discovery and GTM, but significantly increasing PM influence and strategic scope.
pull_quote: AI will shrink product teams by 25-50%, increasing PM influence and scope. PMs will spend less time on building, more on discovery and GTM. The bar for strategic product leadership is rising.

insight: Scale your output and quality by strategically delegating specific tasks to a small team of specialized freelancers, rather than aiming for full-time hires.
pull_quote: You don't need a full-time team to scale. I built a 1M-subscriber newsletter with zero full-time employees, relying on freelancers who specialize in leveling up quality and handling delegable tasks.

insight: Initial product growth relies on consistent, high-value publishing, amplified by strategic content partnerships or viral content series, not broad marketing tactics.
pull_quote: For early growth, forget paid ads. My newsletter took off by consistently publishing valuable content every week, augmented by a few strategic guest posts and a viral content series.

episode_title: 14 habits of highly effective product managers
insight: Your communication quality reflects your thinking quality; it is the most fundamental PM skill, more than strategy or execution.
pull_quote: People judge the quality of your thinking by the quality of your writing and speaking. Effective communication is the *most* fundamental PM skill you need to master.

episode_title: 14 habits of highly effective product managers
insight: Building an 'I've got this' aura means being highly selective, saying no often, then over-delivering on your few commitments.
pull_quote: Great PMs build an aura of 'I’ve got this'. They are very selective with what they take on (say *no*!) and then spend more time than anyone else on those chosen tasks.

episode_title: 14 habits of highly effective product managers
insight: Team dysfunction comes from misalignment. Proactively fixing differing information or priorities is a PM's highest-leverage work.
pull_quote: Team dysfunction often stems from simple misalignment on information, motives, or priorities. Hunting for and fixing this is some of the highest-leverage work a PM can ever do.

episode_title: 14 habits of highly effective product managers
insight: Great PMs always have a strong POV, but model adaptability by publicly and quickly changing their stance with new information.
pull_quote: Great PMs always have a point of view, but loosely held. They bring novel insights, but publicly change their stance when new information arises, showing true adaptability.

episode_title: 14 habits of highly effective product managers
insight: No matter how confident, PMs are frequently wrong about customer needs, making consistent, structured customer interaction systems crucial.
pull_quote: No matter how confident PMs are about what customers want, they are frequently wrong. Great PMs create systems to interact with customers regularly, like weekly 'Lunch with a customer'.

episode_title: 25 proven tactics to accelerate AI adoption at your company
insight: AI adoption isn't about vague mandates; provide specific workflows, lead by example in real time, and focus on the 'how' to demonstrate value.
pull_quote: Only 8% of U.S. workers use AI daily, but successful teams provide specific tactics, not just mandates. Whoop's Head of Product shows PMs live how she solves problems with AI, creating strong incentives to try it.

episode_title: 25 proven tactics to accelerate AI adoption at your company
insight: To drive AI adoption, make its use part of performance reviews and transparently track usage and business impact across teams.
pull_quote: People will change their behavior with the right incentives. Shopify asks employees to rate colleagues on AI usage in performance reviews, while Ramp publishes AI power user counts to create accountability.

episode_title: 25 proven tactics to accelerate AI adoption at your company
insight: Expedite AI tool approvals and provide learning budgets; otherwise, employees will use unapproved personal accounts, posing security risks.
pull_quote: Most companies have long approval processes for AI tools, but employees are already using AI from personal accounts. Duolingo gave every employee $300 to try AI tools, incentivizing experimentation.

episode_title: 25 proven tactics to accelerate AI adoption at your company
insight: Build an AI-forward culture by empowering internal enthusiasts to teach others and making 'upleveling' part of promotion criteria.
pull_quote: Every company has AI power users itching to share what they've learned. Make 'upleveling others' part of promotion criteria, as Whoop does, to formalize teaching and foster an AI-forward culture.

episode_title: 25 proven tactics to accelerate AI adoption at your company
insight: Prioritize AI initiatives by targeting high-volume, repetitive tasks that save significant time, and always demand rigorous data and evaluations, not just flashy demos.
pull_quote: Most companies fail at AI adoption by trying to do everything at once. Duolingo rebuilt content creation with AI, going from 100 courses in 12 years to 150 courses in just 12 months. Demand data, not just shiny demos.

episode_title: 28 Ways to Grow Supply in a Marketplace 📈
insight: Clearly displaying potential earnings is often the single most effective way to attract marketplace supply, as obscuring it severely hurts growth.
pull_quote: At Airbnb, the earnings estimate was an order of magnitude more effective than any other value prop. Any time we hid or obscured it, growth dipped. It’s critical that this estimate is realistic.

episode_title: 28 Ways to Grow Supply in a Marketplace 📈
insight: To overcome the 'cold start' problem, build single-player utility for the supply side, making the platform valuable even without immediate demand.
pull_quote: OpenTable sold software to restaurants that created value for them without requiring any diners. This 'single-player mode' for supply is crucial, as supply is king in most marketplaces.

episode_title: 28 Ways to Grow Supply in a Marketplace 📈
insight: Identify your marketplace's 'magic number' of supply needed to unlock an inflection point in demand conversion, then relentlessly pursue it.
pull_quote: Our co-founder Nate Blecharczyk determined that 300 listings, with 100 reviewed listings, was the magic number for Airbnb to see growth take off in a market. Bookings growth saw a step-function change.

episode_title: 28 Ways to Grow Supply in a Marketplace 📈
insight: Strong reputation systems with user reviews are crucial; high reputation effectively overcomes inherent biases like perceived dissimilarity in marketplaces.
pull_quote: When you have less than three reviews, nothing changes. But if you’ve got more than ten, everything changes. High reputation beats high similarity. The right design can overcome our most deeply rooted biases.

episode_title: 28 Ways to Grow Supply in a Marketplace 📈
insight: Marketplace success comes from many small wins, not silver bullets. Focus on user value, experiment widely, and double down on what shows promise.
pull_quote: There were no silver bullets—success came from many wins building on each other. Most things we tried didn’t have an impact, but enough did. It all only made sense in hindsight. Focus on user value and double down on what works.

insight: Sustained product growth stems purely from consistent delivery of high-quality value, not from clever strategy or design.
pull_quote: Things that don’t matter: your design, your title, your strategy, your growth plan, vision . . . most things. Things that matter: quality, consistency. There’s no trick to making something like this grow.

insight: Define your product's 'job to be done' for a specific audience and strive to execute it better than anyone, or you'll struggle for relevance.
pull_quote: Start by figuring out what job you’re doing for someone by answering: Who *exactly* is your audience? What is the *concrete* job you’re doing for them? If I can do this job super-well for you, consistently, you’ll keep reading.

insight: Product success demands disproportionate time on core value creation, not auxiliary tasks like optimizing design or crafting elaborate strategies.
pull_quote: I spend a median of 10 hours per post. Some posts take hundreds of hours. Any time you have allotted for working on your newsletter that is not writing (and making your writing better) is time wasted. This gives you an advantage.

insight: Ruthlessly edit your communication, cutting 30-50% of words and intros, to ensure maximum clarity and immediate value.
pull_quote: The biggest lesson I’ve learned is to cut, cut, and cut some more. Cut every word that isn’t necessary. Make your intro 50% shorter. Get into the meat of your post as quickly as possible. 'Start your story right before you get eaten by the bear.'

insight: To truly add value, your product or content must contribute *new* ideas or insights, often derived from deep primary research.
pull_quote: Most writing is a rehashing of something that’s already been said. What *new* ideas, insights, and concepts are you contributing? This is what people want. My top posts are from spending tens of hours doing primary research and uncovering new insights.

episode_title: 60 ideas to boost your growth
insight: For early products, temporary "Turbo Boosts" like viral videos or limited offers are crucial to kickstart initial user acquisition and learn quickly.
pull_quote: Your long-term "Growth Engines" are vital, but when starting, "Turbo Boosts" are incredibly valuable. They bring in users, giving you a chance to learn what’s working, and often help kickstart your engine.

episode_title: 60 ideas to boost your growth
insight: Cutting through market noise requires growth tactics that are remarkable, often leveraging surprise, humor, controversy, or solving an immediate problem.
pull_quote: With so many businesses being created every year, standing out is only getting harder. To get people’s attention, you need to do something special. Something remarkable.

episode_title: 60 ideas to boost your growth
insight: Never mistake a successful "Turbo Boost" for product-market fit or sustainable growth; these one-off events are unreliable, non-scalable, and won't prove long-term viability.
pull_quote: Although "Turbo Boosts" can be powerful, do not fall into the trap of over-relying on them. They are an unreliable source of growth, neither scalable nor repeatable, and won’t help you find product-market fit.

episode_title: A comprehensive survey of Product Management
insight: Companies value Communication, Execution, and Product Sense most for PM hires, often less so for Design/UX, Empathy, or raw intelligence.
pull_quote: Survey reveals Communication, Execution, and Product Sense are top skills for PM hires. Surprisingly, Design/UX, Empathy, and raw intelligence are valued least frequently by companies.

episode_title: A comprehensive survey of Product Management
insight: PMs get promoted by demonstrating significant business impact, but crucially, also by keeping external stakeholders onboard, countering the "mini-CEO" myth.
pull_quote: Forget the 'mini-CEO' myth: PMs get promoted by showing huge business impact AND keeping external stakeholders onboard. Keeping your team and manager happy is often a distant third.

episode_title: A comprehensive survey of Product Management
insight: PMs tend to overestimate their influence; they believe they "run the show" twice as often as non-PM colleagues perceive.
pull_quote: PMs often overestimate their influence: 13% of PMs think they 'run the show,' but only 6% of non-PMs agree. There's a clear perception gap on PM power.

episode_title: A founder’s guide to community
insight: Community efforts must align with clear business objectives (SPACES model) that shift based on your product's stage, from pre-PMF feedback to scaling support.
pull_quote: The objective that startups focus on depends on their current stage. Pre-PMF is Product feedback, Growth is Acquisition & Engagement, Maturity is Support & Success.

episode_title: A founder’s guide to community
insight: Don't assume users join for belonging; they seek tangible benefits, so research their needs and motivations *before* building, not after.
pull_quote: Your customers are going to join your community because of benefits, not belonging. Belonging comes *after* someone has been a part of a community and formed relationships.

episode_title: A founder’s guide to community
insight: Not every community action has a directly measurable ROI; focus on overall health and alignment, accepting that some value is qualitative.
pull_quote: If your company expects every single community action and program to have measurable ROI, then you have unrealistic expectations and you should step back.

episode_title: A founder’s guide to community
insight: Launching a community successfully means starting incredibly small (10-50 founding members) to intentionally seed the culture and content before scaling.
pull_quote: The biggest mistake I see companies make when launching a community is going too big too fast. Start small with 10 to 50 founding members to shape the cultural mold.

episode_title: A founder’s guide to community
insight: Scale community events by empowering members to self-organize using a host playbook, but first, find "community-market fit" by running events yourself.
pull_quote: Where companies are able to really scale their community is by empowering members to self-organize events. Create a host playbook and start small, finding market fit.

episode_title: A free year of Devin: the world’s most advanced autonomous AI software engineer
insight: Autonomous AI engineers aren't just tools; they can become a team's top code contributor, radically compressing project timelines and costs.
pull_quote: Gumroad's #1 code contributor is an AI engineer, merging over 1,500 PRs. Nubank migrated a 6-million-line ETL monolith in weeks with AI, a project that previously needed 18 months and 1,000 engineers.

episode_title: A free year of Devin: the world’s most advanced autonomous AI software engineer
insight: AI engineers automate low-level development tasks, freeing PMs to concentrate exclusively on strategic discovery: defining what to build and ensuring market fit.
pull_quote: Autonomous AI engineers take on routine development tasks. This frees PMs to focus more time on figuring out *what* to build and how to get it into users' hands.

episode_title: A free year of Devin: the world’s most advanced autonomous AI software engineer
insight: AI engineers expand beyond coding, assisting with strategic tasks like roadmap scoping, QA, documentation, and initial data analysis across the product lifecycle.
pull_quote: AI software engineers aren't just for writing code. They're used for scoping roadmap ideas, QA, updating documentation, and even initial data analysis requests across product teams.

episode_title: A guide for finding product-market fit in B2B
insight: Product-market fit is not a binary state but a continuous process of satisfying larger market segments, often never feeling "done."
pull_quote: Even at 100M DAUs or $100M ARR, founders like YouTube and Databricks never felt they had full product-market fit. It's always about stretching to the next target.

episode_title: A guide for finding product-market fit in B2B
insight: Achieving product-market fit demands extreme, often absurd, obsession with making your first few customers successful, even deploying engineers onsite.
pull_quote: Figma's co-founder called a "red alert" for a single alpha user, stating, 'There are customers depending on us.' Retool shipped a major on-prem feature in 36 hours for an early client.

episode_title: A guide for finding product-market fit in B2B
insight: Expect 2 years from idea to feeling B2B product-market fit, and 9-18 months from a working product, before worrying about lack of traction.
pull_quote: The median time from idea to feeling B2B product-market fit is roughly 2 years, with some exceptions like Figma taking 4+ years. Don't panic too early.

episode_title: A guide for finding product-market fit in B2B
insight: When early users eagerly adopt your B2B product, you should almost certainly charge significantly more than you initially think.
pull_quote: Amplitude's co-founder was advised to 'charge more.' He considered $50/month, then thought $100, then tried $1,000. The customer called it 'so cheap, amazing.'

episode_title: A guide for finding product-market fit in B2B
insight: A critical signal of PMF is a palpable shift from you pushing your product to the market actively pulling it from you, often through organic virality.
pull_quote: Lenny quotes Dropbox's Drew Houston: a 'visceral sense of your thing taking on a life of its own and lurching forward, like getting pressed into the back of your seat by a fast car.'

episode_title: A guide to AI prototyping for product managers
insight: AI prototyping tools enable PMs to transform designs or PRDs into functional apps in minutes, drastically accelerating discovery and idea validation without coding.
pull_quote: We just created a working prototype of a CRM in less than five minutes—something that would have previously taken weeks of an engineer’s time. Unbelievable.

episode_title: A guide to AI prototyping for product managers
insight: PMs should choose AI prototyping tools strategically; v0 for beautiful designs, Replit for internal tools/data, Bolt for flexible designs, and Lovable for production apps.
pull_quote: Choose v0 for beautiful designs by default, Bolt for quick prototypes with flexible designs, Replit for internal tools or products that store or transform data, and Lovable for building production apps.

episode_title: A guide to AI prototyping for product managers
insight: Non-technical PMs overcome AI prototyping issues by forcing the AI to reflect and plan, breaking tasks into small batches, and being hyper-specific in prompts.
pull_quote: The most important strategy for useful outputs is Reflection: forcing the AI to build a plan first vs. going straight to code. Also, break prompts into smaller batches and be hyperspecific.

episode_title: A playbook for fundraising
insight: Series A isn't just about revenue; it's proving a credible value hypothesis where a market slice deeply values and regularly uses your product.
pull_quote: Series A is the 'Product-Market Fit Round': You want to show you’ve convinced a slice of your market your product is valuable enough to pay for (or use regularly), and that this user base is growing.

episode_title: A playbook for fundraising
insight: Your pitch needs a compelling narrative tying product, vision, and market strategy, not just impressive but disconnected data points.
pull_quote: If you can’t tell a credible and compelling story about your vision and how your plans will capitalize on broader societal, market, cultural, economic or other trends, you’re dead in the water.

episode_title: A playbook for fundraising
insight: VCs invest in your future potential, not your present need; they want to see how their capital multiplies the value of their equity stake.
pull_quote: Many founders only start fundraising when they are running out of money. VCs don’t invest because you’re running out of money – they invest because they believe their equity stake will be worth a lot more in the future.

episode_title: A playbook for fundraising
insight: Rapid, defensible growth often excites investors more than higher but slower ARR, especially if the business seems sustainable.
pull_quote: Investors ultimately want to invest in growth. If a company is growing fast relative to its peers, this can make up for lower ARR, especially if the business seems defensible and sustainable.

episode_title: A playbook for fundraising
insight: Coordinate investor meetings and diligence closely to keep all potential funders on similar timelines, creating competitive tension for term sheets.
pull_quote: Try to keep all the funds on similar timelines. Don’t create a market of one buyer that was able to get to a decision fastest, which can force you to reject better funds later.

episode_title: A PM’s guide to influence
insight: Influence starts not with presenting, but by proactively understanding each stakeholder's unique goals, fears, and decision-making process.
pull_quote: Don't just pitch your project; dig deep into each stakeholder's world. Know their OKRs, their competing projects, who they listen to, and what concerns them. This intel shapes how you win them over.

episode_title: A PM’s guide to influence
insight: To persuade, reframe your proposal from the stakeholder's perspective, demonstrating how it helps *their* goals, not just your team's.
pull_quote: "Negotiation is the art of letting the other side have your way." Instead of focusing on your team's goals, frame your proposal around how it directly helps your stakeholders achieve their most important objectives.

episode_title: A PM’s guide to influence
insight: Build a coalition before the big meeting by separately engaging detractors to understand concerns and champions to reinforce support.
pull_quote: Never just show up and hope for the best in a controversial exec meeting. Prepare key attendees beforehand; you're building a coalition and converting detractors *before* the decision moment.

episode_title: A PM’s guide to influence
insight: People won't listen to you until they feel fully heard and validated; play back their concerns in your own words.
pull_quote: When stakeholders raise concerns, don't immediately counter. First, make them feel heard by playing back their statements. "What I'm hearing is you're worried about X..." This opens them to your perspective.

episode_title: A PM’s guide to influence
insight: Take control of meeting time, explicitly stating the desired outcome upfront and assertively redirecting tangents to reach a decision.
pull_quote: As the conductor of the meeting, manage the clock. Start by stating the concrete desired outcome, then ruthlessly steer discussions back to decisions to ensure you walk out with what you need.

episode_title: A product manager’s guide to web3
insight: Web3 PMs prioritize community success over traditional acquisition, engagement, or revenue metrics; if the community fails, the project fails.
pull_quote: Web3 PMs are responsible for the success of communities rather than increasing acquisition, engagement, or revenue. In web3, if the community—such as the NFT creators, liquidity providers, or software developers—is not successful, you are NGMI.

episode_title: A product manager’s guide to web3
insight: Web3 PMs prioritize rapid execution over long-term vision or strategy, as the fast-paced web3 world makes plans quickly obsolete.
pull_quote: Web2 PM careers reward setting long-term vision and crafting strategy. Not in web3. The best web3 PMs spend 95/5 on execution/vision, not 70/30, quickly hammering out specs, QA'ing, building hype, and launching.

episode_title: A product manager’s guide to web3
insight: Web3 PM is more art than science, relying on intuition over data and A/B testing as web3 ethos limits tracking and user bases are small.
pull_quote: Web3 product management is more art than science because there is very little user data and A/B testing given web3 values and small user bases. This requires more intuition and the ability to make decisions in a very high-ambiguity environment.

episode_title: A product manager’s guide to web3
insight: Web3 PMs do not unilaterally control roadmaps; DAOs govern most protocols, requiring PMs to lead by gaining community consensus.
pull_quote: User ownership over the protocol or dapp means the community is a participant in decision-making, not an afterthought. PMs don't have full autonomy; product leadership must happen through DAOs, like Uniswap needing a vote for Polygon deployment.

episode_title: A product manager’s guide to web3
insight: Web3 PMs are versatile generalists, directly handling user research, data, design, BD, and marketing for lean, interdependent web3 teams.
pull_quote: Because web3 is so new, and teams lack traditional organizational design, PMs have to do everything: listen to Discord, run on-chain analysis, sketch UX, form partnerships, write blog posts, and make memes.

episode_title: A Three-Step Framework For Solving Problems 👌
insight: Product failures often stem from mistaking a desired solution for the actual user problem you're trying to solve.
pull_quote: "Nothing is more certain to cause a project to fail than a misunderstanding of the problem you are solving." We built "social travel" thinking travelers wanted to hang out, but they really wanted high-quality non-touristy things to do.

episode_title: A Three-Step Framework For Solving Problems 👌
insight: A strong problem statement is a single, solution-agnostic sentence, focused on a user need (what and why), not a broad business goal.
pull_quote: Most "problem statements" are too broad or already assume a solution. A truly strong one is a single, solution-agnostic sentence, focused on a user need, clearly stating the 'what' and 'why'.

episode_title: A Three-Step Framework For Solving Problems 👌
insight: Assume your team and stakeholders have different understandings of the problem; actively align them early to prevent costly downstream misalignment.
pull_quote: "Problem statements are like silver burritos. Everyone on your team has a unique version of the problem in their heads." Eradicate this misalignment early, before you get too deep into design/eng.

episode_title: A Three-Step Framework For Solving Problems 👌
insight: Regularly review the core problem statement in design reviews and stakeholder updates to combat scope creep and ensure the solution stays on target.
pull_quote: "We often don’t hold on to the problem we set out to solve" during execution. Nothing reduces scope creep more than returning to the problem statement and success metrics in every review.

episode_title: A year free of PostHog ($16,500 value): The all-in-one analytics, experimentation, feature flag, surveys, session replay, error tracking, data warehouse, LLM analytics platform
insight: Building a successful all-in-one platform is incredibly difficult, but mastering it can unlock generational business potential.
pull_quote: The all-in-one platform strategy is incredibly hard to pull off. I stupidly passed on investing in PostHog because I didn’t think it would work. But if you pull it off, you can build a generational business.

episode_title: A year free of PostHog ($16,500 value): The all-in-one analytics, experimentation, feature flag, surveys, session replay, error tracking, data warehouse, LLM analytics platform
insight: The holy grail for PMs is a truly integrated workflow, seamlessly connecting session replays, analytics, experimentation, and feedback.
pull_quote: Being able to follow an issue from a session recording, to its impact in analytics, to shipping a fix as a feature flag, to testing a variant, to collecting feedback with surveys—that’s the holy grail.

episode_title: A year free of PostHog ($16,500 value): The all-in-one analytics, experimentation, feature flag, surveys, session replay, error tracking, data warehouse, LLM analytics platform
insight: AI is transforming product analytics, allowing natural language queries for data, automated dashboard creation, and intelligent summarization of session replays.
pull_quote: PostHog AI lets you ask questions about your data, magically create dashboards, build surveys using natural language, and summarize session replays.

episode_title: Accelerating Growth at Scale 🔥 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: As marketplaces scale, the viable growth levers reduce dramatically to a core set of eight, requiring a strategic shift from early 'don't scale' tactics.
pull_quote: Early on, you do things that don’t scale. At some point, though, you need to scale the things you’re doing, reducing levers from sixteen to only eight common ones.

episode_title: Accelerating Growth at Scale 🔥 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Performance marketing is the #1 growth lever for 70% of scaled marketplaces, often measured by 'Time to Contribution Margin Break-Even,' not LTV.
pull_quote: Performance marketing becomes the single most commonly used growth lever for 70% of scaled marketplaces. DoorDash invests based on 'Time to Contribution Margin Break-Even', not LTV.

episode_title: Accelerating Growth at Scale 🔥 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Geographic expansion is the #2 growth lever for 65% of scaled marketplaces; new markets often reach critical milestones faster as you get better.
pull_quote: For many years, geographic expansion was a huge lever for Instacart; as they got better at it, new markets hit milestones faster and faster. OpenTable also found it a top driver.

episode_title: Accelerating Growth at Scale 🔥 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Dedicated product investment to solve customer complaints and optimize conversion is a major growth driver for 50% of scaled marketplaces.
pull_quote: 'We paid close attention to our customer feedback and solved top customer complaints with product investments.' This helped new customers have a great first experience, boosting growth.

episode_title: Accelerating Growth at Scale 🔥 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: There are no silver bullets in growth; sustained progress comes from relentlessly applying many 'lead bullets' and iterating, not single hacks.
pull_quote: Many early growth levers are 'moment in time things' that no longer work at scale. There are no silver bullets, unfortunately. Just lots of 'lead bullets'.

episode_title: Adding a work trial to your interview process
insight: Work trials often expose a significant gap between interview performance and actual job capability, making hiring decisions surprisingly clear.
pull_quote: "It makes it obvious who to hire. It is *frequently* surprising how someone performs relative to what we thought in the interviews about their skills." Forget interviews—real work reveals real talent.

episode_title: Adding a work trial to your interview process
insight: Design work trial projects with strategic ambiguity to assess a candidate's judgment, prioritization, and ability to "riff" on real problems.
pull_quote: "We don’t want to be super-specific because I don’t want to tell anyone what to do. This is all about choice." Give vague tasks to see how candidates prioritize and think, not just follow instructions.

episode_title: Adding a work trial to your interview process
insight: Work trials act as a powerful two-way filter, assessing both candidate fit and their genuine commitment to the specific role and company culture.
pull_quote: "Many people generally don’t need much convincing [to do a work trial]... Thoughtful applicants see this as a positive, as they can evaluate the team and company better." It's a myth that candidates resist work trials.

episode_title: Adding a work trial to your interview process
insight: As companies grow, the most realistic work trials become impractical, often necessitating a trade-off for shorter, synthetic projects with less signal.
pull_quote: "Our work trial task... isn’t actual work we’d use or ship. It used to be actual work. We changed it to representative work because: 1. Actual work comes with tricky implications... 2. The codebase is now way too big and complex." Scaling your hiring process means accepting trade-offs in work trial realism.

episode_title: All the ways to grow your product
insight: Channel partnerships can be game-changing for distribution and credibility, but treat them as high-risk, slow-burn plays, not early growth engines.
pull_quote: Kayak struck a deal with AOL in '04 to take over travel search, giving them huge attention and cred. Netflix put coupons in DVD player boxes from Toshiba and others. These aren't easy, but they change the game when they hit.

episode_title: All the ways to grow your product
insight: Geographic expansion is a massive, one-time growth accelerant; deploy it post-PMF, but before your core engine is maxed out.
pull_quote: Instacart, OpenTable, and Grubhub founders all cited geo-expansion as a top growth lever. It's a huge accelerant, but you can only truly do it once, typically after product-market fit.

episode_title: All the ways to grow your product
insight: Growth strategies like channel partnerships and geographic expansion are mid-stage accelerants, not early kickstarts or continuous engines.
pull_quote: Don't confuse growth strategies across stages. Channel partnerships and geo-expansion aren't for early traction or continuous loops; they're powerful 'mid-stage growth accelerants' deployed post-PMF.

episode_title: An AI glossary
insight: Measuring AI system performance with structured evals is a critical, yet often overlooked, skill for building successful AI products.
pull_quote: Top product leaders describe 'Evals' as the most critical, yet overlooked, skill for building successful AI products. These structured measurements define 'good' for your AI and quantify progress.

episode_title: An AI glossary
insight: AI agents exist on a spectrum of autonomy; PMs must intentionally design proactive, planning, and self-iterating behaviors for product success.
pull_quote: AI systems become 'agentic' when they proactively act, make their own plans, take real-world actions, draw on live data, and create their own feedback loops. Think of AI agents as a spectrum.

episode_title: An AI glossary
insight: Preventing AI hallucinations often boils down to providing real-time, relevant context through RAG, rather than solely relying on the model's pre-trained knowledge.
pull_quote: One common source of AI 'hallucinations' is when you don't give the model the context it needs. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) acts like an open-book test, preventing the AI from confidently making things up.

episode_title: Autonomy vs. direction - Issue 35
insight: Most team disagreements stem from different information; assume you're wrong and seek out the data your team has that you don't.
pull_quote: Most disagreements are the result of having different information. When you find your team heading in the wrong direction, come back to the facts. What does your team see that you don’t? You may find they know more than you give them credit for.

episode_title: Autonomy vs. direction - Issue 35
insight: Consensus-driven teams rarely build great products, slow everything down, and are often less optimal than setting clear expectations for consulting or collaboration.
pull_quote: I’ve rarely seen a great product come out of consensus-driven teams, while some of the best products come out of the *least* consensus-driven cultures (e.g. Apple, Tesla, Amazon). Operating by consensus slows everything down and wastes time.

episode_title: Autonomy vs. direction - Issue 35
insight: Don't fear making the final decision; your team often prefers you make a call and move forward after being heard, using a 'disagree and commit' model.
pull_quote: There's a reason there's a boss on a team: to make decisions. Your team often prefers you make a call and move on after being heard. Jeff Bezos famously says he 'disagrees and commits all the time' to avoid slow decision cycles.

episode_title: Autonomy vs. direction - Issue 35
insight: Occasionally go against your best judgment (e.g., 10% of the time) on two-way-door decisions to boost morale and empower your team.
pull_quote: It’s OK to occasionally give your team a chance to prove you wrong, especially if it’s a two-way-door decision and not a large investment. Use this '90/10 rule' as a pressure release valve to avoid morale issues.

episode_title: Autonomy vs. direction - Issue 35
insight: As a leader, always have a strong point-of-view, even if you plan to change it, to guide your team and reveal the true path forward.
pull_quote: Many leaders don't know what they believe. Always ask yourself: 'If I take people’s feelings out of the equation, what would I do?' This simple question often reveals the right answer and provides a clear point of reference for your team.

episode_title: Become a more technical product manager
insight: Grasping the client-server-database model and API function is foundational for PMs to understand product capabilities and constraints.
pull_quote: Almost every software product is built with the same three parts: a client, a server, and a database. Your client communicates with your server through an application programming interface, or API.

episode_title: Become a more technical product manager
insight: PMs significantly improve product quality by conducting end-to-end and user acceptance tests with engineers, catching issues pre-production.
pull_quote: Testing is a high-leverage area for PMs to quickly improve your product quality. A quick round of end-to-end testing with your engineering team before a major release will help them build context and catch issues.

episode_title: Become a more technical product manager
insight: Familiarity with Git concepts like branches, commits, and pull requests helps PMs understand engineering progress and potential bottlenecks.
pull_quote: Engineers use Git to track changes, committing work from local copies to a shared repository. They create branches to make changes in isolation, then submit pull requests for review before merging back to the main branch.

episode_title: Become a more technical product manager
insight: Continuous Integration/Deployment (CI/CD) automates testing and deployment, allowing teams to ship code rapidly and reliably, like Stripe's 400 deployments daily.
pull_quote: CI/CD helps teams move quickly and keep the pace of shipping code to production high. Stripe's annual letter highlights more than 400 deployments per day and 1.4 million automated tests!

episode_title: Becoming a senior Product Manager
insight: Senior PMs don't just build features; they proactively define long-term strategy, prioritizing market expansion and vision over immediate user requests.
pull_quote: Newer PMs tend to prioritize work based on how much incremental value it adds to the product. Senior PMs prioritize work based on how much it moves the product towards the long term vision, often focusing on expanding the customer base.

episode_title: Becoming a senior Product Manager
insight: True autonomy as a senior PM isn't just competence; it's proactively sharing your challenges and solutions with your manager to earn trust.
pull_quote: Ironically, if you have a high level of autonomy it can be harder to earn trust. Your manager won’t see all the great decisions you make, so you’ll need to intentionally share your work with them.

episode_title: Becoming a senior Product Manager
insight: Senior PMs abandon rigid frameworks, deeply understanding cross-functional goals to navigate ambiguous 'it depends' scenarios with structured reasoning.
pull_quote: The more senior you get, the more you’ll be faced with decisions where the right answer is 'it depends.' Senior PMs recognize these decisions and reason through them in a structured way.

episode_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
insight: Mastering AI evaluations, not just prompt engineering, is quickly becoming the defining skill for AI Product Managers to drive real impact.
pull_quote: Every PM obsesses over prompts and LLMs, yet almost no one masters evaluations, the hidden lever behind every exceptional AI product. Evals decide if your product thrives or dies; they're the defining skill for AI PMs in 2025 and beyond.

episode_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
insight: Unlike deterministic traditional software tests, AI evals are non-deterministic and qualitative, requiring a mindset more akin to giving someone a driving test.
pull_quote: Evaluating AI systems is less like traditional software testing and more like giving someone a driving test. Traditional unit testing is like checking if a train stays on its tracks; AI evals are like driving a car through a busy city.

episode_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
insight: LLM-based evals offer a highly scalable, automated way to get human-like feedback on AI outputs, allowing PMs to write evaluation prompts directly.
pull_quote: LLM-based evals are highly scalable, like a human labeling your data but much cheaper, and PMs can write evaluation prompts directly. They can generate explanations for judgments, making debugging more reliable.

episode_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
insight: Effective AI evaluation requires a continuous, iterative workflow from gathering user data and initial evaluation to refining prompts and production monitoring.
pull_quote: Evals aren’t just a one-time check. Gathering data, writing evals, analyzing results, and integrating feedback is an iterative workflow from initial development through continuous improvement after launch.

episode_title: Breaking into growth
