insight: To transition into a specialized PM role, proactively and consistently voice your ambition to your manager, asking for a joint plan to build necessary skills.
pull_quote: Want to move into growth PM? Ask. This step alone was the primary reason I got the chance. My manager knew it was important, so they helped me grab the opportunity.

episode_title: Breaking into growth
insight: Becoming a growth PM demands mastery across a wide range of disciplines: from robust experimentation and growth loops to SEO, paid marketing, virality, and advanced data skills like SQL.
pull_quote: Many think growth PM is just A/B testing, but it's far broader. You need robust experimentation, advanced growth strategy, SEO, paid marketing, virality, conversion optimization, plus strong data and SQL.

episode_title: Breaking into growth
insight: Theoretical learning isn't enough; actively seek opportunities to 'do the job you want' by designing experiments, running campaigns, and getting hands-on growth experience.
pull_quote: Learning theory will get you only so far. Find time to do some actual growth work. As someone smart said, 'Do the job you want.' Demonstrate you can succeed.

episode_title: Build your personal AI copilot
insight: AI tools are only effective for intelligent knowledge work when given deep, ongoing context, mirroring what a human colleague needs.
pull_quote: LLMs can be highly effective for intelligent knowledge work, but only when we provide them with the same background knowledge that any person would need to do the same work.

episode_title: Build your personal AI copilot
insight: Treat AI as a thinking partner that inspires rather than replaces your thinking; this approach significantly sharpens your product output.
pull_quote: Remember my fear of losing my edge? When we treat LLMs as partners that inspire rather than replace our thinking, our output only gets sharper.

episode_title: Build your personal AI copilot
insight: AI copilots support high-level PM tasks like strategic decisions, roadmap brainstorming, data analysis, and soft skill development.
pull_quote: Their copilots challenge their thinking, analyze data, apply decision frameworks, prioritize, and help rehearse hard conversations.

episode_title: Build your personal AI copilot
insight: Building an AI copilot is like onboarding a teammate, requiring role instructions, company knowledge, and dedicated initiative threads.
pull_quote: With Projects, building your AI copilot is much like bringing on a new teammate. Each project is made up of three elements: project knowledge, instructions, and chat threads.

episode_title: Build your personal AI copilot
insight: Informally “gossiping” daily updates to your AI copilot keeps its context fresh, enabling better, more relevant support over time.
pull_quote: I call this habit “gossiping” to AI, because I fill in my copilot the same way I’d lean over and vent to a colleague sitting next to me. Even if I don’t have any action items, my AI copilot remembers and references these updates later.

episode_title: Building a referrals program
insight: When built strategically, referral programs can become a company’s most efficient and highest-quality growth channel, outperforming traditional paid acquisition.
pull_quote: For Airbnb, the host referral program grew to be the single biggest driver of attributable supply growth. It was also their most efficient paid-growth channel, driving the highest quality supply.

episode_title: Building a referrals program
insight: Don't build a referral program without strong existing word-of-mouth, a large user base, and a product that benefits from trust-based recommendations.
pull_quote: Referral programs only add fuel to an existing fire; they don't start one. They work best if most growth is already word-of-mouth, you have many users, and your product requires trust.

episode_title: Building a referrals program
insight: Always factor in cannibalization when evaluating a referral program; many referred users would have signed up organically, so adjust your impact metrics conservatively.
pull_quote: Don't assume all referred users are incremental. Many would have signed up anyway, so you need to conservatively "haircut" your numbers to understand true impact. This is tricky but essential.

episode_title: Building a referrals program
insight: Don't blindly increase referral incentives; they can plateau in effectiveness and disproportionately increase fraud, requiring robust detection mechanisms.
pull_quote: Doubling referral bonuses doesn't always double impact; incentives plateau. Higher incentives also invite fraud, so you must make the cost of fraud execution higher than its benefit.

episode_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
insight: Don't start AI evaluations with generic metrics; use rigorous human error analysis to discover specific, real user problems.
pull_quote: The most common mistake in AI evals is starting with 'fashionable metrics' like hallucination or toxicity. This often leads to tracking scores disconnected from actual user problems. Instead, ground your evals in reality through error analysis.

episode_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
insight: AI product requirements aren't fully specifiable upfront; true success criteria emerge by iteratively reviewing outputs and articulating what feels 'wrong.'
pull_quote: Research shows people aren't good at specifying complete AI requirements upfront. True success criteria emerge through reviewing outputs and articulating what feels 'wrong.' This iterative process defines what you truly want.

episode_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
insight: For human-labeled ground truth in AI evaluations, use binary pass/fail judgments instead of Likert scales, as binary forces clarity.
pull_quote: Many teams use 1-to-5 Likert scales for AI evaluations, but this is a trap. Binary decisions force clarity for establishing ground truth, while Likert scales introduce subjective and inconsistent nuance.

episode_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
insight: Don't rely solely on accuracy for AI judge performance; use True Positive Rate (TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) to understand specific mistake types.
pull_quote: A single accuracy score is dangerously misleading for AI judge performance, especially with imbalanced datasets. Instead, True Positive Rate (TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) tell you precisely how your judge is likely to make mistakes.

episode_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
insight: In RAG systems, prioritize evaluating and fixing the retriever (information finding) before the generator, as missing information makes the generator useless.
pull_quote: A RAG system is a two-part machine: a retriever finds information, and a generator writes an answer. Evaluate and fix your retriever first. If the correct information isn't retrieved, the generator has no chance of success.

episode_title: Choosing a take rate
insight: Your marketplace's take rate is determined by how much convenience and demand you provide, offset by market competition.
pull_quote: Setting your marketplace's take rate isn't arbitrary. It's a formula: Convenience + Demand - Competition. Higher convenience and demand allow for higher take, but fierce competition drives it down.

episode_title: Choosing a take rate
insight: Marketplaces (which drive demand) command higher take rates (10-50%) than platforms (5-15%) that primarily offer convenience.
pull_quote: Don't confuse marketplaces with platforms when setting fees. Marketplaces that bring new customers can charge 10-50% take rates, but platforms mainly offering convenience are stuck around 5-15%.

episode_title: Choosing a take rate
insight: In winner-take-all markets, often a lower take rate is better, reducing friction and deterring competitors, even if it feels counterintuitive.
pull_quote: Bill Gurley argues it's often better to *lower* your take rate. High fees create friction and invite competitors, especially in winner-take-all markets, making a greedy rake dangerous.

episode_title: Choosing a take rate
insight: Your initial take rate is a starting point; continuously adjust it by enhancing convenience, improving demand, or implementing tiered pricing.
pull_quote: Don't set your take rate in stone. Successful platforms constantly tweak fees. You can increase your take by adding features or better demand, or lower it strategically through subscriptions or volume discounts.

episode_title: Choosing Your North Star Metric
insight: A North Star Metric isn't just a number; it fundamentally defines your entire product strategy, making them inseparable.
pull_quote: "Your North Star Metric is your strategy, and your strategy is your North Star Metric." This isn't just a metric to track; it's the core of what you're building and how you will win.

episode_title: Communicating bad news - Issue 26
insight: Large-scale redesigns of critical user flows often fail to improve metrics, even with positive initial feedback from users.
pull_quote: I led an onboarding overhaul that took six months and ended up being negative on metrics. A key lesson: be careful investing in large-scale redesigns of a critical user flow.

episode_title: Communicating bad news - Issue 26
insight: When delivering bad news, anticipate what leadership needs: full transparency, quick disclosure, and a concrete plan for next steps.
pull_quote: The trick to sharing bad news effectively is to imagine being on the other side of the table. You'd want to know everything, as soon as possible, plus exactly how to best move forward.

episode_title: Communicating bad news - Issue 26
insight: When a project fails, proactively communicate the bad news, frame it with learnings and context, and always propose clear next steps.
pull_quote: The trick to sharing bad news effectively is to imagine being on the other side of the table. You'd want to know everything, as soon as possible, plus exactly how to best move forward. Get ahead of it and provide clear next steps.

episode_title: Communicating bad news - Issue 26
insight: Don't tie your personal identity and confidence to the success or failure of a specific project; separate yourself from its outcome.
pull_quote: Although it’s important to be excited and bought into the projects you take on, it’s also important to not build your identity around your projects. Projects will come and go; some will succeed and some won’t.

episode_title: Communicating bad news - Issue 26
insight: Sometimes, the only way to convince stakeholders a project won't work is to let the experiment run to an unambiguous failure.
pull_quote: I took on a project I knew was doomed. My colleague said, 'Let it blow up. Let it fail.' I did, and it was negative on every metric. We both saw the results plainly and were finally able to move on.

episode_title: Content-driven growth
insight: Effective content strategies vary significantly based on content creator (user/employee) and primary goal (SEO/virality).
pull_quote: I posit that there are five unique content-driven growth strategies: User-Generated SEO (UGSO), Editorially Generated SEO (EGSO), Data-Generated SEO (DGSO), User-Generated Virality (UGVO), and Editorially Generated Virality (EGVO).

episode_title: Content-driven growth
insight: Content marketing is a long-term investment, requiring years of patience and consistent dedicated resources before seeing significant growth returns.
pull_quote: Content marketing initiatives don’t generate returns as quickly as other forms of marketing, so be sure to hold your nerve. It took years for each company chronicled below to see significant payoff.

episode_title: Content-driven growth
insight: Beyond acquisition, strategic video content can dramatically boost new-user activation and conversion by effectively teaching users product value.
pull_quote: Webflow uses content in a very different way: to increase new-user success. The people who enroll in our courses are *much* more likely to become subscribers to the service.

episode_title: Content-driven growth
insight: To stand out, content must be genuinely exceptional; you're not just competing with rivals, but every piece of digital media vying for audience attention.
pull_quote: Brands need to realize they’re not only competing with direct competitors. They’re competing with every tweet, post, podcast that is vying for some of their audience’s precious time. You need to publish media that is great. Good no longer gets you results.

episode_title: Content-driven growth
insight: Integrate content creation into team goals to leverage internal expertise; this incentivizes high-quality contributions and fosters a culture of sharing.
pull_quote: Everyone at the company was incentivized to create content. By adding content creation to personal and team goals, it was clear it was part of your job and benefited you in terms of career growth.

episode_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products
insight: Building AI products often requires starting with "what is technologically possible" and prototyping, a departure from traditional problem-first approaches.
pull_quote: With AI, it is wholly unclear if something is possible to build. Often the best place to start projects is by asking, ‘What is technologically possible?’ and prototyping. This is a big mindset shift.

episode_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products
insight: For AI products, user segmentation often shifts from functional needs to attitudes toward the technology itself: "AI embracers" vs. "AI skeptics."
pull_quote: AI changes this dynamic because the most meaningful segmentation often depends on attitudes toward the technology itself: AI embracers versus AI skeptics. This impacts everything from messaging to testing.

episode_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products
insight: Proprietary data and superior interfaces will become more crucial competitive advantages than the underlying AI models, which are increasingly commoditizing.
pull_quote: The data and the interfaces may become more important than the models themselves, which are becoming increasingly commoditized. Companies that have or really understand data in a deep vertical will have an advantage.

episode_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products
insight: The most impactful AI features are often small, almost invisible improvements to frequent, mundane workflows, not "Big AI Features™" like chatbots.
pull_quote: The smallest (and almost invisible) features are usually the fan favorites. You don’t need a big idea to ship useful AI features into your app.

episode_title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products
insight: Successful generative AI products can paradoxically reduce time spent in-app by increasing productivity and giving users time back.
pull_quote: Don’t be surprised if the best AI features reduce metrics like time spent in-app. This new wave of generative AI seems to find the best PMF with products that increase productivity and give time back to people.

episode_title: Defending your big bets
insight: To protect high-risk, long-term big bets in large companies, consistently deliver small, incremental wins as 'cover fire' to maintain support and resources.
pull_quote: I like to think of a “big bet” project like a highly-experimental new weapon that you’re trying to smuggle to the front. The solution is to create “cover fire” by consistently shipping small, consistent wins.

episode_title: Defending your big bets
insight: Tactically, allocate 80% of team effort to short-term, low-risk wins and 20% to high-risk, long-term bets to sustain buy-in.
pull_quote: My rule of thumb is to spend 80% of my team’s time on short-term low-risk incremental wins, and 20% on high-risk, long-term bets. This way, we can continue to move our KPIs while bringing our big bet alongside.

episode_title: Defending your big bets
insight: Showing steady success with incremental wins doesn't just provide cover; it builds critical trust and earns the right to pursue bigger risks.
pull_quote: If you *only* work on long-term big bets, it’s difficult for leaders to give you the benefit of the doubt indefinitely. By showing steady success, you earn the right to take bigger bets.

episode_title: Defending your big bets
insight: Incubate big bets by keeping them small and avoiding excessive attention until a compelling, functional prototype can be demonstrated.
pull_quote: “Keep it small/avoid too much attention until ready.” — Sriram Krishnan, ex-PM at Twitter, Snap, FB. Don't let your big bet become an easy target for resource pulls by keeping it under the radar until you have a working prototype.

episode_title: Demand driving supply: The little-understood growth loop behind a surprising number of iconic billion-dollar companies
insight: Only lean into a demand-driving-supply growth loop if you see it happening organically; don't try to force it from a cold start.
pull_quote: If you’re thinking about exploring this viral loop for your business, my number one piece of advice is to **first see if it’s already happening. Only lean into it if you’re already seeing this behavior**, rather than trying to force it from a cold start.

episode_title: Demand driving supply: The little-understood growth loop behind a surprising number of iconic billion-dollar companies
insight: To amplify demand-to-supply conversion, identify why users convert and, crucially, why *not*, especially if they understand your full offering.
pull_quote: When you do see this growth loop happening and you want to see if you can increase the rate of conversion, **it’s imperative to figure out why, and also why not**. Why aren’t *more* users converting?

episode_title: Demand driving supply: The little-understood growth loop behind a surprising number of iconic billion-dollar companies
insight: Strategically place clear calls-to-action and consistent branding in high-traffic demand-side areas to convert users to supply.
pull_quote: SurveyMonkey's "survey complete" page, though lacking subtlety, was highly impactful in converting demand-side users to supply-side creators. Even obvious, end-of-flow CTAs can significantly boost virality.

episode_title: Demand driving supply: The little-understood growth loop behind a surprising number of iconic billion-dollar companies
insight: Measuring demand-to-supply conversion is tricky due to latency; use low-latency cohorts as leading indicators and track pre/post changes.
pull_quote: Measuring these efforts to improve buyer-to-seller conversion is not as straightforward as A/B testing a landing page or an onboarding flow. For long-latency conversions, look for low-latency cohorts as leading indicators.

episode_title: Differentiating your product
insight: Competing to be *better* (operational effectiveness) rarely sustains success; true competitive advantage comes from being *different*.
pull_quote: Constant improvement in operational effectiveness is necessary to achieve superior profitability. However, it is not usually sufficient. Few companies have competed successfully on the basis of operational effectiveness over an extended period. Competing to be unique is ultimately more sustainable than competing to be the best.”

episode_title: Differentiating your product
insight: Product differentiation isn't abstract; there are 7 concrete, proven strategies like being cheapest, highest quality, or most convenient.
pull_quote: I spent this week looking at hundreds of companies and found that there are seven common ways to differentiate: Be the cheapest, highest quality, most convenient, safest, proprietary, makes people feel great, or focus on a niche.

episode_title: Differentiating your product
insight: For early-stage products, focusing on a niche underserved market is a powerful differentiator that sidesteps direct competition.
pull_quote: An underappreciated differentiator, especially powerful in the early stages, is to laser-focus on a small, underserved market. By definition, if a market is underserved, there’s very little competition.

episode_title: Differentiating your product
insight: Don't settle for one differentiator; combining multiple strategies like quality and safety, or proprietary supply and niche, builds a much stronger moat.
pull_quote: You may have noticed, many companies have found their way to offering multiple differentiators. One differentiator is great, but more are better. Patagonia: Highest quality + makes you feel good. Apple: Highest quality + safest.

episode_title: Ecosystem is the next big growth channel
insight: In the AI era, traditional B2B growth channels are declining in effectiveness; relying on ecosystem partners is now essential, not optional.
pull_quote: The B2B channels you’ve long relied on for growth are increasingly becoming less effective. Search traffic is declining due to LLMs, social is flooded, and inboxes overflow. Ecosystem is the answer.

episode_title: Ecosystem is the next big growth channel
insight: An ecosystem isn't just another channel; it makes every existing growth channel more effective by fueling content and distribution.
pull_quote: The best reason to invest in an ecosystem is that, when done well, it makes every channel better by serving as both a distribution path and a source of content. It fuels inbound and accelerates product virality.

episode_title: Ecosystem is the next big growth channel
insight: Effective ecosystem strategies demand senior leadership buy-in and deep product involvement to design integrations and features, not just marketing.
pull_quote: The most effective ecosystem strategies aren’t bolted on or handled by a single partner marketer. Product needs to be deeply involved to design integrations, build referral mechanics, and embed as experts.

episode_title: Ecosystem is the next big growth channel
insight: Judge ecosystem partnerships by a "win-win-win" principle: the deal must benefit your company, the partner, and the prospect to be truly effective.
pull_quote: My personal bar for partnerships is that they must be a 'win-win-win' for you, the partner, *and* the prospect, never one or two out of three. Value can come as shared data or special product feature access.

episode_title: Ecosystem is the next big growth channel
insight: Go beyond competitor analysis and conduct "complement research" to map all players who have existing reach and credibility with your target audience.
pull_quote: Competitive research is common, but 'complement' research is often skipped. Identify and map all players in your ecosystem who already have reach and credibility with your audience, looking beyond the obvious.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 1
insight: Managers mistakenly take on "monkeys" (problems) from subordinates, becoming the bottleneck and enabling reverse delegation.
pull_quote: Before the two of them met, on whose back was the ‘monkey’? The subordinate’s. After they parted, on whose back was it? The manager’s. Subordinate-imposed time begins the moment a monkey successfully leaps from the back of a subordinate to the back of his or her superior.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 1
insight: Real agency combines forming autonomous goals and the relentless efficacy to solve problems, often gently finding the path of least resistance.
pull_quote: Agency, as I see it, is an amalgamation of two skills, or mental dispositions: autonomy and efficacy. Often, agency is almost gentle—an attunement to the world and the self, a finding of the path of least resistance.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 1
insight: Your product taste will always outpace your current ability; close this gap by relentlessly producing a high volume of work, not by passively waiting.
pull_quote: Your taste, the thing that got you into the game, is still killer. But what you're making isn't so good. The most important thing you can do is do a lot of work. It is only by going through a volume of work that you’re going to catch up and close that gap.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 1
insight: Focus fiercely on making 2-3 core features truly great; a product with many "good" features is probably not innovative or compelling enough.
pull_quote: Pick three key attributes or features, get those things very, very right, and then forget about everything else. If your product needs ‘everything’ in order to be good, then it’s probably not very innovative.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 1
insight: A great market is the most critical factor for startup success, powerfully pulling even a merely viable product to fulfillment.
pull_quote: In a great market—a market with lots of real potential customers—the market *pulls* product out of the startup. The product doesn’t need to be great; it just has to basically work.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 2
insight: Prioritize deeply understanding the problem and target audience before crafting any solutions; fuzziness here is a critical red flag.
pull_quote: A product succeeds because it solves a problem for people. This sounds very basic, but it is the single most important thing to understand about building good products. If the problem you’re solving isn't easy to communicate, it's a big red flag.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 2
insight: When communication fails, it's always the speaker's responsibility; humbly seek feedback to bridge the gap between intent and understanding.
pull_quote: When I’m doing a poor job of communicating it can feel like I’m pushing with a rope. It is tempting to blame the audience for not understanding. But make no mistake, when this happens, it is your fault.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 2
insight: For executive buy-in, always lead with the answer to the core question, then explain the situation, complication, and rationale (SCQA).
pull_quote: The best executive communication starts with the state of affairs. Then comes the complication. The question falls automatically out. Then the answer, first, at the top of a pyramid.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 2
insight: Even a great product can fail with the wrong distribution channel; choosing the right route to market is a critical, often neglected, strategic decision.
pull_quote: Products often fail because the company chose the wrong route to market. Selecting the right channel is critical for any business. Many new entrepreneurs demonstrate a deep misunderstanding of how sales channels should be designed.

episode_title: Essential reading for product builders—part 2
insight: Overcome 'schlep blindness' by tackling ambitious, difficult problems; their inherent complexity deters others, creating less competition and higher value.
pull_quote: Founders often shy away from hard problems (schleps). That scariness makes ambitious ideas doubly valuable: less competition because everyone else will have been frightened off by the challenges involved.

episode_title: Ethan Smith: The power of internal linking for SEO
insight: Improving internal linking is often the quickest, most impactful SEO win for growth, yet 90% of websites neglect it.
pull_quote: Consistently, we'll see [that] if you don't have good link spread, fixing it has significant opportunity. Of anything that I can recommend to companies, as the quick win, the thing to do at the start, it's this.

episode_title: Ethan Smith: The power of internal linking for SEO
insight: Google's crawlers discover pages exclusively through links; without a robust internal link structure, many pages simply won't be found.
pull_quote: Google is crawling the web and your site through links. And if you don’t have links, then Google doesn’t have paths to find all of your pages. This makes internal links make-or-break for ranking higher on search engine results pages.

episode_title: Ethan Smith: The power of internal linking for SEO
insight: Maximize SEO by linking low-traffic pages from high-authority "crawl points" to shorten the path and distribute link equity effectively.
pull_quote: Your site audit will show a small percentage of pages bringing in the most traffic. Low-traffic pages benefit from additional internal links to these pages that already rank well. You want the shortest path from a crawl point.

episode_title: Ethan Smith: The power of internal linking for SEO
insight: Counterintuitively, manually written editorial content has become more effective for driving traffic than programmatic SEO.
pull_quote: Don’t underestimate the power of the written word — editorial SEO has become more effective at driving traffic than programmatic SEO. Manually written articles and content are underappreciated for their growth potential.

episode_title: Ethan Smith: The power of internal linking for SEO
insight: While AI aids content workflow, using it for writing itself risks factual inaccuracies that harm SEO performance and user trust.
pull_quote: Although AI is useful for parts of the content creation workflow... he cautions against using it for the writing itself. The problem is that you want the sentence to be factual. Inaccuracies can have a negative impact on your site’s performance.

episode_title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea - Issue 31
insight: Most marketplace failures stem from fundamental business flaws, not just marketplace dynamics, so evaluate the core business first.
pull_quote: Most marketplaces fail not because of the marketplace, but due to fundamental reasons like lacking product-market fit or a small market. Always start by evaluating the core business idea.

episode_title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea - Issue 31
insight: A successful product demands a specific 'why now' – what's changed in tech, regulation, or user behavior that makes this idea uniquely viable today?
pull_quote: Every startup idea has likely been tried. The real question is, what has changed in the world (tech, regulation, beliefs) that makes it the right time for this idea to succeed *now*?

episode_title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea - Issue 31
insight: Poor distribution, not product quality, is the top cause of startup failure; focus on a clear, unique acquisition strategy from day one.
pull_quote: Poor distribution — not product quality — is the number one cause of failure. Most businesses get zero distribution channels to work, making a unique growth strategy critical.

episode_title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea
insight: Before optimizing for marketplace mechanics, ensure your core business solves a real problem, has a viable market, and strong distribution.
pull_quote: Most marketplaces fail not because of the marketplace, but due to fundamental reasons — like not building something people want, too small a market, or inability to acquire users. Start with the bigger picture.

episode_title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea
insight: A stellar product means nothing without an effective, often unique, distribution strategy to efficiently reach your target users.
pull_quote: Peter Thiel states: "Poor distribution — not product — is the number one cause of failure." Don't just build a great product; ensure you have a clear, unique growth strategy.

episode_title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea
insight: For marketplaces, your singular focus should be aggregating demand; without enough customers, you'll never attract or retain quality suppliers.
pull_quote: Bill Gurley, a 20-year marketplace investor, states: "Aggregating demand is the one and only key." Focus relentlessly on bringing customers to the platform.

episode_title: Evaluating a (marketplace) business idea
insight: Prevent transaction leakage by embedding value like convenience, anonymity, or protection that users can't easily replicate off-platform.
pull_quote: “The real risk to most marketplaces is that they provide all of these valuable services and, at the moment of the transaction, the transaction ‘leaks’ and the buyer and seller settle the transaction off-platform.” — Charles Hudson.

episode_title: Everyone should be using Claude Code more
insight: AI agents excel at synthesizing raw customer call transcripts into structured evidence, directly validating or invalidating product hypotheses and requirements.
pull_quote: My current Claude Code jam is synthesizing transcripts of calls with customers to compile evidence that supports or invalidates a running tally of assumptions/requirements/hypotheses/whatever.

episode_title: Everyone should be using Claude Code more
insight: AI agents can proactively identify documentation gaps and autonomously generate updates by exploring software, creating "self-driving" documentation.
pull_quote: The most interesting use case we’re playing with is something I call ‘self-driving documentation.’ Basically, how can we give an Agent the responsibility of figuring out how/where our documentation can be better and more comprehensive.

episode_title: Everyone should be using Claude Code more
insight: AI agents automate competitive analysis by scraping ad libraries and social platforms to identify working strategies and user pain points.
pull_quote: Extract ads from competitors to find the problem, use case, or copy/asset that’s working for them, and then repurpose it for my ads. Claude Code built out these scripts that would screen-grab all ads running on the ad library.

episode_title: Everyone should be using Claude Code more
insight: AI can analyze meeting transcripts or personal logs to identify behavioral patterns or discrepancies between intent and action, offering unique self-awareness.
pull_quote: I download all of my meeting recordings, put them in a folder, and ask Claude Code to tell me all of the times I’ve subtly avoided conflict.

episode_title: Everyone should be using Claude Code more
insight: AI agents streamline PM administrative tasks like generating job descriptions, drafting changelogs, or refining Linear issues by synthesizing existing data.
pull_quote: I used it to generate a full job description, hiring plan, interview plan, and rubric for a new role at Clay. I made a folder with internal Notions of our hiring material for PMs, then asked to generate the collateral.

episode_title: Examples and templates of 1-Pagers and PRDs
insight: Don't squander the unique advantage of internal archives; study your company's past successful 1-Pagers to learn what truly works.
pull_quote: If you’re at a mid-to large-size company, you have the rare gift of having access to boatloads of real-world 1-Pagers from past and current projects. Don’t squander it. Pick a few big successes, find their original 1-Pagers, and study them.

episode_title: Examples and templates of 1-Pagers and PRDs
insight: A truly effective product spec crystallizes the core problem upfront, ensuring the entire team focuses their brainpower on the same challenge.
pull_quote: Great 1-Pagers are problem-oriented: They crystallize the problem being solved in a few strong sentences—ideally near the top of the document—to focus the brainpower of every teammate in the same direction.

episode_title: Examples and templates of 1-Pagers and PRDs
insight: Provide *just enough* direction and constraints in product specs to guide engineers and designers, without eliminating their opportunity to innovate with better solutions.
pull_quote: Effective product docs give the reader just enough an idea of what the project will entail—including requirements and constraints—without eliminating the opportunity for (your super-smart) teammates to come up with even better solutions.

episode_title: Examples and templates of 1-Pagers and PRDs
insight: Define success criteria *super-specifically* to justify the project and guide tradeoffs, and include clear timelines to prevent scope explosion and keep momentum.
pull_quote: Great 1-Pagers super-specifically define what success looks like when the product ships, first to ensure it's worth doing, and later to help everyone make tradeoff decisions. There's also a clear timeline to keep the project moving and prevent scope creep.

episode_title: Examples of great job postings
insight: Treat job postings like a crucial product — invest as much thought into them as you do your sign-up flow to attract top talent.
pull_quote: Founders rarely spend as much time on job postings as their product's sign-up flow, yet it's a high ROI investment. Remarkable postings drive better applicants and strengthen your brand.

episode_title: Examples of great job postings
insight: Great job postings don't just list what you want; they proactively communicate who *isn't* a fit, filtering candidates early.
pull_quote: Many job descriptions only list desired traits. The best ones explicitly state who the role is *not* for, or 'why you may NOT be excited about us.' This is a powerful filter.

episode_title: Examples of great job postings
insight: Instead of bulleted lists, tell a compelling story that helps candidates viscerally understand the need for the role and its potential impact.
pull_quote: Most job descriptions are boring lists. The best ones tell a great story, explain the product's impact, and show you what the team is *already* like, not just what they're looking for.

episode_title: Examples of great job postings
insight: Embrace radical transparency in job postings, including salary and 'why you may NOT be excited,' to build trust and attract the right fit.
pull_quote: Radical transparency in job postings, from full salary details to 'why you may NOT be excited about us,' builds immense trust. It helps ideal candidates self-select and shows genuine company values.

episode_title: Finding your distribution advantage
insight: Building a great product is now table stakes; poor distribution, not product, is the #1 cause of startup failure.
pull_quote: Justin Kan says "Distribution > Product." Peter Thiel agrees: "Poor distribution—not product—is the number one cause of failure." Building a great product is now just table stakes.

episode_title: Finding your distribution advantage
insight: Design unique viral loops within your product to drive exponential, efficient growth, turning users into active promoters.
pull_quote: Faire incentivized supply to drive demand, and demand to drive supply, creating an ever-accelerating growth flywheel. Unique viral loops are magical for growing more quickly and efficiently.

episode_title: Finding your distribution advantage
insight: Being first on an emerging platform can provide a massive distribution advantage, but requires early experimentation and risk.
pull_quote: Zynga on Facebook, Instagram on iPhone, Pinduoduo on WeChat Mini Apps – companies broke out by riding new platforms. Tinker with new platforms when they launch to find the next big wave.

episode_title: Finding your distribution advantage
insight: A truly remarkable product or story earns free PR and word-of-mouth, but the bar for what's "remarkable" is surprisingly high.
pull_quote: Airbnb, Etsy, Zillow grew through free PR and word of mouth because they were truly remarkable. The more interesting, unique, or surprising your product or team is, the more free awareness you'll earn.

episode_title: Finding your distribution advantage
insight: Strategic partnerships are usually a time sink, but a truly rare and impactful early deal can single-handedly define your startup's distribution.
pull_quote: Netflix made a deal with DVD manufacturers, Kayak with AOL, PayPal partnered with eBay—these early strategic deals basically made these companies. Yet, relying on partnerships early on is rarely successful and often a waste of time.

episode_title: First-principles thinking
insight: Revolutionary breakthroughs come from the tedious, difficult work of questioning assumed truths, not just clever variations of existing solutions.
pull_quote: The primary downside [to first-principles thinking] is it’s really difficult. It's like building an original song from scratch, not just being a cover band playing someone else's music.

episode_title: First-principles thinking
insight: True breakthroughs often come from experimenting with ideas 'everyone knew wasn't going to work,' ignoring conventional wisdom and perceived limitations.
pull_quote: Everybody knew that you cannot train deep networks. It cannot be done. Back propagation is too weak... How could such an obvious thing be not known? People were really focused on machine learning models, where they could prove that there was an algorithm which can perfectly train them.

episode_title: First-principles thinking
insight: To innovate radically, break down a product into its fundamental material constituents and their raw market value, ignoring historical cost assumptions.
pull_quote: Historically, all rockets have been expensive, so therefore, in the future, all rockets will be expensive. But actually that’s not true. What is a rocket made of? Break it down: the raw material cost is like 2% of what a rocket costs.

episode_title: First-principles thinking
insight: Don't settle for 'good enough'; design an extreme 10-star experience first, then reverse-engineer how to industrialize the 6th or 7th star.
pull_quote: Most people try to design something that’s just good enough. But if you can add that 6th or 7th star—if you can design something really amazing—then you can reverse engineer how to industrialize this millions of times over.

episode_title: First-principles thinking
insight: Systematically question every assumption by repeatedly asking 'why' until you hit the floor, and challenging conclusions with 'what evidence do you have?'
pull_quote: The challenge is to keep asking ‘why’ until you hit the floor. Use questions like ‘Are we sure that’s true?’ and ‘What evidence do you have for that conclusion?’ to systematically dismantle assumptions.

episode_title: Five habits of highly annoying product managers
insight: Don't treat product frameworks as gospel; success comes from adapting processes to your team and getting their buy-in, not rigid adherence.
pull_quote: The best way to annoy teammates is to obsess over the 'right way' of building product. Not everything you read about will work within your specific business or team. It's more important to have a team that is bought-in and excited about your ways of working than having the perfect process.

episode_title: Five habits of highly annoying product managers
insight: Impulsive prioritization without clear rationale erodes team trust and morale; always explain the 'why' behind decisions to get buy-in.
pull_quote: If you're getting pushback on prioritization more than 50% of the time, you're probably annoying (and losing the trust of) your team. Always have a strong 'why this is a problem' argument and speak with teammates in person.

episode_title: Five habits of highly annoying product managers
insight: Stop calling projects 'quick'; this alienates your team and burns them out. Instead, collaborate by asking what conditions would make it doable in X days.
pull_quote: The best way to annoy your team is to suggest everything will be 'super-quick.' Everyone's been burned promising quick turnarounds. Instead of suggesting it will be quick, ask, 'What would need to be true for this to be doable in the next x days?'

episode_title: Five habits of highly annoying product managers
insight: Don't delay decisions waiting for full consensus; often, teams prefer you make a call and move forward with 70% of the info.
pull_quote: Most decisions should probably be made with somewhere around 70% of the information you wish you had. If you wait for 90%, in most cases, you’re probably being slow. Ask your team if they'd rather keep hashing it out or for you to just make a call.

episode_title: Five habits of highly annoying product managers
insight: Avoid micromanaging by being overly prescriptive; instead of detailing every button, prioritize conversation and give your team creative room.
pull_quote: Being overly prescriptive about what to build leaves zero room for creative thought, and often means your specs are longer than other PMs'. Prioritize conversation over documentation; don't assume more writing will solve nuanced problems.

episode_title: Five principles for successfully managing managers
insight: Transitioning from a line manager to a manager of managers is a fundamentally different role with a steeper, often unrecognized, learning curve.
pull_quote: The learning curve from line manager to manager of managers is just as steep—if not steeper—and is not just the same bag of tricks applied to a larger group.

episode_title: Five principles for successfully managing managers
insight: Force yourself to empower direct report managers by imagining you can only pull information from them, not their ICs, to make decisions.
pull_quote: “If I had zero access to any IC in my organization and could only push or pull information from my managers to make decisions, how would I go about being effective?” This forces new skip leads to grapple with how they’d create value.

episode_title: Five principles for successfully managing managers
insight: Directly solving problems for your skip reports or giving strong opinions without involving their manager disempowers your direct reports.
pull_quote: The natural instinct for new skip leaders is to offer strong opinions, solve problems, and make decisions on the spot without getting full context from anyone else. This undermines your direct-report managers.

episode_title: Five principles for successfully managing managers
insight: Covering for an underperforming direct report manager by doing their work yourself is detrimental; report issues and set clear expectations.
pull_quote: The instinct to quietly solve an issue yourself or provide cover for a direct-report manager’s underperformance is deeply rooted, but prevents crucial feedback and growth.

episode_title: Five principles for successfully managing managers
insight: Delegate projects based on a manager's task-relevant maturity (TRM) for that specific situation, then tailor review cadence by project uncertainty and impact.
pull_quote: Delegating well is so difficult because it requires constant matchmaking between a variety of projects and a variety of people. Use Andy Grove’s task-relevant maturity (TRM) to evaluate and match.

episode_title: Five proven prompt engineering techniques (and a few more-advanced tactics)
insight: Get domain-specific guidance from AI by assigning it an expert persona, helping PMs fill knowledge gaps or get diverse perspectives.
pull_quote: "You are an expert software architect known for bridging the gap between product vision and technical implementation. Help me prepare talking points..." By assigning a role, AI provides tailored, nuanced guidance, just like a human consultant.

episode_title: Five proven prompt engineering techniques (and a few more-advanced tactics)
insight: Provide AI with specific output examples (user stories, notes) to ensure consistency and adherence to team-specific formats.
pull_quote: "Here are some examples of user stories from our backlog: Generate a user story for adding a new ‘dark mode’ feature..." To get consistent results, show the AI exactly what you want, don't just tell it.

episode_title: Five proven prompt engineering techniques (and a few more-advanced tactics)
insight: Use AI to generate diverse examples like user personas or test scenarios when real data is scarce, accelerating discovery and iteration.
pull_quote: "Generate ten examples of user personas for our new fitness tracking app... then generate potential customer feedback using our user personas." Short on real user data? AI can quickly bootstrap diverse personas and feedback to speed up product discovery.

episode_title: Five proven prompt engineering techniques (and a few more-advanced tactics)
insight: For complex product decisions, instruct AI to "think step-by-step" to reveal its reasoning process, aiding in critical evaluation and logic.
pull_quote: "Think this through step-by-step: We need to decide whether to prioritize feature A or B for our next sprint. Consider development time, user impact..." For tough product decisions, make AI show its work to understand its logic and reasoning.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion, part 2
insight: Without robust data infrastructure and proper product analytics, B2B product-led growth efforts are flying blind and will fail.
pull_quote: Product-led growth is data-led growth. Without data, you are flying blind. Many B2B companies are significantly lacking in product analytics—but without a strong foundation, PLG will never work.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion, part 2
insight: Don't build your own experimentation platform; it requires significant engineering and data science expertise, making third-party tools a smarter choice for most teams.
pull_quote: The most common mistake I see is that companies skip buying and jump right into building. Building an experimentation platform requires engineering, data science, and statistical expertise. Don't reinvent the wheel.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion, part 2
insight: There's no fixed PLG team structure; start small with a dedicated growth team or tiger team, then scale and specialize as PLG proves its value.
pull_quote: There is no 'standard' PLG team setup that works for all companies; the ideal structure will change over time. Start small with a growth product team or a cross-functional tiger team, then invest more once PLG is proven.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion, part 2
insight: A Head of Growth reporting to a CRO can act as a general manager for the self-service product, owning self-service ARR to align incentives and minimize sales conflict.
pull_quote: Sometimes a head of growth reports to a CRO, acting like a general manager for the self-service product line. They own self-service ARR as a KPI, minimizing conflict with sales and aligning incentives across GTM.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion
insight: Product-Led Growth isn't just for startups; existing sales-led companies can strategically layer it on to unlock new segments or improve efficiency.
pull_quote: Many successful B2B companies, including HubSpot and GitLab, started sales-led, then layered on PLG to gain efficiency and reach new segments. It's not about choosing one, but strategically combining both motions.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion
insight: B2B PLG demands defining a cascade of 'aha moments' – from individual user to team, buyer, and paid customer – far beyond a single user's initial thrill.
pull_quote: For B2B, the 'aha moment' isn't a single event. You must identify multiple aha moments: for the individual user, their team, the ultimate buyer, and even after they become a paid customer.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion
insight: Product-led activation is 'product-led, not product-only'; a multi-channel approach with human and marketing touches is crucial, not just product onboarding.
pull_quote: Product-led activation doesn’t mean you rely only on product. The ideal experience is product-led, powered by data, and supported by automated channels and high-touch human assistance.

episode_title: Five steps to starting your product-led growth motion
insight: The biggest hurdle for established companies adopting PLG is often the internal fear of 'losing control' by offering free product access, challenging traditional sales-led gating.
pull_quote: The enterprise B2B sales cycle and PLG are inherently at odds—one is a very controlled experience and the other is not. Shifting to PLG means getting used to giving up that initial control.

episode_title: Flywheels, flywheels, flywheels
insight: A flywheel isn't just a descriptive diagram; it's a strategic tool for teams to identify and align on which core investments truly accelerate growth.
pull_quote: A flywheel is just a tool for you and your team to identify and align on which parts of the business matter most. It clarifies which investments accelerate your flywheel and which don’t.

episode_title: Flywheels, flywheels, flywheels
insight: To build a flywheel, list your core assets, actions, user needs, outputs, and optimizations, then connect the elements that directly drive each other into a reinforcing loop.
pull_quote: Start your flywheel by listing core assets, actions, user needs, outputs, and optimizations. Then, find elements that directly drive others to create a reinforcing loop.

episode_title: Flywheels, flywheels, flywheels
insight: Resist the urge to overcomplicate your flywheel; a simple, useful diagram is more effective for alignment and action than a messy, overly detailed one.
pull_quote: Don’t overcomplicate your flywheel. A simple, useful diagram with 4-6 components is better for alignment than a complex, accurate one with 'loops within loops and arrows in every direction'.

episode_title: Flywheels, flywheels, flywheels
insight: Don't stress if your flywheel isn't clear upfront; many great companies only recognize and articulate their flywheel after their success has begun to compound.
pull_quote: Oftentimes, leaders only figure out their flywheel after the fact. Don't worry if you can't map yours out perfectly from day one; the act of thinking about it is what counts.

episode_title: Fostering a culture of experimentation
insight: Hiring a dedicated data scientist as one of your first employees cultivates trust in data and shapes an experimentation culture from the ground up.
pull_quote: As an early employee, I was able to build relationships with the people that would go on to lead all sides of Airbnb. This had long term implications for the culture of data at Airbnb.

episode_title: Fostering a culture of experimentation
insight: Aligning around one quantifiable north-star metric clarifies prioritization, unifies teams, and forces leaders to embrace experimentation for measurable impact.
pull_quote: The CFO was experiencing a similar version of this problem, so we came up with the idea of establishing a north star metric: nights booked. An added (and unexpected) benefit of doing this was it propelled the company's use of experiments.

episode_title: Fostering a culture of experimentation
insight: Embed data scientists directly into product teams. They become critical partners, ensuring data informs roadmap planning and initial decisions, not just post-facto analysis.
pull_quote: Data scientists were not just 'consulted' on product, but data itself became critical to decision-making. Data scientists sat next to PMs and along with the engineers and designers were a critical voice in roadmap planning.

episode_title: Fostering a culture of experimentation
insight: To overcome data resistance, frame it as 'the voice of customers.' This shifts decision-making from opinion to fact-based, crucial for scale.
pull_quote: We made a real effort to characterize data in a human light. I liked to frame data as the voice of our customers. Data is what allows you to hear from your community at scale.

episode_title: Fostering a culture of experimentation
insight: Invest in simple, scrappy experimentation tooling early, evolving it for rapid iteration and trusted results, but avoid overbuilding before systems stabilize.
pull_quote: The search experience team... became increasingly frustrated with the amount of time it took to run experiments. So they decided to build their own experimentation tooling. It is essential to have a way to rapidly iterate.

episode_title: Freemium vs. trial
insight: Offering a free product isn't a revenue strategy; it's a critical acquisition tool to lower CAC and grab attention in crowded markets.
pull_quote: Making a product free isn’t a revenue, pricing, or monetization strategy—it’s an acquisition strategy. It’s always a means to lower CAC and get people’s attention.

episode_title: Freemium vs. trial
insight: Most successful SaaS companies don't choose between freemium and free trials; nearly 90% of freemium products also offer a trial.
pull_quote: You don’t have to choose—you can do both. Nearly 90% of freemium products also offer a 7- to 30-day trial of their paid product, with about half of all SaaS companies using both.

episode_title: Freemium vs. trial
insight: Choose free trials over freemium if your product needs human intervention, complex integration, or many stakeholders, especially with a high price point.
pull_quote: Go with a trial (instead of freemium) if your product significantly benefits from hand-holding and has a high price point. This applies if your product requires complex integration or many stakeholders.

episode_title: Freemium vs. trial
insight: For freemium, make features that drive virality and core 'aha' moments free, but charge for professional needs and higher usage limits.
pull_quote: For freemium, make features that enable spread and core 'aha' moments free. Charge for features that professionals or teams need, or those that remove annoyance for power users.

episode_title: Freemium vs. trial
insight: Avoid launching freemium too early; first, understand how to convert leads, or you risk generating noisy data and false positives.
pull_quote: Don’t implement freemium until you truly understand how to convert leads to customers. Doing it too early will only increase noise and false positives while you're still figuring out your segment beachheads.

episode_title: General management, functional, and hybrid models: Which org design works best for top companies?
insight: Companies should only adopt a GM model after achieving significant scale with multiple proven product lines, not as an early growth strategy.
pull_quote: Shifting to a GM structure typically happens after a company reaches hundreds of millions in revenue, an extensive product portfolio, and thousands of employees, like Block near its IPO. Airbnb reverted after divesting new businesses.

episode_title: General management, functional, and hybrid models: Which org design works best for top companies?
insight: A GM model suits companies with North Star metrics directly tied to revenue (e.g., transaction volume); functional models work better when product metrics are indirectly linked to revenue.
pull_quote: If your product's North Star metric is close to revenue (e.g., transaction volume for Block), a GM model is ideal. For indirect revenue drivers like user engagement (Meta), functional structures usually perform better.

episode_title: General management, functional, and hybrid models: Which org design works best for top companies?
insight: Functional models excel at delivering cohesive customer journeys that drive organic growth flywheels, which is critical for scaling products and building stickiness.
pull_quote: Shopify's shift to a functional model created a seamless merchant experience across its core back-office, amplifying organic growth by fostering product cohesion and customer stickiness.

episode_title: General management, functional, and hybrid models: Which org design works best for top companies?
insight: GM models demand deep domain experts for independent business units; functional structures thrive with flexible 'crafters' whose discipline expertise can be deployed across projects.
pull_quote: GM organizations require leaders with deep, specific domain expertise for their autonomous business areas. Functional structures, conversely, benefit more from adaptable 'crafters' who can apply their skills broadly.

episode_title: General management, functional, and hybrid models: Which org design works best for top companies?
insight: Pure GM or functional structures often create promotion bottlenecks; a hybrid model provides essential flexibility in career ladders to retain diverse senior talent.
pull_quote: Both pure GM and functional structures can limit senior leader growth, forcing them to wait for specific openings. A hybrid model offers crucial flexibility in career paths, helping retain a broader range of talent.

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: Your new product will be ignored by default; you must intentionally make it "worth making a remark about" to cut through the noise.
pull_quote: Here’s the hard truth: No one cares about your new product. As Marc Andreessen said, “everyone’s time is already allocated.” Your goal is to create something worth remarking about.

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: A truly remarkable value proposition, like "Throw away your USB drive" or "No Due Dates, No Late Fees," is a potent buzz generator on its own.
pull_quote: Offer a remarkable value prop: Robinhood’s '$0 commission stock brokerage' and Spotify’s 'All of the world’s music – for free' show how a simple, clear benefit creates immense launch buzz.

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: Don't shy away from controversy or extreme offline stunts; they grab attention and create shareable stories that traditional marketing can't.
pull_quote: Salesforce hired fake protesters. WePay dropped 600 pounds of ice at a rival’s conference. These extreme offline stunts cut through the noise and generate unforgettable launch buzz.

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: To capture attention in a saturated market, your product launch must be genuinely *remarkable* – something people *have* to talk about.
pull_quote: “No one cares about your new product.” With hundreds launching daily, you must “DO SOMETHING REMARKABLE.” It means something *worth making a remark about*, not just “neat.”

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: Engineer virality into your product or pre-launch by designing referral programs that reward users for inviting friends, like Dropbox's free storage.
pull_quote: Dropbox grew from 100k to 4M users in 15 months by giving free storage for referrals. Jet.com acquired 350k users pre-launch by giving stock options for referrals. Engineer virality.

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: Create an aura of exclusivity with an invite-only model or waitlist, then leverage influencers to validate the product and build intense FOMO.
pull_quote: Hey.com and Superhuman generated massive waitlists (180k, 275k) by being invite-only and getting tech influencers to visibly use and promote them, cultivating strong FOMO.

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: A truly remarkable value proposition can be your most powerful marketing tool, generating organic buzz by fundamentally shifting user expectations.
pull_quote: Robinhood’s "$0 commission stock brokerage" and Gmail's "Free email with 1GB storage" created instant buzz because they fundamentally redefined user expectations for a product.

episode_title: Generating buzz
insight: Breakthrough the noise with bold, unconventional offline stunts or controlled controversies that earn media and get people talking, even if provocative.
pull_quote: Salesforce hired fake protesters at a rival's event. WePay dropped 600 lbs of ice in front of PayPal. These bold, unconventional stunts generated immense buzz by being outrageous.

episode_title: Getting better at product strategy
insight: Don't just optimize current flows; define the ideal future user experience to uncover breakthrough product strategies.
pull_quote: We spent months optimizing the booking flow, but only helped a bit. Eventually, we asked, 'what would the *ideal* guest experience look like?' That led to our strategy: 'If you could see it, you could book it.'

episode_title: Getting better at product strategy
insight: Great strategy identifies the one or two critical issues – the pivot points – and then focuses action and resources only on them.
pull_quote: "Good strategy… identifies the one or two critical issues in the situation—the pivot points that can multiply the effectiveness of effort—and then focuses and concentrates action and resources on them."

episode_title: Getting better at product strategy
insight: A brilliant strategy fails if poorly communicated; make it short, memorable, and use frameworks or metaphors so everyone understands.
pull_quote: Even the most genius of strategies will fail if communicated badly. Use frameworks or metaphors, like the "CAN and WANT" framework, to make it easy to understand and remember.

episode_title: Getting better at product strategy
insight: A strategy is a living hypothesis; constantly reinforce it for prioritization and be ready to evolve it as new learning emerges.
pull_quote: A good strategy is never static. It's a hypothesis about what will work. Remind people over and over, use it for all prioritization, and be ready to evolve it.

episode_title: Getting buy-in
insight: Effective buy-in comes from co-creating ideas with your team, not presenting fully-formed solutions you developed in isolation.
pull_quote: If you co-create your idea with your team, your team will be infinitely more receptive to your suggestions. You don’t need to come up with the best idea; you need to execute on the best idea.

episode_title: Getting buy-in
insight: Eliminate weak language like 'sorry,' 'just,' or 'actually' to project confidence and instantly increase your perceived influence.
pull_quote: Natalie, a senior engineer at Salesforce, realized she said 'sorry' all the time. Within a week, her team commented that she presented more compelling ideas and showed up stronger. A seemingly simple change can have a powerful impact.

episode_title: Getting buy-in
insight: To gain buy-in, always frame your ideas by focusing on *why* stakeholders should care, directly linking it to their goals or values.
pull_quote: One big reason we fail to get buy-in is that we assume everyone else is thinking what we’re thinking. Good storytelling means you present a narrative that resonates with your audience.

episode_title: Getting more technical
insight: Non-technical PMs don't need to learn to code first; focus on foundational concepts and engineer dialogue to gain most technical benefits.
pull_quote: Many PMs feel impostor syndrome talking to engineers, but you don’t have to learn to code to see most of the benefits. Instead, start by learning basic concepts and asking your engineering partners a ton of questions.

episode_title: Getting more technical
insight: Your engineering team is your best resource for technical learning; use targeted questions to understand system architecture and development processes.
pull_quote: Engineers are experts at the very thing you want to learn, and most love nothing more than explaining their craft to curious people. Leverage them with questions like 'Can you draw how our system is architected?'

episode_title: Getting more technical
insight: Mastering core technical concepts like front-end/back-end, databases, APIs, and system architecture is a high-ROI investment.
pull_quote: Investing a few hours in learning basic terms, technologies, and concepts involved in building product will immediately level up your game. A structured lesson plan covers everything from programming basics to AWS.

episode_title: Getting more technical
insight: Understanding your product's system architecture is key to predicting timelines, anticipating blockers, and making informed trade-offs.
pull_quote: As an ex-engineer, I can tell you a technical background is a PM superpower for predicting timelines and avoiding black holes. Grasping how software systems are architected is fundamental to this skill.

episode_title: Growth ideas
insight: Generate buzz for a new product by doing something controversial, remarkably shareable, or releasing unique one-off marketing products.
pull_quote: Sometimes you just need ideas. Do something controversial to get people talking about you, or release a shareable one-off product. Orchestrate a PR stunt or create a viral video to grab attention fast.

episode_title: Growth ideas
insight: Sustainable long-term growth fundamentally relies on investing in product virality, SEO, and strategic assets, not just short-term marketing stunts.
pull_quote: Driving long-term top-of-funnel growth means increasing your product's virality, investing in SEO, or launching a content company. It's about building sustainable channels, not chasing fleeting buzz.

episode_title: Growth ideas
insight: For mature products, driving expansion revenue from existing users often generates more growth than solely acquiring new customers.
pull_quote: To improve monetization, double down on expansion revenue instead of endlessly acquiring new customers. Consider charging power users more or bundling your product with a valuable bonus.

episode_title: Growth ideas
insight: Optimizing conversion isn't always about removing steps; sometimes adding guidance or even a crucial step improves user success and activation.
pull_quote: Improving conversion and activation isn't always about simplifying. Sometimes you need to add a step, provide more guidance, or even schedule one-on-one onboarding to truly help users succeed.

episode_title: Growth ideas
insight: Improve retention by offering 'pause' options instead of outright cancellation, and crucially, by stopping the acquisition of low-intent users.
pull_quote: To genuinely improve retention, consider letting users 'pause' or 'snooze' their subscriptions instead of forcing cancellation. More importantly, stop acquiring low-intent/quality users who will inevitably churn.

episode_title: Growth inflections
insight: Contrary to the 'Next Feature Fallacy,' a single, high-leverage product improvement can be the direct catalyst for a massive growth inflection.
pull_quote: The biggest inflection for Figma began with Team Libraries. The sheer amount of leverage it provided to design teams made Figma an obvious winner against competitors.

episode_title: Growth inflections
insight: Significant growth inflections often come from external events, sometimes engineered through PR or influential users, pushing a product to new audiences.
pull_quote: Tinder's growth spiked after an Olympic snowboarder mentioned it at the Sochi Games. This shows how capitalizing on unexpected media attention can drive massive awareness and growth.

episode_title: Growth inflections
insight: The most durable growth inflections stem from aggressively optimizing your primary growth engine, leveraging community content for SEO or strategic partnerships.
pull_quote: Airtable's biggest growth inflection came from adding templates, which kicked off their SEO flywheel and accounted for up to a third of total site traffic. Community-seeded content was key.

episode_title: GTM motions of 30 B2B SaaS companies
insight: 100% of successful product-led companies eventually add a sales team, often shifting completely sales-led for enterprise adoption and scaling.
pull_quote: Of the 30 companies I looked at, 19 started off product-led, but 100% of them added a sales team to assist with growth/expansion. Zero went the other way (from sales-led to product-led).

episode_title: GTM motions of 30 B2B SaaS companies
insight: Every B2B SaaS company eventually moves upmarket because it's easier to add enterprise features than simplify a complex product for SMBs.
pull_quote: Every one of the companies I looked at moved upmarket. It is easier to add things to adapt to enterprise than it is to start complex and then simplify over time.

episode_title: GTM motions of 30 B2B SaaS companies
insight: Most B2B SaaS companies start by targeting VSBs or SMBs for faster decisions and early adoption, prioritizing customers likely to survive.
pull_quote: Of the 30 companies, 21 went after VSBs and SMBs early on. Default to SMB, prioritize customers who are likely to survive. Follow the pain and find the early adopters.

episode_title: GTM motions of 30 B2B SaaS companies
insight: Successful B2B SaaS companies initially focus on one (max three) specific personas to avoid product sprawl and achieve clear positioning.
pull_quote: Everyone I looked at, other than Dropbox and Notion, had a very specific role or function they targeted within organizations. Attacking everyone will pull your product in different directions.

episode_title: GTM motions of 30 B2B SaaS companies
insight: Many sales-led companies strategically add a self-serve product, not for direct revenue, but to generate leads from users who want to "kick the tires."
pull_quote: Many sales-led companies added a self-serve product they use primarily to generate leads for their sales teams. You won’t ever know that you missed out on customers who want to try your product without sales.

episode_title: Healing your co-founder relationship
insight: To navigate team friction, first identify your own underlying feelings (disappointment, resentment) to expand choices and avoid reactive responses.
pull_quote: Many leaders jump to fixing team issues externally. But start inward: what are *you* feeling? Disappointed, resentful, angry? Understanding your own emotions expands your choices and lessens thoughtless reactions.

episode_title: Healing your co-founder relationship
insight: When confronting colleagues, describe their specific *behavior* and its *impact on you*, avoiding assumptions about their intent or motives.
pull_quote: If you’re having conflict, don't say, "You always have to have the last word." Instead, "stick with your reality": describe their behavior and how *you* feel, without attributing motives or judgment.

episode_title: Healing your co-founder relationship
insight: Resolve team conflicts by simultaneously considering "What do I need?", "What do you need?", and "What is best for the organization?".
pull_quote: When resolving conflict, you must answer three questions simultaneously: "What do I need?", "What do you/others need?" and "What is best for the organization?" That superordinate goal shifts blame to joint problem-solving.

episode_title: Healing your co-founder relationship
insight: Accurately distinguish if team tension is rooted in objective business factors or subjective interpersonal behavior, as resolution strategies differ.
pull_quote: Is your co-founder relationship fraying due to market conditions, strategic disagreements, or something else? Or is it primarily their *behavior*? Interpersonal issues are often more challenging to resolve, complicating core business dilemmas.

episode_title: Hiring your early team
insight: Some B2B startups hire a PM as one of their first three employees to focus on core UX, anchor product operations, or free up founders.
pull_quote: Three companies hired a PM within their first three hires: Coda, Persona, and Snyk. Snyk's CEO hired a PM early because he believed security products' fit for developers was a product problem, not a tech problem, needing UX breakthroughs. He wanted to free himself for company building.

episode_title: Hiring your early team
insight: Your first hire shouldn't always be an engineer; consider subject-matter experts, recruiters, or customer success to de-risk or differentiate.
pull_quote: Over two-thirds of companies hired an engineer as #1, but Vanta hired a compliance expert, Coda a recruiter, and Segment a customer success person. Vanta's CEO stated, 'We wanted the check on are we building the right thing, given our background wasn’t in the space.'

episode_title: Hiring your early team
insight: Don't hire a full-time salesperson too early; wait until founder-led sales consistently generates $300-500k ARR or you stop learning.
pull_quote: Most founders wait until $300-500k ARR to hire their first full-time salesperson. Retool’s CEO, David Hsu: 'The main thing you get from doing sales yourself is that you have a very good sense of how customers think about the product.'

episode_title: Hiring your early team
insight: Your first sales hire should be an entrepreneurial 'hungry senior AE' focused on generating insights and shaping GTM, not just closing deals.
pull_quote: Your first salesperson should be a 'product person' who generates insights, not a typical closer. Gusto's CPO: 'In the beginning, you really are looking for a product person that’s all about generating insights. Their job in the beginning is to experiment.'

episode_title: How a traumatic brain injury made me a better PM—and person
insight: PMs should map their energy levels to identify "energy killers" in their calendars, then restructure their weeks to maintain sustainable output.
pull_quote: The frame of viewing my calendar as a tool for energy management was a game changer. I had to systematically think through how to manage energy troughs and maintain the right levels of energy across the week.

episode_title: How a traumatic brain injury made me a better PM—and person
insight: Imposing artificial constraints, like shorter work hours, forces PMs to ruthlessly prioritize high-leverage tasks, improving effectiveness and team empowerment.
pull_quote: Shorter hours raised the bar for what I considered worthy enough to invest my time in, which naturally led to better prioritization of my work. It’s not prioritization unless it hurts.

episode_title: How a traumatic brain injury made me a better PM—and person
insight: PMs can quickly calm their nervous system and prevent emotional spirals during high-stakes meetings or difficult interactions using deep breathing.
pull_quote: I’ll often do this type of breathing to calm myself down before product reviews or important meetings where I expect things to get a little spicy. Even just 20 to 30 seconds can bring me closer to baseline.

episode_title: How a traumatic brain injury made me a better PM—and person
insight: Reduce professional anxiety by explicitly writing fears, estimating probability, and outlining concrete steps to survive or navigate the worst-case scenario.
pull_quote: My primary fear was that I’d never be able to return to work. I played out the scenario of never being able to work again. I knew that while not being able to work would be a very painful future, I would find a way to survive if it did end up happening.

episode_title: How a traumatic brain injury made me a better PM—and person
insight: When facing project setbacks or career plateaus, zoom out to evaluate progress over months or years, not just days, for a more accurate view of growth.
pull_quote: How wide is your frame of reference? Even if the preceding days or weeks had not been great, if I zoomed out to take a wider frame of reference, it was clear that the overall trend line was positive.

episode_title: How AI will impact product management
insight: AI will significantly disrupt high-level PM skills like strategy and vision *before* it replaces soft skills like communication or empathy.
pull_quote: I believe the truth is the complete opposite. AI will have the most profound impact on the high-level (and historically most valued) skills of product management: developing a strategy, crafting a vision, identifying new opportunities, and setting goals.

episode_title: How AI will impact product management
insight: As AI automates hard skills, soft skills like influence, communication, product sense, and empathy become your most valuable assets.
pull_quote: Soft skills like product sense, communication, creativity, and being the glue that enables a team to operate at their very best will become even more important (and a differentiator among companies).

episode_title: How AI will impact product management
