insight: Ditch large brainstorming sessions for naming; instead, use small, disciplined teams to generate thousands of bold, fresh ideas, guided by a strategic framework.
pull_quote: Creating the right name requires more than one or two brainstorming sessions. Large brainstorming sessions with four+ participants usually deliver marginal results. You need to explore again, generating at least 1,000 creative ideas.

episode_title: How to find the perfect name
insight: A name's sound and emotional resonance often matter more than its literal description, changing how people experience a product rather than just labeling it.
pull_quote: The best names don’t describe reality; they change it. Don’t underestimate the power of sound: imagine the sound of your brand first. Swiffer made mopping feel like a quick, joyful action, not just a label.

episode_title: How to get better at influence
insight: Don't just pitch your goals; reframe your ask to directly address the other team's north-star metric or personal objectives to secure buy-in.
pull_quote: Initially, my pitch was around my team’s goals. I adjusted my pitch to focus on what the search team cared about most: increasing guest-side conversion. I told them this had a good shot at actually increasing conversion, and that we would run a specific A/B test to find out. They finally agreed, and results were positive.

episode_title: How to get better at influence
insight: Influence isn't about power; it's a "trust battery" you charge through consistent reliability and helping others, which you can then draw upon for big asks.
pull_quote: My favorite way to think about trust in the workplace is as a battery—as Tobi Lütke (CEO of Shopify) popularized: 'People sort of think about trust as almost an on/off kind of thing. Like, I trust my mother, I don’t trust the NSA. But it’s really a gradient … with a lot of different points on [the] spectrum.'

episode_title: How to get better at influence
insight: For deep influence, slow down and take others on your full thought journey, explaining the "why" and vision rather than jumping to the "what."
pull_quote: Rush people into a quick decision, and they’ll either quickly change their mind or it’ll later likely blow up in your face. Instead, if you take the time to make sure the other person truly sees what you see, and understands why this is the correct (and only) path, they’ll follow you, and stick with you through difficult times.

episode_title: How to get better at influence
insight: Your past success is your most powerful influencer; consistently hitting goals and making good calls builds credibility that makes future asks easier.
pull_quote: If you’ve often been right before (e.g. have hit your goals, shipped great stuff, made the right calls), people are more likely to trust that you know what you’re doing, and go along with your suggestions. As it should be.

episode_title: How to get better at influence
insight: Don't assume disagreement is personal; often, people just need more compelling evidence, so lead with the data that convinced you.
pull_quote: Influencing others is often as simple as showing them hard data. If you’re struggling to convince someone, think about what evidence convinced you of this path. Often we think someone disagrees with us because of a personal issue, when in reality you just don’t have a convincing case.

episode_title: How to get into product management
insight: The PM role is often thankless, nerve-racking, and all-consuming; success comes from solving problems and driving growth, not seeking appreciation.
pull_quote: The PM role is often thankless, nerve-racking, and all-consuming. Don't pursue it for appreciation or having your way. It's for those fulfilled by solving problems and driving growth, not personal glory.

episode_title: How to get into product management
insight: Starting your own company, though work-intensive, is a viable path into PM as founders naturally develop crucial product skills for future roles.
pull_quote: The most work-intensive, yet effective, path into product management? Starting your own company. Founders often transition into PM roles after acquisition or as their company scales.

episode_title: How to get into product management
insight: Communication isn't merely a PM skill, it *is* the job; every output and influence you have happens through writing, speaking, and meetings.
pull_quote: Engineers code, designers design, and product managers… communicate. Everything you do is through writing, speaking, and meetings. As Boz puts it, communication *is* the job.

episode_title: How to get into product management
insight: A PM's ability to execute and ship is so fundamental that a team is better off without a PM who cannot consistently get things done.
pull_quote: A PM good at nothing else but execution is still valuable. But a team with a PM who *can't* execute is better off without them. Execution is non-negotiable for product managers.

episode_title: How to get into product management
insight: The most underrated yet valuable PM trait is the 'I got this' aura, built by being exceptionally prepared, detail-oriented, and having a high bar.
pull_quote: The most underrated, yet valuable, PM trait? The 'I got this' aura. It's built by being exceptionally prepared, detail-oriented, and holding yourself to a higher bar than anyone else.

episode_title: How to get promoted
insight: Promotions rarely come from busy work; consistently delivering significant, measurable business impact is the most direct path.
pull_quote: Companies desperately want to give more responsibility to people who deliver results. If you aren’t getting promoted, it usually means you aren’t seen as someone who can consistently deliver significant business impact. Focus on hitting ambitious goals or saving the company money.

episode_title: How to get promoted
insight: Don't wait for a promotion; proactively demonstrate you can handle the next level's scope and responsibilities to prove your potential.
pull_quote: People don’t get promoted for doing their jobs really well. They get promoted by demonstrating their potential to do more. Show you can handle the next level by taking on increased responsibility *before* you're officially asked.

episode_title: How to get promoted
insight: Proactively identify your specific skill gaps with your manager, create a concrete action plan, and consistently show progress to unlock the next level.
pull_quote: To get promoted, you'll need to demonstrate you’ve addressed the specific skill or behavior keeping you from the next level. Push your manager to articulate these 2-3 gaps, then build a concrete action plan and review it monthly for accountability.

episode_title: How to get promoted
insight: Beyond your performance, securing an influential champion to fight for you in promotion calibrations is an often-underestimated necessity.
pull_quote: The most underappreciated, and often most powerful, ingredient in getting promoted is to have someone with influence fight for you in the calibration process. Do a great job, then arm them with your wins and impact to advocate effectively.

episode_title: How to get promoted
insight: Every company has unwritten rules for promotion; observe who gets promoted to understand the true priorities beyond the career ladder.
pull_quote: There’s a reason no two company career ladders are alike; explicitly or implicitly, every company prioritizes different skills and behaviors. Pay attention to who gets promoted to see what *actually* matters at your company.

episode_title: How to get the most out of your product pass, part 1
insight: Specialized AI prototyping tools empower PMs to generate high-quality, style-consistent prototypes for customer feedback, bypassing generic building limitations.
pull_quote: While many tools build apps, Magic Patterns is 'designed specifically for PMs and designers' to create the 'highest-quality, most useful, and most correct' prototypes. It excels at quick iterations using existing product styles for customer feedback.

episode_title: How to get the most out of your product pass, part 1
insight: AI tools like Gamma automate the creation of polished presentations and documents from simple prompts, dramatically reducing the 'annoying parts' of PM work like deck building.
pull_quote: 'If Cursor is making engineers faster, Gamma is doing the same for PMs.' This AI tool generates polished presentations, landing pages, and documents from a simple prompt, even turning PRDs into decks.

episode_title: How to get the most out of your product pass, part 1
insight: AI-powered terminals like Warp enable PMs to execute complex technical tasks and solve system problems without engineering assistance, dramatically expanding their operational capabilities.
pull_quote: Warp, an agentic terminal replacement, makes you 'feel like a superhero' by using AI to solve any Mac terminal problem you run into. This means PMs can analyze data or install packages without technical friction.

episode_title: How to get the most out of your product pass, part 1
insight: Best-in-class task management tools like Linear are integrating AI agents to handle initial task passes, allowing PMs to delegate tickets and streamline workflow without manual hand-offs.
pull_quote: Linear, the project management tool Lenny says every modern product team should use, now features 'deep agent integrations.' You can delegate tickets to an AI agent to take a first pass at tasks. The future is now.

episode_title: How to get the most out of your product pass, part 1
insight: Curated design reference libraries like Mobbin save PMs hundreds of hours, providing instant access to best-in-class flows for benchmarking and design inspiration.
pull_quote: 'I wish this existed when I was a full-time PM.' Mobbin is the world's largest mobile and web design library, saving 'hundreds of hours of screenshotting competitors’ flows' to unblock design thinking and benchmark products.

episode_title: How to get your entire team prototyping with AI
insight: Ensure AI prototypes maintain branding and high visual quality by building reusable component libraries from screenshots, browser extensions, or actual code.
pull_quote: Component libraries are the first big improvement you can bring to your team. They allow you to maintain branding and consistent styling without having to manually clean up each prototype to look like your product.

episode_title: How to get your entire team prototyping with AI
insight: Scale AI prototyping beyond individual exploration with "baselines and forks" workflows for rapid, consistent team iteration from a polished starting point.
pull_quote: Baselines and forks are best used when you want to explore multiple different ideas without having to rebuild the starting page each time. They provide your team with another leg up in getting high-quality results quickly.

episode_title: How to get your entire team prototyping with AI
insight: AI prototyping introduces "medium fidelity"; PMs must strategically match prototype fidelity to the specific PDLC stage and audience to avoid wasted effort.
pull_quote: It’s critically important to choose the appropriate fidelity for your prototype based on the context, and then set clear expectations with team members around that level of fidelity.

episode_title: How to get your entire team prototyping with AI
insight: Prototypes significantly accelerate and clarify communication across the entire product development lifecycle, making abstract ideas concrete for all stakeholders.
pull_quote: Using the prototype for communication is a much faster and easier way to drive these important conversations than hoping an engineer reads your whole PRD and adds a comment or two.

episode_title: How to get your entire team prototyping with AI
insight: AI-generated code from prototyping tools is generally useless for engineering teams; manage expectations and don't assume direct code hand-off.
pull_quote: It’s important to remember that the code generated by AI prototyping tools is mostly useless to your engineering team... It doesn’t follow any of the existing patterns, use any of the same libraries, or may not even be written in the same programming language.

episode_title: How to get your marketing team to drive more impact
insight: Use the GACCS framework (Goals, Audience, Creative, Channels, Stakeholders) as a mandatory brief for all marketing projects to ensure alignment and prevent wasted effort.
pull_quote: Much of the work marketers produce is a waste of time. The secret to an effective marketing team isn’t expertise, but a simple 5-part GACCS brief that keeps teams from busywork and drives cross-team alignment.

episode_title: How to get your marketing team to drive more impact
insight: Share detailed briefs like GACCS with stakeholders early to get feedback before work begins, preventing wasted effort and late-stage rework.
pull_quote: It’s a waste of time to get feedback from reviewers if it’s too late in the process or they don’t have the necessary context. Share the GACCS with key stakeholders before you do the work.

episode_title: How to get your marketing team to drive more impact
insight: Project execution struggles often trace back to a missing marketing foundation: unaligned OKRs, undefined ICPs, or unclear brand story.
pull_quote: If you are struggling to write a GACCS, it might mean you don’t have alignment on your marketing foundation. This includes shared OKRs, ideal customer personas, your overall story, and primary growth levers.

episode_title: How to get your marketing team to drive more impact
insight: A project is useless without a clear distribution plan; effective marketing gets "mileage" by repurposing content across multiple channels.
pull_quote: Distribution is just as important as, if not more important than, what you are creating. If no one is going to see it, all the time you’ve spent making it is useless. Get 'mileage' from your content.

episode_title: How to get your marketing team to drive more impact
insight: Implement an Areas of Responsibility (AORs) list to explicitly define who owns what, reducing confusion and speeding up collaboration.
pull_quote: Too much time is wasted figuring out who you should collaborate with. Use the AORs framework to create extreme clarity on who owns what, so you know exactly who to Slack or invite to a kickoff.

episode_title: How to hire your first growth team
insight: Don't hire a growth team until after you've achieved Product-Market Fit and validated your distribution model with founder-led growth.
pull_quote: Many founders fall for growth hype and hire too early. Growth has nothing to grow pre-PMF; you'll only waste capital and equity. Validate product-market fit and your distribution model first.

episode_title: How to hire your first growth team
insight: Your first growth hire shouldn't be a leader; prioritize a 'builder' profile who can execute tactically on a specific bottleneck.
pull_quote: Don't hire a growth leader expecting a shortcut to growth. Even top leaders need 6-12 months to grasp your unique ecosystem. Instead, hire a 'builder' focused on execution for specific growth problems.

episode_title: How to hire your first growth team
insight: Growth teams connect more people to an existing product's value by optimizing distribution, not by building entirely new features.
pull_quote: Growth teams don't build net new products or features. Their mission is to create distribution strategies that help acquire, activate, engage, and monetize customers on the existing product value.

episode_title: How to hire your first growth team
insight: Growth team structure (centralized vs. decentralized) has trade-offs in velocity vs. culture and must evolve with business maturity.
pull_quote: Centralized growth teams excel at velocity but can centralize responsibility. Decentralized teams foster a growth culture but face priority conflicts. The optimal structure is not static and changes with your business.

episode_title: How to identify your ideal customer profile (ICP)
insight: Don't fear an "almost comically narrow" initial ICP; deeply serving a tiny segment before expanding is key to early traction.
pull_quote: Gusto started with six ultra-narrow ICP attributes: 5 employees or fewer, in California, no benefits, salaried only, no other deductions, paid 8 days after payroll. This depth-first approach enabled growth to 300,000 businesses.

episode_title: How to identify your ideal customer profile (ICP)
insight: Prioritize outbound sales signals over network leads when validating your ICP, as network connections often show fake interest.
pull_quote: Leads from investors or friends can create "fake interest" because people want to be nice or network. Outbound sales data is "much purer" for understanding genuine customer intent.

episode_title: How to identify your ideal customer profile (ICP)
insight: Beyond conversion rates, look for "the nod" – a visceral, enthusiastic agreement with your problem framing – as a strong ICP validation signal.
pull_quote: When Hex pitched their pain point, they looked for "a specific type of nod" – an immediate, knowing agreement from customers. That visceral reaction signaled genuine alignment and a strong ICP.

episode_title: How to identify your ideal customer profile (ICP)
insight: Identifying your ICP is an iterative, months-long process; embrace early mistakes and expect it to evolve as your product matures.
pull_quote: Persona did the ICP exercise 17 times in their early days, and for Notion, a horizontal tool, "every quarter, you feel like the persona that’s most important changes." It takes time and flexibility.

episode_title: How to identify your ideal customer profile (ICP)
insight: When struggling to define your ICP, pragmatically focus on customers who can buy *this month*, even if it means quoting high prices.
pull_quote: An early Amplitude sales coach advised: "Only focus on people who can buy this month. This month is the thing. If someone says the project is a few months out, don’t talk to them."

episode_title: How to increase virality
insight: Don't chase virality unless it's your product's primary growth engine; most companies scale from just one of four core engines.
pull_quote: You should only invest in virality if you are confident that virality will be your primary growth engine for your product. Most companies find the vast majority of their growth from just one of those four growth engines.

episode_title: How to increase virality
insight: Virality is a natural fit only if your product is better with friends, inherently fun to share, or truly remarkable.
pull_quote: Virality is a natural fit for your product if: Your product is better with friends or colleagues; The product is innately fun or rewarding to share; Your product is remarkable — something worth remarking about.

episode_title: How to increase virality
insight: Virality isn't just invites; recognize seven distinct types, from passive word-of-mouth to active experiential sharing.
pull_quote: When you think virality, you probably think about getting asked to invite your friends. But the concept of virality is broader. Any mechanic that gets your existing users to bring in new users is a form of virality.

episode_title: How to increase virality
insight: To increase virality, focus on three core levers: boosting sharing rate, improving conversion rate, and growing engaged users.
pull_quote: To increase your product’s virality, you have three levers at your disposal: Increasing sharing rate; Increasing conversion rate; Increasing engaged users.

episode_title: How to increase virality
insight: Drive organic word-of-mouth by crafting remarkable experiences, making users feel high-status, and simplifying sharing.
pull_quote: Word-of-mouth is primarily driven by remarkable experiences (an experience worth remarking about), so start there. How might you create a remarkable experience for your users?

episode_title: How to increase your product's retention
insight: Monitor your retention curve to see if it flattens; this is the strongest indicator of whether a group of users finds consistent value and signals Product-Market Fit.
pull_quote: Most importantly, whether your retention rate *flattens* — if it flattens, or stops decreasing over time, that means there is a group of users who continue to find value in your product. This is the best measure of product-market fit.

episode_title: How to increase your product's retention
insight: The biggest lever for dramatic retention improvement is optimizing the new user experience, not just adding features for existing, already-retained customers.
pull_quote: The standard advice of listening to long-term customers who are already retained, and adding features for them— that doesn’t work. The real levers to improve retention dramatically are in the experience for new users.”

episode_title: How to increase your product's retention
insight: Get new users to your product's core value as quickly as possible, guiding them with smart defaults rather than overwhelming them with choices.
pull_quote: “The first principle we learned at Pinterest is that we should get people to the core product as fast as possible — but not faster.” New users don’t want to make choices; use smart defaults to pull them through the first mile.

episode_title: How to increase your product's retention
insight: A surprisingly effective retention strategy can be shifting your business model to encourage annual plans, significantly reducing short-term churn.
pull_quote: “One portfolio company spent years trying to improve churn. The most important lever was shifting 70% of new cohorts to annual.” Sometimes, the simplest solution works best.

episode_title: How to increase your product's retention
insight: Offer users a 'pause' or 'snooze' option instead of direct cancellation; many just need a temporary break and will return later.
pull_quote: One of the more successful experiments we ran at Airbnb to reduce host churn was to give hosts a way to “pause” their listing, instead of removing it. This allows users to easily come back when ready.

episode_title: How to increase your retention
insight: Don't measure retention as a blended average; use cohort retention to see how many *new* users stick around long-term.
pull_quote: The easy (but less useful) way to measure retention... blends old and new users, and thus hide a key piece of information... Instead, I suggest looking at what’s called cohort retention.

episode_title: How to increase your retention
insight: A flattening retention curve is the best indicator of product-market fit, signaling a group of users consistently finds value.
pull_quote: Most importantly, whether your retention rate *flattens* — if it flattens... that means there is a group of users who continue to find value in your product. This is important because it happens to be the best measure of product-market fit.

episode_title: How to increase your retention
insight: To dramatically improve retention, focus on the new user experience, not just adding features for your existing, retained customers.
pull_quote: The standard advice of listening to long-term customers... and adding features for them— that doesn’t work. The real levers to improve retention dramatically are in the experience for new users.”

episode_title: How to increase your retention
insight: Get new users to the core value fast, but don't sacrifice the necessary steps that ensure they truly understand and experience that value.
pull_quote: The first principle we learned at Pinterest is that we should get people to the core product as fast as possible — but not faster.” This means balancing speed with ensuring users truly experience the value.

episode_title: How to interview product managers
insight: Many early-stage teams drastically underestimate the sheer volume of candidates needed to hire a single great PM, often needing 23 interviews for one offer.
pull_quote: Many early-stage teams underestimate how many candidates they need to speak to to make one hire. In our data we also saw that only 13% of inbound applicants even make it to the first screen. You will have to fill the funnel with sourced candidates and referrals.

episode_title: How to interview product managers
insight: The interview project is the most predictive component of PM hiring, revealing how candidates approach problems and communicate, not just if they find the 'right' answer.
pull_quote: Years later I can see that the performance on the project was closely related to their success at Slack over the course of years. The goal is to see how they break down the problem, structure their solution, and communicate it to you.

episode_title: How to interview product managers
insight: To effectively compare candidates and reduce bias, standardize your interview process by asking the same questions in the same order.
pull_quote: Companies should rely on a structured interview that standardizes the process among candidates, eliminating much subjectivity. These interviews pose the same set of questions in the same order to all candidates, allowing clearer comparisons between them.

episode_title: How to interview product managers
insight: While most prefer take-home projects, running the PM project during the interview day creates more equitable conditions, though it limits candidate prep time.
pull_quote: I have done it in their own time in the past but have since moved to doing it during the interview. Many reasons why, and still today kinda on the fence/grapple with it, but mainly I choose to do it as I believe it’s more equitable.

episode_title: How to interview product managers
insight: Top PM candidates perform more diligence than investors; treat the offer stage like fundraising to win them over with a compelling, personalized pitch.
pull_quote: We treat closing a candidate almost exactly the same as closing an investor. In fact, we’ve found the best candidates will do even more diligence than the average investor. Arguably, candidates are investing something even more valuable than capital: their time.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 2: Identify your super-specific who
insight: To kickstart a consumer product, don't target broadly; identify a "comically narrow" set of early adopters who deeply care about your specific value.
pull_quote: When it comes to the question of the target customer, the most common mistake is the definition is too broad. It must be almost comically narrow, to the point where you may be misunderstood for such a narrow focus.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 2: Identify your super-specific who
insight: Your ideal early adopter may not be who you initially assume; it's often discovered by observing who *actually* resonates deeply with your product.
pull_quote: Ben Silbermann assumed tech people for Pinterest, but 30-something female bloggers were the ideal early adopters. The product didn't change, the audience did, leading to over a million users in a year.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 2: Identify your super-specific who
insight: Discovering a market that genuinely loves your product is so powerful that it can compensate for almost any other operational misstep.
pull_quote: If you can find a market that really wants your product—if the dogs are eating the dog food—then you can screw up almost everything in the company and you will succeed.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 2: Identify your super-specific who
insight: To identify your core early users, focus on those who are "desperate" for your solution or whose lives are *instantly* transformed by it.
pull_quote: Don't just look for "cool"; aim for customers who "jump out of their chair and yell, ‘That’s *amazing* — I need that right now!’" These are your "high-expectation customers."

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 3: Craft your pitch
insight: In a crowded market, your product's pitch must be "remarkable"—worth talking about and noticing—not just "very good," to grab attention.
pull_quote: Competition is so fierce that you have to create something remarkable in order to succeed. Something remarkable is worth talking about. Worth noticing. Exceptional. New. Interesting. The opposite of remarkable is very good.”

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 3: Craft your pitch
insight: Finding the right product pitch is rarely a first-try success; expect to iterate, test, and tweak your hook based on user reaction, sometimes for months.
pull_quote: Netflix iterated on its product offering for 18 months and eventually found a hook that worked: the unlikely combination of 'no due dates, no late fees' and 'subscription.' Within days of testing it, they knew they had a winner.”

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 3: Craft your pitch
insight: To craft a compelling pitch, explore four strategies: highlight your unique differentiation, adopt users' language, identify the core job-to-be-done, or create a bold, attention-grabbing claim.
pull_quote: To find your differentiating value, compare your product to existing alternatives. Focus on what's truly unique, not just better. Netflix's 'no late fees' and Robinhood's '$0 commission' were groundbreaking differentiators that immediately grabbed attention.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 3: Craft your pitch
insight: If your product pitch isn't resonating, diagnose whether the problem is your pitch, your target audience, or if people simply don't want your product at all.
pull_quote: If your pitch isn't working, one of three things is happening: your pitch is bad, you're pitching the wrong people, or people don't actually want your product. Diagnose the root cause to adapt effectively.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 4: Find your early adopters by doing things that don’t scale
insight: Finding your first 1,000 users demands unscalable, manual effort like direct outreach, not relying on ads or viral content.
pull_quote: After reviewing 100+ consumer companies, the 7 reliable ways to get your first 1,000 users almost all involve manual, unscalable efforts. Think personally recruiting or going door-to-door.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 4: Find your early adopters by doing things that don’t scale
insight: Most startups get the vast majority of early growth from just one or two channels, so focus aggressively instead of trying everything.
pull_quote: Almost every startup finds the vast majority of their early growth from just one or two channels. Some see all their early growth come from a single channel. Don't order everything on the menu.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 4: Find your early adopters by doing things that don’t scale
insight: Align your early growth strategy with your product type: typical consumer, marketplace/platform, or a product with a remarkable story.
pull_quote: There are three common archetypes of early startup growth: typical consumer, marketplace/platform, or if you have a remarkable story to tell. Each has a distinct path to its first 1,000 users.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 4: Find your early adopters by doing things that don’t scale
insight: Start with the acquisition channels closest to you—friends, colleagues, then targeted strangers—before expanding outwards.
pull_quote: Think of growth strategies as concentric circles, with you at the center. The further out from the center, the harder the strategies generally are to succeed with. Start with your immediate network.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 4: Find your early adopters by doing things that don’t scale
insight: For marketplaces, manually acquiring supply via direct outreach (DMs, calls, door-to-door) is often the most critical early growth tactic.
pull_quote: Manually recruiting targeted strangers is key for marketplaces; one unit of supply is highly valuable, justifying intense manual cold outreach. Cameo, Airbnb, and DoorDash all started by directly reaching out.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 5: RETAIN: Iterate until enough people stick around
insight: True Product-Market Fit requires not just people wanting your product, but also a profitable business model and a sustainable growth engine.
pull_quote: For True Product-Market Fit, aka building a real business, your product needs to achieve three things: Product-market fit, Product-business model fit, Product-growth engine fit.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 5: RETAIN: Iterate until enough people stick around
insight: For durable consumer businesses, aim for 6-month retention of ~25-40% (good) or ~45-70% (great), depending on the app category.
pull_quote: Here’s what is considered good and great user retention at six months: Consumer social: ~25% good, ~45% great. Consumer subscription: ~40% good, ~70% great.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 5: RETAIN: Iterate until enough people stick around
insight: Before data confirms PMF, look for "visceral excitement" – users who desperately need your product or whose "pupils dilate" when they use it.
pull_quote: The real metric for both consumer apps and enterprise is—do someone’s pupils dilate when they use your stuff? If the customer doesn’t scream, you don’t have PMF.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 5: RETAIN: Iterate until enough people stick around
insight: Most successful consumer companies find PMF within 6-18 months of iterating, with fewer than 20% hitting it immediately.
pull_quote: Over 80% found PMF within 2 years. Less than 20% felt clear PMF immediately. If it wasn’t immediate, most took 6-18 months.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business—Step 5: RETAIN: Iterate until enough people stick around
insight: When struggling for PMF, your core problem is likely a wrong product, inefficient distribution, poor onboarding, or targeting the wrong audience.
pull_quote: When iterating toward product-market fit, there are four situations you’ll find yourself in: Wrong product, Right product-wrong distribution, Right product-wrong onboarding, Right product-wrong audience.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business
insight: Most successful consumer startup ideas emerge organically from founders' curiosity or observations, not from solving personal problems or direct user research.
pull_quote: Less than a third of consumer startup ideas emerged out of founders trying to solve their own problem. Only one company, DoorDash, came up with its idea by actively talking to potential customers/users.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business
insight: Many billion-dollar consumer businesses started with ideas that seemed trivial or simple at the outset, like sharing disappearing photos or renting air beds.
pull_quote: Just over half of the biggest consumer business ideas felt very trivial at the time, e.g. Twitter, Snap, Pinterest, Coinbase, Tinder, Calm, Airbnb.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business
insight: A good startup idea demands founder obsession, unique insight, and the immediate ability to prototype, as passion fuels persistence through inevitable challenges.
pull_quote: Startups only really fail when the founder gives up. Most successful consumer companies emerged out of a scrappy experiment that came together hours/days/weeks after the founders had the idea.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a consumer business
insight: Launching quickly and observing what unexpectedly gains traction allows you to pivot and double down on genuinely valuable product elements, even if your initial idea fails.
pull_quote: The best part about this strategy is that it should motivate you to launch whatever it is you’re currently most excited about—because even if the idea doesn’t work, it may lead you to the bigger idea.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 2: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Supply vs. Demand
insight: The vast majority of successful marketplaces prioritize supply growth early on, as it often naturally drives demand acquisition.
pull_quote: The lesson we took away from this was that all that matters is supply. So we decided to focus on that above all else. Creating scaled supply early on matters more than anything.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 2: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Supply vs. Demand
insight: Prioritize demand only when supply is inherently easy to acquire, such as when it offers flexible income or leverages public data.
pull_quote: For us, the scarce resource was demand. I don’t think we were ever supply constrained. Supply was easy. It was the dynamics of the market...

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 2: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Supply vs. Demand
insight: Mistaking your product for a marketplace can misalign your roadmap; correctly identifying your business model is critical for success.
pull_quote: A lot of people wrongly think of Patreon as a marketplace. Realizing we weren't a marketplace was the best thing that happened to us. It allowed us to take ‘Discovery’ off our roadmap.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 3: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Supply
insight: To kickstart marketplace supply, successful companies focused on just two key levers, not a broad array of tactics.
pull_quote: Lenny's research shows the median number of levers to kickstart supply growth for major marketplaces was just TWO. Don't spread yourself thin; figure out your core levers and double down for early success.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 3: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Supply
insight: Direct, one-on-one sales, often door-to-door, was a surprisingly crucial lever for 60% of successful marketplaces to acquire initial supply.
pull_quote: "One of the most significant learnings (and surprises) for me in doing this research was how important one-on-one direct sales was to most early marketplaces." Many started by pounding the pavement.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 3: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Supply
insight: Build a "single-player mode" by offering a tool useful to one side of the marketplace on its own, then layer in the demand.
pull_quote: Bootstrapping one side of a marketplace with a standalone tool (single-player mode) was often the most important tactic, like OpenTable's software for restaurants, before the demand side was even active.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 3: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Supply
insight: Performance marketing (SEM, Display, FB ads) was rarely effective for growing initial supply in successful marketplaces.
pull_quote: "I was also surprised to learn how rarely performance marketing was used to grow initial supply, being a major lever early-on only for Lyft and Uber." It's often not the answer for initial bootstrap.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 3: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Supply
insight: To ensure liquidity and quality, and deeply understand the problem, company employees often served as the initial supply.
pull_quote: For companies like Rover, TaskRabbit, and DoorDash, their own employees were the initial supply — creating liquidity and forcing deep understanding of the problem space from day one.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 4: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Demand (plus a Bonus!)
insight: Only shift focus to demand once supply grows easily, is underutilized, and you've proven real customer value.
pull_quote: Knowing when to invest in demand is crucial: many early marketplace players like OpenTable and Uber spent little initially, waiting for key signals like easy supply growth and underutilized inventory.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 4: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Demand (plus a Bonus!)
insight: Word-of-mouth and supply directly driving demand are often the most impactful early growth channels for marketplaces.
pull_quote: For over half of today’s biggest marketplaces, word-of-mouth was the most important early growth channel. The second most common lever was supply directly driving its own demand, surprising many.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 4: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Demand (plus a Bonus!)
insight: Early marketplace success often requires "doing things that don't scale," manually hustling to acquire and onboard initial users.
pull_quote: "Starting a marketplace business is HARD." Every founder agreed that manually doing whatever it takes, like Breather's founder personally onboarding thousands, is critical for cold-start growth.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 4: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Demand (plus a Bonus!)
insight: Demand-side growth leverages more channels than supply, and specific levers have vastly different impacts on each side.
pull_quote: Growth levers don't impact supply and demand equally. Direct sales is top for supply, but less so for demand. Expect to use a median of three distinct levers to drive early demand, compared to two for supply.

episode_title: How to Kickstart and Scale a Marketplace Business – Part 4: Cracking the Chicken-and-Egg Problem 🐣 - Growing Initial Demand (plus a Bonus!)
insight: For many marketplaces, paid marketing was not an effective or affordable early growth lever; focus on organic instead.
pull_quote: "Paid marketing was not a big lever for us." Many successful marketplaces like Thumbtack, TaskRabbit, and Instacart spent very little on digital ads early on, prioritizing organic or non-scalable efforts.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a marketplace business
insight: Forget growth hacks; sustainable marketplace growth always boils down to mastering selection, delivery quality, and pricing.
pull_quote: Everyone's always looking for the hack... But every time we looked for a reason we weren't growing, it always came back to the basics – selection, delivery quality, pricing. That's it. Always come back to first principles.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a marketplace business
insight: Don't over-rely on a single company's success story; what worked for one may not be effective for another due to unique factors.
pull_quote: I’ve become increasingly wary of teams relying too heavily on a single company’s experience. What’s effective once may not be again; focus on ways of thinking, not just specific tactics.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a marketplace business
insight: Counterintuitively, the fastest way for a marketplace to scale is by first going small, constraining scope geographically or by category.
pull_quote: The first major learning: almost every marketplace constrained their initial scope. To some this may seem counter-intuitive — why limit growth? It turns out the best way to get big is by first going small.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a marketplace business
insight: When constraining a new marketplace, choose geographical focus if supply/demand must meet physically; otherwise, constrain by category.
pull_quote: Marketplaces constrain in two ways: geographically (e.g., Uber in Seattle) if physical meeting is needed, or by category (e.g., Etsy with handmade items) if not. This distinction guides initial market focus.

episode_title: How to kickstart and scale a marketplace business
insight: Sometimes, eschewing conventional marketplace constraints (broad categories/geographies) is crucial if it boosts usage frequency and experimental velocity.
pull_quote: Thumbtack defied conventional wisdom, launching across all categories and geographies. This seemingly 'boiling the ocean' approach was key: it increased usage frequency and provided the experimental velocity needed to scale.

episode_title: How to know if you're doing a good job as a product manager
insight: Your #1 job as a PM is to deliver business impact. If your team isn't, you're failing, regardless of execution or popularity.
pull_quote: Even if you're executing like a boss and everyone adores you, if your team isn’t delivering meaningful business impact, you won’t get far. Your job as a PM is to provide leverage for impact, and if your team isn’t delivering, you are failing.

episode_title: How to know if you're doing a good job as a product manager
insight: More senior PMs must shape 'what' gets built—strategy, ideas, insights—not just coordinate the 'how'.
pull_quote: The company isn’t looking to you to simply coordinate and execute—especially as you become more senior. Contribute to *what* is built, not just *how* it’s built. Your company is expecting more from you than simply coordinating, note-taking, and communicating.

episode_title: How to know if you're doing a good job as a product manager
insight: Beyond results, stakeholder perception is key. Aim for 80% approval; if everyone loves you, you probably aren't pushing hard enough.
pull_quote: I remember shipping impactful features, but a few key leaders were unhappy. This hurt my review. Perception matters heavily. If 100% love you, you may not be pushing hard enough. Aim for 80% love, 5-10% uneasy, and (occasionally) a few people being like WTF.

episode_title: How To Know If You're Supply or Demand Constrained 🤹‍♂️ - Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Use data to identify the precise user experience threshold (e.g., 3-minute ETA) that unlocks instantaneous perception and drives retention.
pull_quote: Lyft found its "aha moment" at a 3-minute ETA. Below that, rides felt instantaneous, dramatically improving conversion and retention; above it, users shopped around.

episode_title: How To Know If You're Supply or Demand Constrained 🤹‍♂️ - Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: When demand-constrained, charge suppliers an application fee to reduce volume, improve quality, and manage onboarding costs, rather than acquiring supply indiscriminately.
pull_quote: TaskRabbit, despite being a marketplace, charged new suppliers an application fee. This counterintuitive move reduced application volume and increased quality, proving not all supply is good supply.

episode_title: How To Know If You're Supply or Demand Constrained 🤹‍♂️ - Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Set and achieve a specific daily transaction volume target in new markets; failure to hit this quickly causes both supply and demand to churn.
pull_quote: DoorDash found that failing to hit a specific daily delivery volume (e.g., 100) in new markets quickly causes both their drivers (dashers) and restaurants to churn.

episode_title: How To Know If You're Supply or Demand Constrained 🤹‍♂️ - Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: As marketplaces scale, evolve supply/demand metrics from simple heuristics like occupancy rate to sophisticated econometrics for precise resource allocation.
pull_quote: Airbnb's method for balancing supply and demand evolved from simple occupancy rates to advanced econometrics models. This shows how metric sophistication must grow with your marketplace.

episode_title: How To Know If You're Supply or Demand Constrained 🤹‍♂️ - Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Use a surge pricing percentage (e.g., <20-30% of trips) as a direct indicator of under-supply in a market, guiding resource allocation.
pull_quote: Uber balanced its marketplace by targeting less than 20-30% of trips to be surged in a market. Exceeding this threshold instantly signaled under-supply.

episode_title: How to know if you've got product-market fit
insight: Before building, real PMF signals are users' pupils dilating, literal love letters, or an urgent willingness to pay for a product that doesn't exist yet.
pull_quote: Steve Blank asks if someone's 'pupils dilate' using your stuff, or if they say, 'You’re not leaving.' Elad Gil got 'literal love letters.' Nothing beats getting people to pay for a product you don't even have yet.

episode_title: How to know if you've got product-market fit
insight: The most robust quantitative signal for post-product-market fit is a retention curve that flattens out, indicating a core group of users consistently finds value.
pull_quote: Plot active users over time for cohorts; if the retention curve flattens off, you've likely found PMF for some audience. Casey Winters states a deep initial drop-off is okay, but it must flatten somewhere.

episode_title: How to know if you've got product-market fit
insight: A simple survey asking users how they'd feel if your product disappeared can confirm PMF if over 40% say 'very disappointed.'
pull_quote: Survey users: 'How would you feel if you could no longer use the product?' If over 40% answer 'very disappointed,' you've achieved product-market fit, as popularized by Superhuman.

episode_title: How to know if you've got product-market fit
insight: A clear sign of powerful product-market fit is users continuing to use your product rapidly, even when it’s buggy, broken, or poorly managed.
pull_quote: Elad Gil says high retention with a broken product signals raw market adoption, like 'Twitter was constantly going down in the fail whale days, and no one moved off of Twitter.' That's PMF.

episode_title: How to know if you've got product-market fit
insight: Product-market fit is not a one-time milestone; it's a continuous state requiring constant adaptation as markets evolve and competitors emerge.
pull_quote: Ben Horowitz warns that PMF 'isn’t a one-time, discrete point in time that announces itself with trumpet fanfares.' Markets, competitors, and circumstances constantly shift, demanding ongoing focus.

episode_title: How to know when to stop
insight: Many high-achieving tech professionals quietly suffer significant stress, with half considering career changes or crying from work due to burnout.
pull_quote: Nearly 50% of responders had thought about changing their careers at least three times due to it “not being worth it.” And another 50% had cried because of work at least twice. It seems commonplace to push ourselves to a breaking point.

episode_title: How to know when to stop
insight: To sustain high performance, understand your unique 'range of tolerance' by identifying stress triggers, setting boundaries, and prioritizing restorative activities.
pull_quote: We’re all unique. Our biology, circumstances, experiences will influence our range of tolerance. Create a list of 'intolerables,' 'boundaries,' and a 'plan to flourish' to stay within your optimal zone of performance.

episode_title: How to know when to stop
insight: High-level roles dramatically increase 'mind hours'—mentally taxing time spent worrying about work—eroding recovery far more than 'office hours.'
pull_quote: What most people underestimate is the number of mind hours that are spent working, especially if your goal is to get to an executive level. You might work 50-60 office hours a week, but tack on an additional 10-30 hours of mind work.

episode_title: How to know when to stop
insight: Deliberately manage your cost of living low relative to earnings to gain financial freedom faster, allowing you to opt out of high-stress roles sooner.
pull_quote: I kept my annual cost of living low relative to my earnings. This prevented being bound to a $1M+ jumbo loan that would have required more years of work, allowing me to find much more peace and self-confidence.

episode_title: How to know when to stop
insight: Consciously choosing short periods of elevated stress (hormesis) can build resilience and new skills, but this must be deliberate and time-bound, not default.
pull_quote: All organisms have adaptive mechanisms when put under reasonable stress, which may improve tolerance or performance. Seek situations that can produce a 'hormetic career response' by consciously deciding when to push.

episode_title: How to learn the most about a candidate from a single interview question
insight: The rawest answers about product flops and why they failed reveal more about a candidate's self-awareness and learning ability than any success story.
pull_quote: I look for people being brutally honest about how bad it was and why it failed. The rest of the interview, they’re trying to tell you all the wonderful things they did. The rawer the answer in terms of how bad it was and why, the better.

episode_title: How to learn the most about a candidate from a single interview question
insight: Asking what everyone takes for granted but they think is 'hogwash' breaks the interview mindset, revealing genuine opinion and critical thinking.
pull_quote: Everyone always prepares for interviews, and then their entire conversation is predicting what you think you want me to say. There’s no way to answer that question without being genuinely opinionated. I'm testing if this person is authentic.

episode_title: How to learn the most about a candidate from a single interview question
insight: A truly impactful PM can define something specific and tangible that would literally 'not exist' without their direct initiative, demonstrating clear ownership.
pull_quote: The ability to actually define something specific and tangible in itself is a really, really, really good sign. I think a lot of people definitely get startled by that question sometimes, because it requires you to know what it is you put on the table.

episode_title: How to learn the most about a candidate from a single interview question
insight: Discovering what a candidate is 'running away from' in their current role reveals their true frustrations, limits, and ultimately, better cultural fit.
pull_quote: What’s interesting is what they’re running away from. Usually there’s something broken there that’s really wound them up. And you get quite good at getting a sense of what is their limit.

episode_title: How to learn the most about a candidate from a single interview question
insight: A simple hypothetical can act as a 'coded Eigenquestion test,' revealing a PM's ability to cut to the heart of complex problems quickly.
pull_quote: It’s a very simple question, and it’s a coded Eigenquestion test. Interestingly, all of a sudden the sharp product managers... very quickly find what are the one or two Eigenquestions on this topic. Two questions can get to the heart of the problem.

episode_title: How to make an impact in your first 90 days
insight: Simple, overlooked funnel tweaks can yield massive conversion gains; Linktree doubled signups by turning a logo into a 'Create a Linktree' CTA.
pull_quote: A small tweak like turning the Linktree logo at the bottom of a Linktree to a ‘Create a Linktree’ CTA has almost doubled signups. Overlooked opportunities are often hiding in plain sight.

episode_title: How to make an impact in your first 90 days
insight: Don't reserve paid trials only for new users; restart them for existing users to drive urgency and showcase latest value.
pull_quote: Trials shouldn’t be reserved just for new signups. Restart a paid trial for your *existing* user base to instill urgency and enhance the perceived value of paid plans, triggering upgrades.

episode_title: How to make an impact in your first 90 days
insight: Focus on growing the 20-30% of signups that drive revenue, even if it means reducing overall signup volume.
pull_quote: All signups are not created equal. Only 20% to 30% of signups actually matter. Hyperfocus on growing that cohort, even if overall signup volume goes down, to drive non-linear business growth.

episode_title: How to make an impact in your first 90 days
insight: Building trust quickly as a new PM comes from both receiving and bravely giving thoughtful feedback in the first week.
pull_quote: Building trust is critical in the first 30 days. The fastest way to build it is through feedback: ask for it, and be brave enough to give honest, thoughtful feedback early on to elevate relationships.

episode_title: How to make an impact in your first 90 days
insight: Prioritize understanding why 40% of *activated* users churn within seven days, as they offer the highest leverage for quick wins.
pull_quote: 50% of signups won't activate, and 40% of activated users drop within seven days. Focus on that 40% churn first; they made the effort, and you have more info to quickly experiment around.

episode_title: How to measure cohort retention
insight: Define "active" users by their main user action to cut through noise like logins or app opens, ensuring your retention data is clean and precise.
pull_quote: Most companies use logins or app opens to define "active users." But for the cleanest data, use the main user action – the core transaction you expect users to do. It ignores "noise" and gives a pure sample.

episode_title: How to measure cohort retention
insight: Never report a single "blended" retention for free and paid users; it significantly misrepresents activity and growth opportunities for each segment.
pull_quote: Often a mistake I see SaaS companies make is reporting one “blended” retention, with a mix of free and paid users. This can be misleading, because users who pay for the product are likely to use it way more than free users.

episode_title: How to measure cohort retention
insight: Select X-day retention if users engage regularly or KPIs are time-bound, but use unbounded retention if product usage is chaotic or you're tracking long-term behavior.
pull_quote: X-day and unbounded retention return completely different data. If your users use the product regularly, use X-day. If usage is chaotic, use unbounded. For SaaS, generally follow X-day.

episode_title: How to measure cohort retention
insight: Product analytics tools like Amplitude often lack crucial payment data for SaaS retention, necessitating SQL to pull together a complete picture from your database.
pull_quote: Product analytics tools often lack payment data for SaaS products (e.g., subscriptions, purchases, cancellations), making it hard to replicate retention there. For full accuracy, you'll need SQL sourcing from a database.

episode_title: How to measure cohort retention
insight: Retention is an output metric to monitor, not a good baseline for weekly reporting or A/B testing because it's a complex composite of many activities.
pull_quote: Retention (like revenue) is an *output* metric. You should monitor it but not strictly utilize it as a goal for testing or campaigning. Activity is only one component of retention.

episode_title: How to pass any first-round interview (even in a terrible talent market)
insight: Boost interview fit by mirroring job description keywords directly into your experience narratives, making interviewers subconsciously see you as a match.
pull_quote: Use *their* language when describing *your* experiences. Paste the job description, highlight keywords, then map your experience to them. In the interview, use their language to explain what you did.

episode_title: How to pass any first-round interview (even in a terrible talent market)
insight: Instead of memorizing answers to countless behavioral questions, master 3-5 recent, complex projects to provide high-resolution, compelling details.
pull_quote: Writing down answers to hundreds of behavioral questions is exhausting. Instead, pick three to five recent major projects and recall every detail. Your brain will more easily pull from these, providing high-resolution context and quantified results.

episode_title: How to pass any first-round interview (even in a terrible talent market)
insight: Elevate the STAR method by adding 'learnings' and 'future improvements' (STAR++) to demonstrate growth mindset and adaptability in your responses.
pull_quote: Behavioral question formats have evolved. To interview effectively, respond with STAR++, not just STAR. Add what you learned and how you incorporated those learnings in a future situation to show growth.

episode_title: How to pass any first-round interview (even in a terrible talent market)
insight: Win interviews by asking high-signal questions tailored to your desired role signals, showcasing deep research and making interviewers think.
pull_quote: Interviews are often won or lost by the questions *you* ask. Don't grab from a listicle; define the signals you need, do deep research, then formulate questions that get interviewers thinking and make you stand out.

episode_title: How to run SEO experiments
insight: Unlike user-level A/B tests, SEO experiments must consistently assign treatments to pages, not users, to avoid confusing search engine crawlers.
pull_quote: When you’re running an SEO experiment you’re testing on the page level. You need an experimentation system that can do your bucketing on the page level, not user level. Google needs a consistent experience.

episode_title: How to run SEO experiments
insight: Most startups should avoid building custom SEO experimentation frameworks; it’s an optimization tool for mature strategies, not a foundational build.
pull_quote: My recommendation to 95% of startups is that it’s not worth your time to create [an SEO experimentation framework]. Most companies are still in the 'build' phase of their strategy, not 'optimization.'

episode_title: How to run SEO experiments
insight: The only reliable metric for SEO experiment success is a direct increase in organic traffic; other common SEO metrics are often sampled or inaccurate.
pull_quote: Organic traffic. Nothing else. Keyword rankings, average ranking, SERP clicks, and conversions are unreliable for SEO experiment success. Google Analytics is sampled and not accurate enough.

episode_title: How to run SEO experiments
insight: Effective SEO experimentation requires at least 5,000 organic visitors daily to the page type, plus enough pages to support multiple concurrent tests.
pull_quote: You’ll want at least 5,000 organic visitors a day to the page type that you want to run the experiment on. The number of pages you have will also limit the number of experiments you could run at a time.

episode_title: How to run SEO experiments
insight: Simple on-page changes like optimizing title tags and increasing high-quality content consistently drive the largest organic traffic gains.
pull_quote: In aggregate, title tag experiments led to a 15-20% increase in traffic at Airbnb. Content always drives more traffic; every time we added more content to our landing pages, it always led to more incoming traffic.

episode_title: How to ship like a startup
insight: Ditch detailed PRDs; prototypes drive conviction and speed, while docs quickly become outdated and block valuable progress.
pull_quote: A PM’s job is to write detailed PRDs? Not in fast-moving environments. Conviction comes from prototyping, not paperwork. Ditch PRDs; they block valuable work and are rarely up-to-date.

episode_title: How to ship like a startup
insight: Build internal hype for new products by sharing raw, unpolished progress early and often, turning stakeholders into evangelists.
pull_quote: Sharing work in progress with colleagues leads to scrutiny? Not if you grow your product into an unstoppable wildfire. Share raw progress often; it builds trust and keeps excitement high.

episode_title: How to ship like a startup
insight: Recruit based on passion: the most successful products come from teammates begging to join, not those you have to convince or assign.
pull_quote: People work on the products they are assigned to? No. The best teammates are the ones begging their managers to work on your project. Find the believers, not the skeptics.

episode_title: How to ship like a startup
insight: Break down silos; extreme cross-functional collaboration, where everyone acts as a part-time PM, improves product decisions and ownership.
pull_quote: Research hands off to Design, Design to Eng, everyone in their lane? The best teams blur those boundaries. Extreme collaboration isn't just encouraged—it's the norm.

episode_title: How to ship like a startup
insight: To get leadership to fund your big ideas, earn their trust first by consistently executing and delivering on their existing priorities.
pull_quote: Want to sprint on your 'soufflé' idea? First, execute on leadership’s 'meat and potatoes.' Ship good things, get reps, and ensure leaders respect your communication to earn that trust.

episode_title: How to spot a top 1% startup early
insight: Top-tier startups pursue "ludicrous" ambitions that others dismiss, indicating they're ahead of the curve and capable of outsized impact.
pull_quote: Bob pointed out that “both Palantir and OpenAI were considered ludicrous when the companies were first started. Joining Palantir seemed very risky, but if it succeeded, it would be life-changing.”

episode_title: How to spot a top 1% startup early
insight: Great founders prioritize rapid learning and adaptation over a perfect initial strategy, pivoting from unworkable ideas to successful ones.
pull_quote: Bob pointed out that “both Palantir and OpenAI started with an unworkable initial strategy. It’s more important to be able to learn quickly than to have a good strategy.”

episode_title: How to spot a top 1% startup early
insight: Ignore your own judgment of an early product; gauge potential by observing intense customer love and passion, even in a niche market.
pull_quote: Cristina told us that “with Stripe, I never asked to even see metrics. I was instead observing what developers said about the product. I cared that people loved it enough to share it.”

episode_title: How to spot a top 1% startup early
insight: Seek 'Jurassic Park moments' – ugly early prototypes that offer a breathtaking glimpse of a future where the product could dominate.
pull_quote: Soleio had a fantastic metaphor: “early prototypes or demos that leave you breathless and feeling like you are being given a clear glimpse of the future. Even though it’s ugly and small, you can sense this product will grow up and one day eat things.”

episode_title: How to successfully launch on Product Hunt (when it’s right for your startup)
insight: Don't launch on Product Hunt unless you're ready to invest 50-120 hours; often paid ads are more efficient for early testing.
pull_quote: On average, startups that do well on PH invest 50 to 120 hours preparing for launch. With such a large investment of time and team hours, you need to ask yourself—are there better ways you could be using your time?

episode_title: How to successfully launch on Product Hunt (when it’s right for your startup)
insight: Product Hunt success isn't about hitting #1; true wins are measured by business KPIs like signups and trial conversions.
pull_quote: It’s great to aim for the top spot on Product Hunt, but what actually matters are your business KPIs: website visits, new signups, and trial conversions to paid. These are the metrics that will truly drive your growth.

episode_title: How to successfully launch on Product Hunt (when it’s right for your startup)
insight: Product Hunt votes from brand-new accounts created on launch day are likely to be removed by the algorithm, wasting effort.
pull_quote: If your supporters have not signed up to Product Hunt in advance and they create brand-new accounts on launch day to upvote, there is a high likelihood that these votes end up being removed by the Product Hunt algorithm.

episode_title: How to successfully launch on Product Hunt (when it’s right for your startup)
insight: Your Product Hunt launch day should align with your goals: choose weekdays for traffic or weekends/Mondays for better ranking odds.
pull_quote: Picking the right day of the week to launch according to your objectives is a crucial part of successful campaign planning. Mondays and Fridays have great engagement with less competition, while Tuesdays-Thursdays have highest traffic but fiercest competition.

episode_title: How to successfully launch on Product Hunt (when it’s right for your startup)
insight: Product Hunt's algorithm prioritizes upvote velocity, engagement, and upvoter diversity, not just the raw number of votes.
pull_quote: It’s a common misconception that the product with the most upvotes automatically gets the top spot. Product Hunt’s algorithm considers velocity, engagement, and diversity of upvoters.

episode_title: How to use ChatGPT in your PM work
insight: Don't fear AI displacement; learning to work alongside it is quickly becoming a non-negotiable skill for PMs.
pull_quote: Though I don’t believe PMs will be displaced by GPT-4/5/6/n, I do believe that, as with most knowledge work, learning to work alongside AI will quickly become table stakes. When was the last time there was a transformative new tool for product management? Never?

episode_title: How to use ChatGPT in your PM work
insight: Use AI to pressure-test your arguments, identify blind spots, and uncover counterarguments before stakeholder presentations.
pull_quote: AI can significantly strengthen your arguments. It helps you anticipate critical questions, identify gaps and hidden assumptions in your thinking, and highlight edge cases and counterarguments.

episode_title: How to use ChatGPT in your PM work
insight: AI can rapidly synthesize qualitative user feedback and quantitative usage data into actionable insights, saving PMs significant time.
pull_quote: PMs are already using ChatGPT to synthesize survey results, find feature ideas from app store reviews, and extract insights from raw usage metrics. It's becoming a massive time-saver for analysis.

episode_title: How to use ChatGPT in your PM work
insight: Leverage AI to inspire roadmap ideas, develop strategic frameworks, and propose initial product strategies, boosting creative output.
pull_quote: AI isn't just for tactical tasks. It can be a powerful creative partner, assisting with roadmap ideation, developing frameworks, and even proposing strategy. It helps PMs think bigger.

episode_title: How to use ChatGPT in your PM work
insight: Use AI to diplomatically craft 'no' responses to requests, preserving relationships while maintaining product focus.
pull_quote: Perhaps most importantly, AI can help you say 'no.' It crafts polite, well-reasoned rejections to stakeholders, helping PMs manage expectations and maintain focus on key priorities.

episode_title: How to use Perplexity in your PM work
insight: Over half of all PMs now use AI chatbots daily, a rapid and unprecedented shift in product management tooling.
pull_quote: Holy shit, over 50% of PMs who responded are now using a chatbot *daily*. And 85% are using one weekly 🤯 When was the last time a new tool went from non-existent to daily use for the majority of product managers?

episode_title: How to use Perplexity in your PM work
insight: PMs leverage AI to quickly analyze complex growth concepts and dissect historical examples, from growth accounting to detailed referral program evolutions.
pull_quote: Product managers are rapidly adopting AI to crack growth strategy. Prompts like 'Walk through the evolution of Dropbox’s referral program. Why did it work?' reveal how PMs get instant analysis of successful growth tactics.

episode_title: How to use Perplexity in your PM work
insight: AI tools enable PMs to rapidly benchmark performance, analyze competitor GTM strategies, and compare product tools, significantly accelerating research.
pull_quote: AI tools let PMs quickly find benchmarks and understand competitor GTM strategies. Imagine asking, 'What is good retention for a consumer product?' or 'Notion’s AI go-to-market strategy' and getting an instant, researched answer.

episode_title: How to use Perplexity in your PM work
insight: AI helps PMs upskill by quickly explaining complex technical jargon, summarizing best practices, and breaking down frameworks like Jobs to Be Done.
pull_quote: Product managers are using AI as a personal tutor, asking for 'List of brainstorming techniques' or 'How did Spotify use the Jobs to Be Done framework?' even 'What is Kubernetes?' to learn on demand.

episode_title: How to validate your B2B startup idea
insight: Rely on cold outbound sales to validate B2B ideas; warm leads from friends or batch-mates easily delude you into thinking you have product-market fit.
pull_quote: The best signal of this being *real* is to do cold outbound sales. It’s just much purer, basically: pure outbound with your pitch and value prop, with no other incentives attached. You cannot fool yourself and be deceived anymore.

episode_title: How to validate your B2B startup idea
insight: Real validation comes from users expressing extreme emotional pain or deep passion for your solution, not just polite 'that's cool' feedback.
pull_quote: What you’re looking for is really, really deep emotion. The moment you just asked them the simple question of 'What do you feel about your current payroll provider?' they started cursing, literally. When you hear that strong emotion, then you know you have something.

episode_title: How to validate your B2B startup idea
insight: Validate B2B ideas by manually solving the core problem for a few companies; it’s a low-investment way to confirm real pain and value before coding.
pull_quote: The do-it-manually path is a great choice for founders who are unclear whether the problem is important, or even solvable. Vanta manually created compliance reports; Ramp manually built savings reports. It unlocks huge lessons with very low up-front investment.

episode_title: How to validate your B2B startup idea
insight: Complex prosumer collaboration products often require 3-4 years of 'wandering in the dark' to find product-market fit, as seen with Notion and Figma.
pull_quote: Every prosumer collaboration product, including Figma, Notion, Coda, Airtable, Miro, and Slack, spent *three to four years* wandering in the dark until they stumbled on something that clicked. Expect multi-year iteration for ambitious products.

episode_title: How to validate your startup idea
insight: Don't ideate in a bubble; get out and test early models with real customers before writing any code.
pull_quote: Christina Cacioppo of Vanta warns, "Don't do this" when ideating in a bubble. Instead, she recommends getting out into the market, having conversations, and testing early models.

episode_title: How to validate your startup idea
insight: True validation isn't polite agreement; it's unsolicited inbound demand or enthusiastic "wait, you can do WHAT?" reactions.
pull_quote: Vanta's founder knew they were onto something when people proactively reached out to ask about her product. Pinwheel's founder got interrupted with "Wait, you can do WHAT?"

episode_title: How to validate your startup idea
insight: Use zero-code MVPs like spreadsheets, Figma prototypes, or even Photoshop screenshots to test ideas before writing a single line of code.
pull_quote: Vanta validated its core problem with a zero-code MVP (a spreadsheet). Cocoon signed its first customers off Figma prototypes, and Flexport used Photoshop screenshots for its 'fake' product.

episode_title: How to validate your startup idea
insight: Dig past functional needs to uncover users' emotional drivers; this secret insight can make your product 'must-have.'
pull_quote: Lenny's Cover app found Android users felt insecure and wanted to feel 'cool and edgy.' Tapping into this emotional need, not just functional utility, drove massive adoption.

episode_title: How to validate your startup idea
insight: Be willing to do unscalable, manual work like cold-calling hundreds or hand-delivering service to find initial product-market fit.
pull_quote: Good Dog cold-called hundreds of breeders for six months. Snackpass's team once washed blenders to keep up with orders. These gritty, unscalable tasks are essential for early learning.

episode_title: How to win in consumer subscription
insight: Early consumer subscription apps thrive by obsessing over efficiency, staying lean, and prioritizing profitability over rapid growth until product-market fit is undeniable.
pull_quote: Grammarly was bootstrapped, so it could go really deep on R&D without external pressure to launch too quickly. We remained self-funded for a while, which just built a culture and operating identity of being lean, efficient, and focusing on sustainable growth.

episode_title: How to win in consumer subscription
insight: To win in consumer apps, align product and acquisition strategies: build virality or testable paid funnels directly into the core experience for efficient growth.
pull_quote: Duolingo built a massive user base by offering all learning content for free, creating an organic growth engine where 'only payers can use your product and tell their friends' is explicitly avoided.

episode_title: How to win in consumer subscription
insight: Consumer subscription stickiness demands a 'magic trick': your product must be 10x better than free alternatives to compel users to form new habits.
pull_quote: Spotify's magic was 'the illusion of having downloaded all of Napster to your hard drive, instantly accessible, for free.' It had to be 10 times better than piracy to charge $120/year!

episode_title: How to win in consumer subscription
insight: Rapid, high-tempo experimentation is key for consumer apps; expect 90% failure, but fast learning drives conversion and retention wins.
pull_quote: Noom ran up to six experiments per week per PM because '90% of our ideas failed—we had to know quickly.' This rapid learning led to faster performance improvement.

episode_title: How to win in consumer subscription
insight: Consumers are price-sensitive; experiment with longer plans and web funnels for pricing flexibility and better revenue retention than monthly app store rates.
pull_quote: 'Consumers are typically accustomed to paying $10-20/month at most for digital apps (ironically, usually anchored to the price of Netflix), so a higher price can be jarring.'

episode_title: How today's fastest growing B2B businesses found their first ten customers
insight: Unlike consumer products, B2B companies consistently rely on just two or three core strategies to acquire their very first customers.
pull_quote: Only *three* sourcing strategies account for every B2B company’s very early growth (unlike the seven strategies for consumer apps). Thus, your choices are easy, yet limited.

episode_title: How today's fastest growing B2B businesses found their first ten customers
insight: For B2B, the primary early customer acquisition isn't an either/or between network and finding them where they are, but how much you get from each.
pull_quote: Almost every B2B business BOTH hits up their personal network AND heads to the places their potential customers were spending time. The question isn’t *which* of these two routes to pursue, but instead how far your own network will take you before you move on.
