episode_title: How today's fastest growing B2B businesses found their first ten customers
insight: A strong personal network offers a disproportionate advantage in B2B customer acquisition, especially when initial sales require building trust.
pull_quote: It’s a huge advantage to have a strong personal network in B2B. Tapping your personal network is even more important if you need to 'sell' your product, likely because early customers need a reason to trust you.

episode_title: How today's fastest growing B2B businesses found their first ten customers
insight: For B2B, press coverage is rarely the initial driver for acquiring your first ten customers; it generally provides traction later on.
pull_quote: Getting press is rarely the way to get started for B2B companies. While it can create buzz, the fastest-growing businesses relied on direct outreach for their very first customers.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B businesses turned their early users into paying customers – Issue 36
insight: Even purely bottom-up B2B products eventually require a sales team to convert and manage larger accounts.
pull_quote: 100% of companies ended up building a sales team, including every bottom-up driven business. This challenges the idea of a purely self-serve, no-sales model for B2B.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B businesses turned their early users into paying customers – Issue 36
insight: Founders must personally lead initial sales to truly understand customers and kickstart growth, even for products designed for self-service.
pull_quote: Founder-led sales is how most companies kick-started growth. CEOs like Okta's Todd McKinnon describe it as 'a real grind' getting early users to trust and pay.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B businesses turned their early users into paying customers – Issue 36
insight: You don't need a paid plan at launch; many successful B2B companies prioritized product-market fit, then defined pricing with power users.
pull_quote: You don’t need to launch with a paid plan if you are going bottom-up. Seven of the twenty-five companies had no paid plans at launch, instead figuring out pricing with power-users.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B businesses turned their early users into paying customers – Issue 36
insight: A B2B sales strategy isn't static; successful companies like Zoom pivoted from sales-driven to bottom-up, or vice-versa, as they scaled.
pull_quote: Your B2B sales strategy can evolve. Zoom started sales-driven then became bottom-up; Box did the reverse. Don't be afraid to adjust your approach.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B startups turned their early users into paying customers
insight: Even pure bottom-up B2B products require a dedicated sales team to scale and monetize effectively, despite initial self-service success.
pull_quote: 100% of companies ended up building a sales team, including every bottom-up driven business. Self-service alone is rarely enough for long-term growth and enterprise adoption.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B startups turned their early users into paying customers
insight: For early-stage B2B, founders must personally sell to kickstart growth and achieve product-market fit, regardless of the eventual sales model.
pull_quote: Most companies kick-started growth with founder-led sales. 'Frederic and I personally *sold* them. It was looking them in the eye and getting them to trust us. It was hard. A real grind,' says Okta CEO Todd McKinnon.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B startups turned their early users into paying customers
insight: Delaying a paid plan at launch allows bottom-up B2B companies to prioritize product-market fit and inform pricing directly from power users.
pull_quote: You don’t need to launch with a paid plan. Seven of the twenty-five companies (Segment, Figma, Airtable) had no paid plans when they launched. They worked with power-users to define paid tiers.

episode_title: How today's fastest-growing B2B startups turned their early users into paying customers
insight: B2B sales strategies are not fixed; companies like Zoom and Box successfully pivot between bottom-up and sales-driven approaches over time.
pull_quote: You can adjust your sales strategy down the road. Zoom and Amplitude started sales-driven and later became bottom-up. Box and New Relic did the reverse, proving initial strategy isn't permanent.

episode_title: How today’s top consumer brands measure marketing’s impact
insight: You can't just "pull a report" to see marketing impact; even top brands struggle to measure true incremental sales due to the counterfactual problem.
pull_quote: Marketers spending millions are surprised to learn measurement isn't a solved problem. Knowing if $1,000 in ads drives *additional* sales is the core challenge. They might have bought anyway.

episode_title: How today’s top consumer brands measure marketing’s impact
insight: Relying on one marketing measurement method is a mistake; top brands triangulate with MTA, MMM, and CLS to find the true incremental impact.
pull_quote: Single marketing measurement methods are like blind men and the elephant. Over 40% of top brands triangulate using multiple methods like Multi-Touch Attribution, Marketing Mix Modeling, and Conversion Lift Studies to find the true impact.

episode_title: How today’s top consumer brands measure marketing’s impact
insight: Last-touch attribution's known flaws biasing lower-funnel channels don't stop sophisticated companies like DoorDash from widely using it due to its simplicity.
pull_quote: Even sophisticated companies like DoorDash still use last-touch attribution. This is surprising, given it biases lower-funnel channels. Its simplicity and ease of measurement keep it pervasive.

episode_title: How today’s top consumer brands measure marketing’s impact
insight: Privacy changes like iOS 14 have forced a resurgence in Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM), as it relies on aggregate data, bypassing individual tracking limitations.
pull_quote: Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) has exploded in popularity post-iOS 14. Consumer privacy initiatives, like Apple's no-IDFA, have made MMM strategically important for future-proofing marketing measurement.

episode_title: How today’s top consumer brands measure marketing’s impact
insight: Conversion Lift Studies (CLS) are the gold standard for incremental ad value; even $100M ad fraud can be uncovered by measuring what *wouldn't* have happened without ads.
pull_quote: Uber uncovered $100M in ad fraud by running incrementality tests, realizing conversions would have happened anyway. CLS are the gold standard to prove ads' true incremental value.

episode_title: I built a Lenny chatbot using GPT-3. Here’s how to build your own.
insight: General-purpose LLMs often "hallucinate" or lack current/proprietary data, making them unreliable for specific, recent, or factual answers.
pull_quote: GPT-3 tends to 'hallucinate,' returning nonsensical or false completions. It also has a knowledge cutoff, meaning its information is frozen in 2021, missing recent or paywalled data.

episode_title: I built a Lenny chatbot using GPT-3. Here’s how to build your own.
insight: Overcome LLM hallucinations and knowledge gaps by dynamically 'stuffing' specific, relevant context into the prompt using embeddings.
pull_quote: If, in your prompt, you include the equivalent of a notecard with context, it will often get it right. Using embeddings to find relevant text chunks gives the model the information it needs.

episode_title: I built a Lenny chatbot using GPT-3. Here’s how to build your own.
insight: Chatbots transform evergreen content into interactive tools, offering new monetization avenues and instant access to creator expertise.
pull_quote: Every newsletter, book, or podcast can be repackaged as a chatbot. This new format offers instant answers for audiences and new monetization opportunities for creators, reducing repetitive questions.

episode_title: I built a Lenny chatbot using GPT-3. Here’s how to build your own.
insight: LLM-powered chatbots will replace traditional knowledge management, serving as automated research assistants and organizational librarians.
pull_quote: Fancy filing systems for notes are over. Chatbots will become personal research assistants, synthesizing information and acting as automated librarians for all company knowledge, eliminating repetitive internal questions.

episode_title: Ideal sprint length, designer vs. PM roles, running PM team meetings, running post-mortems, best product/executive coaches, and much more
insight: For small teams (2-3 people), ditching formal sprints for continuous, on-demand shipping reduces overhead and increases throughput.
pull_quote: I would go further and suggest that at this scale, perhaps you shouldn't worry about sprints at all. Just focus on continuously shipping features out when you feel like you have something. Teams don't realize that 2wk sprints aren't just 2wks of coding, there is a lot of planning and administrative work.

episode_title: Ideal sprint length, designer vs. PM roles, running PM team meetings, running post-mortems, best product/executive coaches, and much more
insight: PMs own the problem context and customer discovery; designers own the solution experience and product discovery, working together in "co-design."
pull_quote: I like to think of it as co-design. Product brings the context of the problem we're solving and why it matters... Design owns the experience of that solution. For me, Product owns customer discovery and I think it's powerful for Design to own product discovery.

episode_title: Ideal sprint length, designer vs. PM roles, running PM team meetings, running post-mortems, best product/executive coaches, and much more
insight: Build psychological safety in PM team meetings by creating "what feels hard" time to share struggles, identify blockers, and foster trust.
pull_quote: Our PM team has a section carved out for 'what feels hard [harder than it should be]?' The purpose of this time is to be vulnerable and let down the mask... it’s worth having this crosstalk with other PMs. Blockers are identified early and loudly... Stronger team self-worth... Increased esprit de corps.

episode_title: Ideal sprint length, designer vs. PM roles, running PM team meetings, running post-mortems, best product/executive coaches, and much more
insight: Transform leadership meetings from status updates to strategic discussions by moving progress reports to async docs, reserving meeting time for key decisions.
pull_quote: We took all the progress report stuff out. It's sent on slack every week in a document format... The time with leaders in a room is precious, so there'd be one person who's running the meeting who'd decide on 1-2 topics for us to discuss and unblock. Found it to be much more powerful.

episode_title: Ideal sprint length, designer vs. PM roles, running PM team meetings, running post-mortems, best product/executive coaches, and much more
insight: Cultivate blameless post-mortems by focusing on systemic failures, not individual mistakes, and circulating facts beforehand to ensure psychological safety.
pull_quote: The best sessions I’ve seen have given people ample time to think... The other principles I’ve seen important have been psychological safety and withholding blame, and creating an atmosphere where talking about a mistake or failure is not seen as political or personal... examine the root cause of failure in a *system* not failures in people.

episode_title: In defense of feature team product managers
insight: Feature teams, despite common criticism, are often the right choice for founders with a clear vision or companies needing rapid execution or feature parity with competitors.
pull_quote: Counter to the popular narrative, I believe that feature teams are extremely valuable to CEOs in certain scenarios. Many founders have a clear vision of what needs to be built to win, leading to environments that feel like feature factories.

episode_title: In defense of feature team product managers
insight: Feature team PMs do extensive product work—translating vague requests into concrete experiences, collaborating cross-functionally, and measuring post-launch adoption—not merely project management.
pull_quote: I’d like to counter the notion that the only real PMs are those on empowered product teams. I believe that PMs on feature teams are also real product managers. Marty Cagan heavily discounts the product work required for feature teams to be successful.

episode_title: In defense of feature team product managers
insight: Behaving like an empowered PM in a feature factory, by pushing back on requests or focusing purely on discovery, often leads to losing influence and being seen as unimpactful.
pull_quote: When feature team PMs try behaving like empowered PMs... the PM who “won’t play ball” gradually depletes their social capital... and is dinged for being neither collaborative nor impactful.

episode_title: In defense of feature team product managers
insight: To transform a feature factory, one product leader successfully did both feature-team execution and outcome-focused discovery work simultaneously for two quarters to prove the model's financial impact and gain stakeholder buy-in.
pull_quote: My team took a crazy third option. We did both at the same time. For two quarters we did two jobs and twice the work—feature factory and empowered product. It was nuts, but the model was awesome and the results convinced stakeholders to eventually buy in.

episode_title: Increasing team velocity
insight: Counterintuitively, doing fewer things allows teams to move dramatically faster and achieve better results.
pull_quote: The fewer things you focus on, the better those remaining things will go. I’ve seen this work miracles time, and time again.

episode_title: Increasing team velocity
insight: Ruthlessly protect your team's uninterrupted time; constant context-switching kills velocity far more than most realize.
pull_quote: “To produce at your peak level you need to work for extended periods with full concentration on a single task free from distraction.” Protecting deep work time dramatically boosts output.

episode_title: Increasing team velocity
insight: Involve engineers early in understanding the 'why' and customer pain points to boost their motivation, proactivity, and creativity.
pull_quote: The more your team knows about the why and what, and feels the customer’s pain points, the more motivated, pro-active, and creative they’ll be. This also reduces information loss.

episode_title: Increasing team velocity
insight: Give cross-functional teams full autonomy and all necessary resources to own outcomes; this dramatically increases shipping velocity.
pull_quote: Form cross-functional teams, with all of the resources necessary to ship, and empower them to drive outcomes. This shifts focus from output to impact and accelerates delivery.

episode_title: Increasing team velocity
insight: Removing underperformers, though difficult, can significantly unblock an entire team and increase overall velocity.
pull_quote: Sometimes the hardest thing is the right thing. Removing underperformers isn't just about cutting dead weight; it can significantly unblock an entire team and boost morale.

episode_title: Inspiration for the year ahead
insight: True product success isn't about achievement, but about relentlessly prioritizing and delivering measurable value to users and the business.
pull_quote: "Strive not to be a success, but rather to be of value." This motto guides me in life and for companies. It’s a powerful reminder to prioritize meaningful impact over superficial achievements.

episode_title: Inspiration for the year ahead
insight: Most people confuse speed with velocity; true progress comes from slow, deliberate planning and a clear direction, not just rushing.
pull_quote: Most people confuse speed with velocity; true velocity has both speed and a specific destination. To really go fast, you need to go slow first and be very clear on what you're doing and where.

episode_title: Inspiration for the year ahead
insight: Overwhelm is reduced and impact is maximized when you relentlessly filter out noise and only work on what truly matters most.
pull_quote: "Only work on what matters most." I have this on a post-it to remind myself to filter out unnecessary emails and distractions. It just reduces the temperature and keeps me focused.

episode_title: Inspiration for the year ahead
insight: Attempting to satisfy every user or market segment will dilute your product's appeal and ultimately satisfy no one effectively.
pull_quote: "If you try to serve everybody, you serve no one." This principle is about honing in on your product's persona. Trying to be everyone's friend, or build for everyone, rarely works out.

episode_title: Inspiration for the year ahead
insight: The quality of a product and its user experience are primarily defined by meticulous attention to small, often overlooked, details.
pull_quote: "The details are not the details. They make the design." You can’t be a product person without believing this. The devil is in the details, and ignoring nuance is at your peril.

episode_title: Introducing Core 4: The best way to measure and improve your product velocity
insight: Product velocity isn't just raw speed; misaligned teams moving fast in different directions actually slow down overall product delivery.
pull_quote: Product velocity is about speed *and* direction. When teams get too focused on 'moving fast' but don’t have alignment, they end up working on competing priorities and pulling in different directions.

episode_title: Introducing Core 4: The best way to measure and improve your product velocity
insight: Don't frame efficiency projects as 'tech debt'; instead, link them directly to business impact by quantifying savings in FTEs or direct financial ROI.
pull_quote: Instead of presenting efficiency projects as 'tech debt,' link them directly to core business impact. Improving dev experience by one point can save 13 minutes per week per developer, totaling 100 hours per week for 150 engineers – equivalent to two full-time developers.

episode_title: Introducing Core 4: The best way to measure and improve your product velocity
insight: Core 4 metrics (Speed, Quality, Impact, Effectiveness) must be used together as a balanced system to prevent optimizing one dimension at the expense of others.
pull_quote: Core 4 metrics are designed to hold each other in tension: don’t increase speed at the expense of dev experience, or spend more time on new features while quality nosedives. Use them together for a balanced view.

episode_title: Introducing Core 4: The best way to measure and improve your product velocity
insight: While controversial, PR throughput (PRs per engineer) can be a useful *system health* metric when balanced with other Core 4 dimensions, not for individual performance.
pull_quote: PR throughput, measured by pull requests per engineer, can be controversial. But companies like Meta and Uber use it successfully as a key system health input, never in isolation or for individual performance.

episode_title: Introducing DRICE: a modern prioritization framework
insight: You can't double engineering or design speed, but by picking projects even a little better, PMs can double their team's impact and leverage.
pull_quote: You can’t double the speed that engineers build or designers design. But by picking better projects even a little bit better, you can double your team’s impact.

episode_title: Introducing DRICE: a modern prioritization framework
insight: Investing 30 minutes in a 'Detailed RICE' (DRICE) estimate per idea is crucial; it uncovers hidden high-ROI projects and defeats 'promising' duds.
pull_quote: Many 'promising' projects turn out to be defeated by a half-hour investigation, while 'nice to have' features that would have been left on the cutting-room floor end up becoming high-ROI winners. At Dropbox, DRICE doubled our impact rate.

episode_title: Introducing DRICE: a modern prioritization framework
insight: Quantifying project ROI in hard dollars makes managing up easier; teams with direct financial answers get the resources they ask for.
pull_quote: Teams with the most trustworthy and direct answer to 'What is the ROI on this?' are likeliest to get resources. 'Give us X people and we’ll make you $Y dollars' simplifies commitments.

episode_title: Introducing DRICE: a modern prioritization framework
insight: A transparent prioritization framework combats the 'HIPPO' mindset, making engineers feel planning has rules and encouraging them to contribute better ideas.
pull_quote: The 'Highest-Paid Person’s Opinion' is the default mode for many teams. By running a transparent prioritization framework, engineers feel the planning game has rules and learn to think more like a growth PM.

episode_title: Introducing the Foundation Sprint: From the creators of the Design Sprint
insight: Many smart teams struggle to align on their core product strategy; a simple "Founding Hypothesis" sentence forces clarity on customer, problem, approach, and differentiation.
pull_quote: Smart, motivated people who respect their colleagues can still struggle to get on the same page. The obvious stuff is not always so obvious.

episode_title: Introducing the Foundation Sprint: From the creators of the Design Sprint
insight: Stop perfecting slide decks; quickly build a simple prototype of your core idea, as showing often succeeds where two years of pitching fails.
pull_quote: After wasting *two years* chasing a vague idea, we hit warp speed with a single week focused on the basics. Teams need a better way to start projects.

episode_title: Introducing the Foundation Sprint: From the creators of the Design Sprint
insight: Use a two-day Foundation Sprint to rapidly define your Founding Hypothesis, making big decisions fast through structured activities like Note-and-Vote and Magic Lenses.
pull_quote: The Foundation Sprint creates an artificial—but very effective—series of deadlines that force you to make big decisions fast.

episode_title: Introducing the Foundation Sprint: From the creators of the Design Sprint
insight: A Founding Hypothesis is a set of testable predictions; rigorously validate each part with Design Sprints and prototypes to avoid building the wrong product.
pull_quote: Every time I look at a Founding Hypothesis, I think, 'Prove it!' You shouldn't have to waste two years and wait until your project is about to fail to figure this out.

episode_title: Introducing the GAIN framework for feedback: an evidence-based approach to giving feedback that people love, appreciate, and act on
insight: Effective feedback focuses on what the recipient *gains* by changing, shifting from a problem-focused "pain" mindset to a growth-oriented "approach" mindset.
pull_quote: Doctors given data with an *avoid* framing made the most effective treatment decision only 50% of the time. But with an *approach* framing, they made the right decision 84% of the time—the meaning was identical, but the framing changes everything.

episode_title: Introducing the GAIN framework for feedback: an evidence-based approach to giving feedback that people love, appreciate, and act on
insight: Avoid all judgments—even flattering ones—in feedback; instead, clearly state observable actions and their concrete impacts to foster a growth mindset.
pull_quote: Critical judgments trigger defensiveness, but even flattering ones like "rockstar PM" can destroy growth mindset and autonomy by linking success to innate traits, not effort. This redirects focus from impact to approval.

episode_title: Introducing the GAIN framework for feedback: an evidence-based approach to giving feedback that people love, appreciate, and act on
insight: Acknowledging your own contribution to a problem disarms the feedback recipient, shifts the dynamic to "we're on the same team," and makes them more receptive to change.
pull_quote: When you contribute to a situation but don't note it, the recipient focuses on your gap. Preempt this by directly acknowledging your own actions; it's one of the most disarming feedback approaches.

episode_title: Introducing the GAIN framework for feedback: an evidence-based approach to giving feedback that people love, appreciate, and act on
insight: Increase buy-in and motivation for change by asking recipients for their ideas for next steps and making it genuinely easy for them to decline or modify your suggestions.
pull_quote: People will resist change they themselves want if it appears to come at the expense of their autonomy. A meta-analysis showed "Feel free to say no" *doubled* the percentage of time people said *yes* to a request.

episode_title: Introducing the GAIN framework for feedback: an evidence-based approach to giving feedback that people love, appreciate, and act on
insight: Use AI tools to prepare feedback, practice difficult conversations via voice mode, and conduct post-conversation retrospectives to continuously improve your interpersonal skills.
pull_quote: Many fear AI will erode human connection, but it's proving the opposite: people are using AI to expand interpersonal skills and deepen human relationships. You can practice tough conversations 24/7.

episode_title: Joining as the first product manager
insight: As the first PM, resist the urge to immediately change everything; instead, observe, learn, and build on existing successes to earn trust.
pull_quote: Don't upset the apple cart. Your instinct will be to quickly change things, but this is a mistake many new executives make. Move slower than you think. Focus on what is working and build upon those processes.

episode_title: Joining as the first product manager
insight: Your primary job as the first PM is to become the absolute deepest expert on the product, customers, and competition within 3 months.
pull_quote: Within 3 months, you should know about all the nooks and crannies of your product, and you should know it better than anyone else at the company. Talk to your top fans and understand why they use the product.

episode_title: Joining as the first product manager
insight: Your top priority as the first PM is to build the CEO's comfort and trust, allowing them to slowly relinquish control over their "product baby."
pull_quote: Your CEO will have a tough time turning over control of their product baby. Your #1 goal in your first 90 days is to ensure your CEO feels comfortable with you, so they increasingly hand the product reins to you.

episode_title: Joining as the first product manager
insight: As the first PM, go beyond execution and regularly challenge the fundamental product direction: "Is this really the product we should be building?"
pull_quote: One of the biggest mistakes I made was not pushing earlier to identify the need for Lyft's product. A great PM should regularly ask: 'Is this really the product we should be building?'

episode_title: Joining as the first product manager
insight: Since teammates at a startup likely haven't worked with a PM, you must repeatedly explain your role and how it benefits them, not hinders.
pull_quote: Many teammates likely haven't worked with a PM, and may question the need for the role. Regularly explain what a PM does and how it will be better, not worse, for your new teammates.

episode_title: Kickstarting and scaling a consumer business—Step 6: SCALE: Build your growth engine
insight: The majority of successful consumer startups initially grow by becoming world-class at *one* primary growth engine (virality, SEO, or paid), not by trying to master many.
pull_quote: The majority of startups grow primarily through just one of these engines. A common pitfall of early-stage startups is trying to invest in too many engines at once and not nail any. To win a market, you must become world-class at your primary growth engine.

episode_title: Kickstarting and scaling a consumer business—Step 6: SCALE: Build your growth engine
insight: Don't force a growth engine; your product must naturally fit its primary engine (virality, SEO, or paid) based on its intrinsic characteristics and user behavior.
pull_quote: Your product needs to be a fit with the growth engine, and it’ll only work out if it’s a natural fit. What you’re looking for is an unfair advantage.

episode_title: Kickstarting and scaling a consumer business—Step 6: SCALE: Build your growth engine
insight: Truly committing to a growth engine means dedicating significant cross-functional resources and being willing to influence the core product roadmap, not just an ad-hoc effort.
pull_quote: Most companies underestimate how large and disciplined the effort will need to be. Committing to a lane generally includes dedicating significant cross-functional resources and influencing the core product roadmap.

episode_title: Kickstarting and scaling a consumer business—Step 6: SCALE: Build your growth engine
insight: For marketplaces, direct sales is often the only effective way to acquire valuable supply, which then acts as a powerful "supply drives demand" growth engine.
pull_quote: Sales is rarely an effective growth engine for B2C startups—with the exception of marketplaces and platforms, where the supply is incredibly valuable. Restaurants did a lot of the marketing for us.

episode_title: Kickstarting and scaling a consumer business—Step 6: SCALE: Build your growth engine
insight: Optimizations like conversion, activation, and brand are crucial "lubricants" for growth, but retention is the most important as a poorly retained product won't scale.
pull_quote: Lubricants don’t drive growth directly but instead make every other part of your engine more efficient. Without enough lubrication, your engine will stop. Retention is by far the most important.

episode_title: Kickstarting supply in a labor marketplace
insight: To kickstart supply in a labor marketplace, secure initial demand first; high-quality shifts attract providers more than an empty platform.
pull_quote: We learned that to get the initial supply activated, we actually need to have some solid high-quality hospital shifts (demand) listed first to attract vet interest.

episode_title: Kickstarting supply in a labor marketplace
insight: Early labor marketplaces thrive by doing unscalable direct outreach, personally recruiting supply in-person or through existing networks.
pull_quote: We acquired most of our early Pros through direct outreach, often in person. We would visit San Francisco restaurants… waiting outside the back door to recruit cooks.

episode_title: Kickstarting supply in a labor marketplace
insight: Some successful labor marketplaces started by building a strong community first, then leveraged that engaged pipeline into a product.
pull_quote: For us, we started by building a community... We built the product for hiring from the community, which we’d been honing for 5 years.

episode_title: Kickstarting supply in a labor marketplace
insight: Create 'talent magnets' by offering cohort-based courses or upskilling programs; this attracts and pre-qualifies supply simultaneously.
pull_quote: We built cohort-based courses for transitioning service members... These programs are talent magnets and allow us to ensure we’re only featuring ready-to-interview candidates.

episode_title: Kickstarting supply in a labor marketplace
insight: Despite novel approaches, generic job boards like Indeed or Craigslist remain the single most popular and effective channel for early labor marketplace supply.
pull_quote: Our customers tell us they use job boards when looking for work, so we go where our customers go: job boards (like Indeed). In the early days, we got a major portion of supply this way.

episode_title: Leading a PM team meeting - Issue 19
insight: Never hold a PM team meeting just to "do it"; explicitly define its goal (Understanding, Alignment, or Connection) and verify it's the most efficient solution.
pull_quote: Don’t do this meeting just to do it. Make certain that adding (or continuing this meeting) is consistently worth everyone’s time. What problem are you trying to solve? The three typical goals: Understanding, Alignment, Connection.

episode_title: Leading a PM team meeting - Issue 19
insight: PM team meetings are best held 1-2 times a month; weekly becomes redundant with 1:1s, while less than monthly sacrifices most benefits.
pull_quote: I’d suggest PM team meetings one to two times a month. Weekly becomes redundant with your 1:1’s. Meeting less than monthly can be valuable for social/bonding, but most other benefits go away. Also, no laptops allowed.

episode_title: Leading a PM team meeting - Issue 19
insight: Empower team members to proactively add items to a shared, easily accessible meeting agenda, fostering transparency and relevance.
pull_quote: Put the agenda for each meeting into a shared Google doc. You want your team to be able to see what’s planned ahead of each meeting. If you’re a small team, let anyone directly add items to the agenda doc.

episode_title: Leading a PM team meeting - Issue 19
insight: PM team meetings often get too serious; actively inject joy and lightheartedness to strengthen team bonds and engagement.
pull_quote: PM team meetings tend to get super serious. Find ways to add a bit of joy and lightheartedness to the meetings. Have each person share something fun they did recently, invite guest speakers, or do a book club.

episode_title: Leading your company through a pandemic - Issue 20
insight: Before any action, assess if your business is in survival, tweak, or accelerate mode to implement tailored strategic and operational changes.
pull_quote: When you look at your business, you’ll fall into one of three buckets: You are seeing growth, You are falling apart, You are chugging along. Figure out which bucket you are in.

episode_title: Leading your company through a pandemic - Issue 20
insight: Plan for extreme downside and upside scenarios during uncertainty; preserve optionality by strategically cutting spend while retaining the core team.
pull_quote: Job #1 is to build and understand a worst case scenario. However, it is also about understanding the true upside case. Before you know which scenario you’re in, it’s about preserving optionality.

episode_title: Leading your company through a pandemic - Issue 20
insight: In a crisis, empower a dedicated incident response team with full authority to abandon existing OKRs, set new priorities tops-down, and move at extreme speed.
pull_quote: Assign one person as DRI. Give them full capacity to change anything that needs changing. Blow up your OKRs. Set new priorities, tops-down. The most important thing is that you move really fast. One day today is worth a month later.

episode_title: Leading your company through a pandemic - Issue 20
insight: Low ad CPMs during a downturn often mask higher actual CAC due to dramatically lower conversion rates; shift budget to prospecting with new creative.
pull_quote: Facebook CPM is down 45%, dropping 20% each week...However, on-site conversion is also down 53%. As a result overall, CAC is up actually up 28%. Shift spend towards prospecting, away from retention.

episode_title: Leading your company through a pandemic - Issue 20
insight: Capitalize on a downturn by 'filling the lakes' — remove friction and expand free product offerings to build habit and long-term monetization potential.
pull_quote: Lakes are your free active subscribers, your freemium users. If you have cash, now is the time to fill the lakes. Remove all of the friction you can to build habit with the product now, and that stored monetization potential will pay off.

episode_title: Lessons from 140+ angel investments
insight: You'll often be wrong about which product bets become massive winners; diversify your efforts across many credible ideas rather than trying to perfectly pick a few.
pull_quote: Only a third of my best investments were rated 'Great' at the time. If I'd only invested with 100% conviction, I'd have missed two-thirds of my biggest successes. Don't miss the big winners; place many bets.

episode_title: Lessons from 140+ angel investments
insight: To secure resources and drive high-impact projects, focus on becoming the indispensable partner stakeholders *want* to back, not just on having the best ideas.
pull_quote: Angel investing is more about access than picking. For PMs, it's about being someone stakeholders *want* on their 'cap table'—bringing useful knowledge, a strong reputation, or a track record of value.

episode_title: Lessons from 140+ angel investments
insight: While most product resources should back high-conviction, strategically aligned initiatives, reserve a portion for unique, high-potential bets with distinct team insight.
pull_quote: 80% of my high-signal investments are successes, but my single biggest winner was low-signal. As a PM, balance backing established initiatives with unique, high-conviction bets.

episode_title: Lessons from 140+ angel investments
insight: A rigorous product evaluation prioritizes identifying significant customer pain, a large market, clear product-market fit, and an A+ founding team with unique insights.
pull_quote: When evaluating any product idea, start with the core: How significant is the pain? Is the market big enough? Is there evidence of PMF? Is the team uniquely equipped?

episode_title: Lessons from going freemium: a decision that broke our business
insight: Users screaming for less friction or freemium can lead you to break your core business model if their immediate desires don't align with long-term value.
pull_quote: The customer can be wrong. Users may scream for freemium. It might even start OK (we 4x’d users right out of the gate). But initial usage is different from long-term usage. For us, engagement, retention, and revenue tanked.

episode_title: Lessons from going freemium: a decision that broke our business
insight: Counterintuitively, adding strategic friction like a credit card requirement or critical setup tasks can filter for committed users and boost long-term activation.
pull_quote: The prevailing sentiment is that friction in onboarding is bad. Not true. Friction can be critical to driving short-term adoption and long-term retention. Requiring a credit card up front seemed to have the biggest positive impact.

episode_title: Lessons from going freemium: a decision that broke our business
insight: Onboarding's true purpose isn't just to get users *into* the product, but to guide them directly to their first moment of *real value*, even if it means complex initial steps.
pull_quote: The goal of onboarding is not for people to complete onboarding. It’s not to just get people into the product. The goal of onboarding is for people to get their first moments of value from your product.

episode_title: Lessons from going freemium: a decision that broke our business
insight: Freemium thrives only with a massive user base, minimal setup, foundational product utility, low incremental costs, and strong built-in viral loops or network effects.
pull_quote: Freemium requires a specific set of things to come together. Do you have a massive potential user base? A really short time to value? Is your product foundational? Very low incremental cost? Do free users contribute to your growth model?

episode_title: Lessons from going freemium: a decision that broke our business
insight: Learn to filter user feedback; those who complain about necessary upfront investment or friction are often not your ideal, high-pain-point target customers.
pull_quote: Many users will kick and scream when you ask them to do the hard things to use your product. I can now confidently read a message like that and know that this person doesn’t feel enough of the pain Equals solves.

episode_title: Lessons learned from a startup that didn’t make it
insight: A truly effective ICP must be a single, specific market segment, otherwise "fraying" confuses all product decisions and hinders product-market fit.
pull_quote: If you allow your ICP to fray, you’ll lose. Encountering demand outside of your ICP may seem like a good thing, but be very careful about supporting those customers and expanding to a new segment.

episode_title: Lessons learned from a startup that didn’t make it
insight: Horizontal products only win when a single, specific audience faces so many diverse problems that one tool becomes better than multiple vertical solutions.
pull_quote: Horizontal products are only as good as their best vertical use case. They win if a specific audience encounters enough use cases that they want one tool to address them all.

episode_title: Lessons learned from a startup that didn’t make it
insight: Don't underestimate "true" switching costs; old, clunky incumbents are often resilient because politics, emotions, and existing processes lock users in.
pull_quote: A competitive product being old and clunky doesn’t mean it’s vulnerable. True switching costs include politics, emotions, career ambitions, and sheer laziness—making inferior products surprisingly sticky.

episode_title: Lessons learned from a startup that didn’t make it
insight: Confusing support (investor checks, relationship-based deals) for true market signal can lead founders to double down on flawed strategies.
pull_quote: Don’t confuse people rooting for you with market signal. Investor checks at seed stage are often a bet on the team, not validation of a strategy, and early deals from relationships are not true market signal.

episode_title: Lessons on building a viral consumer app: The story of Saturn
insight: For social apps, a robust single-player utility ensures retention, mitigating rapid decay if social engagement falters and buying time to build network effects.
pull_quote: Many apps achieve 'hypergrowth' with novel multiplayer social features, but without retentive single-player utility, most experience a destructive inverse K-factor. When a network starts shrinking, things spiral quickly.

episode_title: Lessons on building a viral consumer app: The story of Saturn
insight: Deeply personalizing the initial user experience, even with unscalable tactics, builds immense trust and drives rapid, magical early adoption.
pull_quote: We started by white-labeling an app for one high school, making it perfectly support their calendar. The night we launched, over half the student body joined in just three hours.

episode_title: Lessons on building a viral consumer app: The story of Saturn
insight: Embrace initially unscalable tactics to gain deep user insights and validate your core product, then productize those learnings for eventual scale.
pull_quote: Many investors told us our ambassador program was unscalable. While it added friction, the lessons and early growth were so valuable that we went deeper, scaling to a thousand schools.

episode_title: Lessons on building a viral consumer app: The story of Saturn
insight: Build products for problems you've personally experienced; this 'self-empathy' provides an unfair advantage in understanding user nuances and creating intuitive solutions.
pull_quote: High school schedules are unimaginably complicated. We didn’t start with a formal product roadmap; we had intuition informed by years of struggling to manage these byzantine calendars ourselves.

episode_title: Lessons on building a viral consumer app: The story of Saturn
insight: Getting a consumer product right takes patience and constant iteration; prioritize healthy, enduring long-term growth over chasing overnight virality for lasting success.
pull_quote: Many apps rocket to the top overnight but fail to establish themselves for the long term. We prioritized healthy, enduring growth at the community level, even if it meant skipping faster growth features.

episode_title: Li Jin launches Atelier Ventures, her debut fund to invest in the passion economy
insight: Don't dismiss 'small' markets; disruptive products target non-consumers, expanding the total addressable market beyond existing solutions.
pull_quote: TAM analysis can belie the actual size of markets. For disruptive innovations, the potential market among non-consumers usually exceeds what is already being consumed.

episode_title: Li Jin launches Atelier Ventures, her debut fund to invest in the passion economy
insight: True founder-market fit means having personally experienced the problem, granting an 'unfair advantage' in gaining early customer trust.
pull_quote: In the creator space, founder-market fit is critical. Founders who've faced challenges first-hand, or built tremendous customer empathy, have an unfair advantage in engendering trust and support for their product.

episode_title: Li Jin launches Atelier Ventures, her debut fund to invest in the passion economy
insight: To predict future professional opportunities, observe what children do for fun; today's play often becomes tomorrow's monetizable skill.
pull_quote: What kids do for fun today will become the new creator professions of the future. The activities giving children joy (even those adults dismiss) are indicators of where the future of work lies.

episode_title: Li Jin launches Atelier Ventures, her debut fund to invest in the passion economy
insight: The future will offer hybrid work models, bridging the gap between traditional employment and solo entrepreneurship with community and support.
pull_quote: Expect new models that bridge being an employee and a solo creator. These offer support, community, and downside protection while retaining ownership and upside — expanding who can participate.

episode_title: Magical growth loops
insight: Magical growth loops enable self-sustaining user acquisition where existing users directly recruit the majority of new users for you.
pull_quote: Magical growth loops are where most of your new users come directly from existing users. Your users grow your business for you; THEY recruit your new users. Magical!

episode_title: Magical growth loops
insight: Magical growth loops typically manifest in four distinct archetypes: supply-driving-demand, demand-driving-supply, demand-driving-demand, and supply-driving-supply.
pull_quote: I’ve compiled the most comprehensive set of examples of magical growth loops: Supply driving demand, Demand driving supply, Demand driving demand (e.g. virality), and Supply driving supply.

episode_title: Magical growth loops
insight: Companies leveraging magical growth loops can strategically focus resources on acquiring only one side of their market, letting users organically grow the other.
pull_quote: With this loop, Cameo can focus most of its efforts into recruiting celebrities, knowing that much of the other side of the marketplace (fans) will come along for free. Amazing!

episode_title: Magical growth loops
insight: Powerful growth can be passive; new users often convert as a byproduct of simply using or interacting with existing users and product features.
pull_quote: New demand discovers the product as a by-product of using it. Think Zoom: user sends meeting invite, co-worker signs up. Or PayPal: user sends money, friend signs up to accept it.

episode_title: Maintaining Quality 🏅 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Use search ranking algorithms as a cheap, powerful lever to promote high-quality supply and control marketplace dynamics.
pull_quote: Search results were the lever that we used to control everything in the business. That *was* the product. We knew where to seed supply in the search ranking, based on data science.

episode_title: Maintaining Quality 🏅 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Identify counterintuitive "early signals" from new supply that predict long-term quality, then use them to boost performance.
pull_quote: The question most predictive of being a good sitter was ‘Where the dog slept at night?’ If they answered ‘In my bed’, we’d boost them in search rankings.

episode_title: Maintaining Quality 🏅 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Extensive manual onboarding and "unsexy operational work" are critical to set initial quality standards and gather direct market feedback.
pull_quote: We do a lot of unsexy operational work with restaurants to help them succeed. We even went to a store and bought one of every item just to create a high-quality catalog.

episode_title: Maintaining Quality 🏅 Phase 2 of Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Quality is an ongoing battle, never "solved," and only gets harder with scale, requiring dedicated teams and constant adaptation.
pull_quote: You never *solve* quality. It’s an ongoing battle, with unforeseen bumps and shifting expectations. And it only gets harder as you scale.

episode_title: Make product management fun again with AI agents
insight: AI agents tackle the "draining-yet-essential" tasks that burden PMs, freeing them for high-impact work like customer immersion and strategic thinking.
pull_quote: What’s maddening about product management is that everything is our job. AI agents are becoming talented enough to take on our least favorite, least impactful—but still necessary—PM tasks, making our jobs more fun.

episode_title: Make product management fun again with AI agents
insight: When adopting AI agents, treat them like a new product: start small, cut scope, and delegate only the single worst, most dreaded part of a task first.
pull_quote: It’s tempting to ask for all our wishes at once from AI agents. Instead, approach your agent like a new product: start small, cut scope, and delegate only the single worst, most dreaded part of a task first.

episode_title: Make product management fun again with AI agents
insight: Using AI to summarize all customer data degrades your intuition; instead, use agents to navigate and cluster data, but insist on raw quotes and direct links.
pull_quote: If I use AI to summarize everything, I’m gonna quickly degrade my customer intuition. AI isn’t endangering your job, but *letting AI read for you* is. Insist on exact quotes and direct links.

episode_title: Make product management fun again with AI agents
insight: Assume AI agents will make mistakes; design workflows with low downside by having them DM, draft, or suggest, rather than taking irreversible actions.
pull_quote: Anything that can go wrong, will go wrong with AI. Don't predict hallucination; design your agent to give all the upside but cap the downside, like sending a DM or creating a draft instead of taking final action.

episode_title: Make product management fun again with AI agents
insight: Overcome the AI agent learning curve by using a well-crafted LLM prompt to generate step-by-step build instructions for your chosen platform.
pull_quote: The elephant in the room around AI agents is the learning curve. You can enjoy building in plain English and leverage pre-built integrations by using a specific AI agent builder prompt to create step-by-step instructions.

episode_title: Managing up
insight: Over-communication is nearly impossible when managing up; send a weekly 'State of The Me' email with priorities, concerns, and blockers.
pull_quote: It’s nearly impossible to over-communicate with your manager. Send a simple weekly 'State of The Me' email with your priorities, things on your mind, and blockers. It’ll work wonders.

episode_title: Managing up
insight: Your performance isn't just about results, but how they compare to your manager's expectations; set realistic goals to avoid disappointment.
pull_quote: Your performance will be judged not just on results, but how they compare to your manager’s expectations. It feels good to say yes now, but you’ll pay for it later.

episode_title: Managing up
insight: Don't wait to be asked; anticipate looming deliverables, problems, or opportunities, as looking around corners has high ROI.
pull_quote: Don’t wait for your manager to come to you. Anticipate looming asks, deliverables, and problems—spending a bit of time looking around corners is a high ROI investment.

episode_title: Managing up
insight: When new work appears, re-prioritize your entire workload and explicitly align with your manager on the new order of operations.
pull_quote: When asked to take on new work, particularly last-minute, *share your updated priorities with your manager*. Don’t assume they know what else you’ve got on your plate.

episode_title: Marketplace city expansion strategy
insight: Most marketplaces expand within 6-8 months; in highly competitive markets, prioritize rapid, aggressive expansion to capture first-mover advantage.
pull_quote: Expand too early and risk both markets failing. Expand too late and give competition a chance. In highly competitive environments, it may make sense to be more aggressive and launch as quickly as humanly possible.

episode_title: Marketplace city expansion strategy
insight: Build a market expansion spreadsheet using 3-5 unique, tailored attributes beyond population, like local culture or specific demographics, to stack-rank cities.
pull_quote: Don't just pick big cities. Create a spreadsheet with 3 to 5 attributes that tell you whether a city will be a good fit, then stack-rank them. Instacart looked at waitlists, Snackpass at market archetypes.

episode_title: Marketplace city expansion strategy
insight: Start city launches with tiny, sales-driven 'paratrooper' teams to secure initial supply, then evolve to a centralized demand team and inside sales for scale.
pull_quote: Early launch teams are very small, primarily salespeople. They land, hire local or set up shop for a month, get initial supply, then move on. Founders are often deeply involved at first.

episode_title: Marketplace city expansion strategy
insight: Focus the early launch playbook intensely on acquiring 'whale' supply and ensuring operational consistency, as demand follows liquidity.
pull_quote: 'Supply is king.' Prioritize getting the market seeded with 'market maker' supply that exemplifies your unique offering. You have only a few months to prove demand before suppliers churn.

episode_title: Meet your Lenny’s Newsletter Fellows ✨
insight: High-potential product leaders are senior enough to have built world-class products, yet still have significant career runway for future impact.
pull_quote: When selecting fellows, I looked for people senior enough to have built world-class, impactful products, but not so senior that the fellowship wouldn’t meaningfully impact their career. This defines a sweet spot for high-potential product leaders.

episode_title: Meet your Lenny’s Newsletter Fellows ✨
insight: Impactful product leadership doesn't follow a single path; it blends deep product craft with diverse skills like coaching, entrepreneurship, or growth.
pull_quote: The chosen fellows show how impactful PM careers diverge: from early Stripe and startup founding, to executive coaching for teams, and driving 10x growth at Slack. There's no single path to product leadership.

episode_title: Meet your Lenny’s Newsletter Fellows ✨
insight: To advance in product leadership, demonstrate tangible impact by building world-class products or contributing to significant growth, not just managing projects.
pull_quote: My fellows all share one thing: they've built world-class, successful, and impactful products firsthand. This tangible impact, like contributing to 10x growth, is what truly defines a top-tier product leader.

episode_title: Mission → Vision → Strategy → Goals → Roadmap → Task
insight: Effective product teams structure their work from a high-level Mission down to daily Tasks, ensuring every action aligns with the ultimate purpose.
pull_quote: Every product decision, from a single task to a company's mission, forms a direct chain: Mission → Vision → Strategy → Goals → Roadmap → Task. This structured approach ensures every effort is purposeful.

episode_title: Mission → Vision → Strategy → Goals → Roadmap → Task
insight: A robust product strategy is a concise plan to achieve your vision, articulated through 3-5 concrete investments that drive winning.
pull_quote: Your strategy is simply your plan to win, a short description with 3 to 5 concrete investments that, if executed right, will bring you closer to achieving your vision.

episode_title: Mission → Vision → Strategy → Goals → Roadmap → Task
insight: Prioritize roadmap items by evaluating their ROI, where 'impact' is directly measured by how much a project contributes to your established goals.
pull_quote: When prioritizing roadmap ideas, you look at ROI—effort vs. impact. The *impact* is based on how much you expect the project will affect your *goals*. Don't prioritize tasks without goals.

episode_title: Mission → Vision → Strategy → Goals → Roadmap → Task
insight: While typically starting with mission, it's perfectly fine to begin with a compelling vision and then refine your mission around that inspiring future.
pull_quote: If you start with your vision before your mission, that’s totally OK. Elon Musk likely started with a vision of the world he wanted to see and then developed his mission around that. No problem. Don’t overthink it.

episode_title: Moving from IC product manager to manager of product managers
insight: As a PM manager, your new level of influence means actively pushing back on bad ideas and "stupid sh*t" across the company.
pull_quote: You are now in a higher echelon of influence and visibility. Use this newfound power! When you see "stupid sh*t" happening, push back and ask direct questions; your opinion now has surprising impact.

episode_title: Moving from IC product manager to manager of product managers
insight: A PM manager's core job is to unblock teams at all levels (short, medium, long term) using influence, not doing IC work.
pull_quote: How do you help your teams become more effective? Not by doing IC PM work. Instead, it’s by leveraging your newfound influence to unblock your teams at every level. Anything that slows your team down or leads to wasted work is now your fault.

episode_title: Moving from IC product manager to manager of product managers
insight: A PM manager must courageously maintain and improve the PM team's quality bar, resisting the urge to let "OK" work slide.
pull_quote: The default path in performance reviews is to make everyone feel good by letting OK work slide. The courageous path is to push everyone around you to keep that bar as high as possible. Rage against the dying of the light.

episode_title: Moving from IC product manager to manager of product managers
insight: As a PM manager, your primary "team" shifts from IC peers to fellow managers; intensely build cohesion with this new leadership group.
pull_quote: As an IC PM, your team was your cross-functional IC peers. As a manager, your team is now your peer managers. It’s essential that you work as one unit because your team will be looking to you for clear direction and confidence.

episode_title: Moving from IC product manager to manager of product managers
insight: Don't assume your manager knows you want to be a manager; explicitly ask for the promotion and express your aspirations regularly.
pull_quote: Finally, and often forgotten: make sure your manager knows you want to get into management. Do not assume this is assumed. When roles open up, leaders need to feel confident you’ll succeed AND know that you want it.

episode_title: My favorite decision-making frameworks
insight: If your team frequently demands decision frameworks, it might signal a lack of trust in your judgment; address that underlying issue first.
pull_quote: If your team asks for a decision framework on every issue, it could mean they don’t super-trust your judgment. You’re better off building influence and trust long-term than always relying on a framework.

episode_title: My favorite decision-making frameworks
insight: Reserve decision frameworks for "one-way-door" decisions with real consequences; don't waste time applying them to low-risk choices.
pull_quote: A decision-making framework isn’t meant for every situation. It’s for hard decisions that would have real consequences, not for choosing kombucha flavors.

episode_title: My favorite decision-making frameworks
insight: True consensus often means no ownership; prioritize listening to all voices, then empower one person to decide and rally the team.
pull_quote: Consensus is impractical and ineffective because it means no ownership. What is important isn’t that everyone agrees, it’s that everyone is listened to, then the right person makes the decision and rallies everyone around it.

episode_title: My favorite decision-making frameworks
insight: Humans are social, not truth-seeking, so high-functioning organizations must build mechanisms and culture to support uncomfortable truths.
pull_quote: Jeff Bezos reminds us: humans are social animals, not truth-seeking animals. Important truths can be uncomfortable and make people defensive. Any high-functioning organization needs mechanisms and a culture that supports truth-telling.

episode_title: My favorite decision-making frameworks
insight: Use structured frameworks like Dory/Pulse in meetings to equalize voices, remove groupthink, and supercharge real-time decisions.
pull_quote: I've used Dory/Pulse over a thousand times. It’s a simple yet impactful ritual for supercharging decisions by equalizing voices and removing groupthink in meetings.

episode_title: My favorite PM interview questions
insight: Great PMs own their product failures, deeply understand their root causes, and demonstrably learn lessons that change their future work.
pull_quote: Share a time you shipped a product that failed. Why did it fail and what did you learn? Look for ownership of the failure, identifying its source, and taking away a lesson that informs how they build product today.

episode_title: My favorite PM interview questions
insight: True PM collaboration isn't just about convincing others, but genuinely listening, understanding diverse perspectives, and even being persuaded to change your own mind.
pull_quote: Tell me about a time you disagreed with an engineer. What to look for: Being able to convince with information while also preserving relationships, genuinely hearing out the other person’s point of view, and ideally, being convinced otherwise.

episode_title: My favorite PM interview questions
insight: A strong PM strategy isn't just a list of features or impacts; it's a clear, cohesive narrative fueled by deep insights that excites and inspires.
pull_quote: Pick a product you worked on—talk me through your product strategy. Look for: Excitement, a clear, succinct, and cohesive articulation, and deep insights into the problem, not just a feature list or vague goals.

episode_title: My favorite PM interview questions
insight: A senior PM demonstrates leadership by proactively addressing team dysfunction and investing energy in improving team dynamics, not just focusing on product tasks.
pull_quote: Tell me about a time when your team didn’t gel. What to look for: Taking it upon themselves to solve this problem—a history of spending time and energy on improving team dynamics—and being able to articulate and resolve the root cause.

episode_title: My favorite PM interview questions
insight: A PM's ability to articulate *how* they define and measure product impact is as crucial as the impact itself, revealing their strategic and analytical depth.
pull_quote: What’s the most important product you shipped? What made it so impactful? Look for: How they actually measure impact/importance, and if their definition aligns with yours, beyond just the scale of the launch.

episode_title: My favorite product management templates
insight: Explicitly defining what your project *will not* do (non-goals) is as crucial for focus and alignment as defining what it will achieve.
pull_quote: Kevin Yien's PRD template highlights the power of 'Non-Goals.' It's not just about what you build, but what you consciously choose *not* to build that drives focus and avoids scope creep.

episode_title: My favorite product management templates
insight: Great product work starts not with solutions, but with framing the problem correctly, as 'Eigenquestions' suggests it's the hardest part.
pull_quote: The 'Eigenquestions' framework teaches that the art of framing the right problem is often more critical and challenging than solving it. Don't jump to solutions before truly understanding the core issue.

episode_title: My favorite product management templates
insight: Start product development by imagining the customer experience and writing a press release *first* to ensure clarity and customer focus.
pull_quote: Amazon's 'working backwards' method is powerful: craft a customer-facing press release for your product *before* building it. This forces you to articulate clear customer benefits and challenges assumptions upfront.

episode_title: My favorite product management templates
insight: Begin every project with a concise one-pager, summarizing the core idea, problem, and goals, before diving into lengthy documentation.
pull_quote: Many top PMs advocate starting every project with a simple 1-pager. It's a powerful way to summarize the core idea and align stakeholders quickly, preventing detailed work on an unvalidated concept.

episode_title: My favorite product management templates
insight: Define clear success criteria and identify major risks upfront in any project plan to guide decisions and anticipate challenges effectively.
pull_quote: Steve Morin's 1-pager template emphasizes two critical elements: clear success criteria and identified risks. Don't start a project without defining what 'winning' looks like and what could derail you.

episode_title: My podcast tech stack, workflows, and lessons
insight: Aim to build a product 100 people *love*, not one a million people merely *like*; intense early devotion sparks word-of-mouth growth.
pull_quote: I forget who gave me this advice, but it was so important early on—and still is now: Instead of trying to make something a lot of people like, create something that 100 people *love*.

episode_title: My podcast tech stack, workflows, and lessons
insight: Launch with B+ quality, then systematically improve; perfection on day one is less critical than continuous, responsive evolution.
pull_quote: You don’t need to *start* at A+. My advice is to aim for B+ to start, then work up from there.

episode_title: My podcast tech stack, workflows, and lessons
insight: During user interviews, actively probe "dropped facts"—offhand remarks that reveal deeper, often unarticulated, needs or interests.
pull_quote: Guests sometimes mention something offhand that sounds interesting but then quickly move on for one reason or another... Come back to this “dropped fact” and see if there’s more *there* there.

episode_title: My podcast tech stack, workflows, and lessons
insight: Build a wishlist of ideal users and proactively pursue them, leveraging unfair advantages and persistent outreach, rather than only relying on inbound.
pull_quote: You build the best products by working backward from the ideal experience... with podcast guests, I work backward from a wishlist of dream guests, and slowly but surely find a connection to each of these people.

episode_title: My podcast tech stack, workflows, and lessons
insight: Don't rely solely on existing channels; proactively seek new platforms like YouTube for organic discovery via powerful algorithms.
pull_quote: There’s no better way for new people to discover your podcast than through YouTube’s algorithms. Over half of my views are from YouTube sending new people my way.

episode_title: Navigating your early career
insight: Chasing resume "checkboxes" like fancy titles or unicorn startups never truly ends and often delays pursuing what you genuinely want.
pull_quote: The pressure to keep building your resume *never* ends. You can often do what you *actually* want to do much sooner, without that perfect resume. Don’t lose sight of what you’re ultimately after.

episode_title: Navigating your early career
insight: True career passion emerges at the intersection of what you enjoy, what you're skilled at, and crucially, what creates tangible value for others.
pull_quote: What people often miss when following their passions/interests is creating value for others. Without that, it’s just a hobby or a dead-end. Strive to do more of the things that give you energy.

episode_title: Navigating your early career
insight: Getting too comfortable in a role for an extended period can hinder personal growth; pivotal career shifts often occur when you proactively seek change.
pull_quote: If you find yourself feeling too comfortable (and you have the means), make a change. Switch teams, switch roles, switch companies. Life is short.

episode_title: Navigating your early career
insight: Your coworkers and company culture fundamentally shape your skill development, define your performance standards, and build your professional network.
pull_quote: Your co-workers will significantly influence the trajectory of your career, particularly: Which skills you build, how high you set your bar, the quality of the network you build.

episode_title: Navigating your early career
insight: Actively pursuing new knowledge, even seemingly unrelated interests, creates unexpected opportunities and valuable career stepping stones.
pull_quote: Good things happen when you follow your interests and learn for learning’s sake. This experience taught me that good things happen when you follow your interests and learn for learning’s sake.

episode_title: New data on the product job market
insight: Growth roles are currently the fastest-growing in tech, signaling a market shift towards product-led growth and capital efficiency.
pull_quote: Unexpectedly, growth roles are expanding faster than any other product-adjacent function right now. Companies are leaning into growth, potentially driven by a need for capital efficiency.

episode_title: New data on the product job market
insight: The PM role has plateaued after years of hypergrowth, maintaining a healthy steady state rather than shrinking despite market anxieties.
pull_quote: The PM role isn't shrinking, contrary to some fears. After an acceleration through 2022, PM hiring and firing have reached a healthy steady state over the past 1.5 years.

episode_title: New data on the product job market
insight: User research is the second-fastest-growing role, suggesting companies overcorrected with layoffs and now realize its value.
pull_quote: User research is suddenly the second-fastest-growing role in tech. After a "reckoning" and significant layoffs last year, companies are now rehiring, realizing they went too far.

episode_title: New data on the product job market
insight: Product Owner roles are experiencing consistent, steady growth, likely driven by expanding product investments in non-tech industries.
pull_quote: Surprisingly, Product Owners are the third fastest-growing role in tech, with consistent, steady expansion. This might be from non-tech companies expanding their product investments.

episode_title: New data on the product job market
insight: Scrum Master roles are the only ones shrinking, potentially indicating a title shift towards Product Owner roles or a diminished need.
pull_quote: Scrum Master is the *only* role currently shrinking, with more people being let go than hired each month. This might partly explain the growth in Product Owner roles.

episode_title: On asking for help (even when you really don’t want to)
insight: Don't let the fear of appearing weak stop you from asking for help; it's a career unlock that builds trust and increases agency.
pull_quote: People often believe asking for help makes them look weak or incapable. In fact, every successful person became successful *because* they were skilled at asking for help. It's a career unlock.

episode_title: On asking for help (even when you really don’t want to)
insight: The exhaustion of assuming you must do everything alone robs PMs of agency, joy, and energy, leading directly to burnout.
pull_quote: "The exhaustion of subconsciously assuming I have to do everything on my own robbed me of agency, joy, and so much energy in my work." This tendency is especially salient among PMs lauded for "ownership."

episode_title: On asking for help (even when you really don’t want to)
insight: To maximize 'yes' responses, clearly structure your ask with a signpost, specific need, strong rationale, and compelling 'why them' for the recipient.
pull_quote: To get the help you need, make your ask compelling: create a relational connection, then make your request as small, easy, and clear as possible for a higher chance of a "yes."

episode_title: On asking for help (even when you really don’t want to)
insight: PMs should expand their concept of help beyond task delegation to include perspective, information, advocacy, and empathetic support.
pull_quote: Not all help is about reducing your task load. PMs often need perspective, data, thought partnership, or even advocacy to move projects forward and manage overwhelm.

episode_title: On asking for help (even when you really don’t want to)
insight: How you receive help is as crucial as asking; express authentic appreciation, share impact, and offer reciprocity to build stronger relationships.
pull_quote: How you receive help is just as important as how you ask. Express authentic appreciation and share the impact to reinforce positive behavior, even if the help wasn't perfect.

episode_title: On being funny at work
insight: Humor isn't just entertainment; it alters brain chemistry to enhance learning, build trust, create empathy, and boost memory in your audience.
pull_quote: Making someone laugh alters a person’s brain chemistry. Laughing swaps the cortisol in our bloodstream with dopamine, oxytocin, and endorphins—three powerful chemicals every leader should want in their audience’s brains.

episode_title: On being funny at work
insight: Use humor strategies like exaggeration or surprising definitions to make complex product ideas or pain points instantly memorable and impactful.
pull_quote: Steve Jobs used visual exaggeration in his 2007 iPhone launch, showing a janky iPod with a kitchen timer for a big laugh. He also defined the iPod as '1,000 songs in your pocket.'

episode_title: On being funny at work
insight: The core of humor is surprise; strategically deploying unexpected details or twists ensures your audience stays attentive and remembers your message.
pull_quote: Humor is the strategic assembly of specific words, spoken in a specific way, to create a surprise that produces a smile or a laugh. It’s a highly effective means of holding an audience’s attention.

episode_title: On being funny at work
insight: Employ specific details or nostalgia to introduce humor; even if not outright funny, they remain informative and engage your audience effectively.
pull_quote: The beauty of specificity is that when it fails to produce a laugh, it’s rarely perceived as a failed joke. It’s simply a specific detail, provided to produce a clearer image in the audience’s mind.

episode_title: On saying no
insight: Sustained success comes from deep focus on one thing; saying yes to too many "good" opportunities dilutes effort and leads to decline.
pull_quote: "The difference between successful people and really successful people is that really successful people say no to almost everything." Don't let new, exciting opportunities dilute your focus from the core work that made you successful in the first place.

episode_title: On saying no
insight: Filter all potential commitments through your top 2-3 core priorities, making objective decisions based on logic, not fear of disappointing others.
pull_quote: When deciding on new commitments, first imagine there's no emotion involved. Filter every ask through your top 2-3 core priorities to make an objective choice, then figure out how to deliver the news kindly.

episode_title: On saying no
insight: Establish clear personal policies for commitments and block dedicated "deep work" time to proactively force prioritization and reduce incoming distractions.
pull_quote: Create clear policies for yourself (e.g., "no talks or events," "no meetings before 3 PM") and communicate them. People respect declared boundaries and you protect critical focus time.

episode_title: On saying no
insight: Combat the tendency to say yes to future commitments you'll regret by using an external sounding board or reflecting on past mistakes.
pull_quote: It's incredibly easy to say yes to something months in the future, only to regret it when the deadline looms. Ask yourself, "Would I be excited if this were tomorrow?" If not, say no.

episode_title: On taking time off
insight: Sabbaticals aren't just a break; they create an "identity workspace" for deep reflection and experimentation, profoundly shifting a PM's career path.
pull_quote: I’ve interviewed more than 250 people from all walks of life, and I’ve never spoken to anyone who regretted taking time off. Instead, people call their time off transformational, life-changing, and one of the best decisions of their lives.

episode_title: On taking time off
insight: True detachment and transformation on sabbatical demands at least 2-3 months; it takes weeks just to shed ingrained work anxiety and identity.
pull_quote: Most people in our study described needing at least six weeks(!) just to lose the anxiety around tasks piling up, phantom phone alerts, and old responsibilities that were no longer theirs. It felt weird to not check emails.

episode_title: On taking time off
insight: To maximize sabbatical benefits, completely disconnect from your primary job; even "just a few hours consulting" will undermine true personal growth.
pull_quote: Yes, it’s okay to work, just don’t *Work*. Doing anything that resembles your current job will make it more difficult to reap the benefits of time off. Even if it’s "just a few hours consulting."

episode_title: On taking time off
insight: A sabbatical can be a powerful differentiator in job interviews and a crucial filter for company culture, not a career impediment.
pull_quote: Most sabbatical-takers actually use the story of their time-off as a differentiator in the interview process. If an employer looked down on you for taking time off, would you *really* want to work for them?

episode_title: One team, one roadmap - Issue 30
insight: Using multiple roadmaps for a single cross-functional team drastically reduces prioritization, alignment, transparency, accountability, and dependency clarity.
pull_quote: It's normal for high-growth companies to have more tools than people, but disparate roadmap docs are broken. Using multiple roadmaps for a single team reduces key benefits like alignment and accountability.

episode_title: One team, one roadmap - Issue 30
insight: A PM's central role is to conduct the cross-functional team with a single, shared roadmap that defines priorities, alignment, transparency, accountability, and dependencies.
pull_quote: PMs are like orchestra conductors, and the roadmap is their score. If violins have one plan and tubas another, it ends poorly. A single source-of-truth roadmap is vital for cross-functional teams.

episode_title: One team, one roadmap - Issue 30
insight: While functional teams can maintain internal roadmaps for sub-tasks, a single, shared source-of-truth roadmap is non-negotiable for cross-functional efforts.
